*Step 4: Clinical interpretation.* *(2 marks)*
Approximately **42.8%** of the incident ultrasound intensity is reflected back from the soft-tissue–bone interface, producing a very bright echo on the B-mode image. The remaining 57.2% enters the bone but is rapidly absorbed, which is why bone casts a strong acoustic shadow behind it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **acoustic impedance matching** with reference to ultrasound transducer design. Why is a coupling gel used between the transducer and the skin during a scan? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasound system receives an echo from a tissue interface **7.5 cm** deep. Assuming a propagation speed of 1540 m/s in soft tissue, calculate: (i) the round-trip time of flight of the echo, and (ii) the axial resolution of a system using a 2-cycle, 5 MHz pulse. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Acoustic Impedance Matching (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Definition of impedance matching.* Impedance matching refers to the technique of introducing an intermediate medium (or layer) whose characteristic impedance lies between those of two adjoining media, in order to **minimize** the reflection coefficient at their interface. *(2 marks)*

*Step 2: Transducer matching layer.* A piezoelectric crystal has $Z \approx 30 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl; soft tissue has $Z \approx 1.63 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl. Without a matching layer, $R$ would be near 1, and no energy would enter the body. A matching layer with $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{crystal}} \cdot Z_{\text{tissue}}}$ drastically reduces $R$. *(2 marks)*

*Step 3: Function of coupling gel.* The gel has $Z \approx 1.5 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl, very close to that of soft tissue. It eliminates the **air pocket** between the transducer face and the skin. Since $Z_{\text{air}} \approx 430$ Rayl, an air gap alone would reflect > 99.99% of the ultrasound. The gel ensures nearly all the acoustic energy enters the body. *(2 marks)*

*Step 4: Conclusion.* Impedance matching is essential for **maximum energy transfer** and **minimum reflection loss** at every interface from the transducer to the tissue of interest. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — Time of Flight and Axial Resolution (7 marks):**

*Step 1: Time of flight calculation.* *(3 marks)*
Depth $d = 7.5$ cm $= 0.075$ m. The wave travels **down and back**, so the total path length is $2d$.

$$t = \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{2 \times 0.075}{1540} = \frac{0.150}{1540}$$

$$t \approx 9.74 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} \approx 97.4 \, \mu s$$

*Step 2: Wavelength of the 5 MHz pulse.* *(1 mark)*

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.308 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 3: Spatial pulse length (SPL).* For a 2-cycle pulse: *(1 mark)*

$$\text{SPL} = n \cdot \lambda = 2 \times 0.308 = 0.616 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 4: Axial resolution.* The axial resolution is half the spatial pulse length (the minimum separation at which two reflectors produce distinct echoes): *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Axial Resolution} = \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{0.616}{2} \approx 0.308 \text{ mm}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square** — Writing $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ instead of the squared form. The squared form is the **intensity** coefficient (most KTU questions ask for intensity).
> 2. **Confusing amplitude and intensity** — Amplitude coefficient is linear; intensity is squared. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Using wrong speed of sound** — Always use **c = 1540 m/s** in soft tissue unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in round-trip time calculations. Depth is one-way; time of flight is round-trip.
> 5. **Mixing up specular and scattering** — Specular is mirror-like; diffuse is rough-surface or particle. Specular produces strong boundary echoes; diffuse produces speckle.
> 6. **Units mismatch** — Acoustic impedance must be stated in Rayl or kg/(m²·s), not in ohms (which is the electrical analog, not the same thing).
> 7. **Sign of $R_{\text{amp}}$** — The amplitude coefficient can be negative (phase inversion at higher-to-lower impedance transition), but $R$ (intensity) is always non-negative.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reflection** is the cornerstone of pulse-echo ultrasound imaging.
- **Characteristic acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ governs the fraction of energy reflected at every interface.
- **Intensity reflection coefficient:** $R = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^{2}$ — always between 0 and 1.
- **Amplitude reflection coefficient:** $R_{\text{amp}} = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ — between $-1$ and $+1$.
- **Snell's law:** $\dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{c_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_t}{c_2}$ governs refraction at oblique incidence.
- **Law of reflection:** $\theta_r = \theta_i$ (valid only for specular reflection).
- **Depth calculation:** $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$, with $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- **Specular reflection** — strong, mirror-like, used to image organ boundaries.
- **Diffuse reflection (scattering)** — weak, multi-directional, produces speckle and characterizes parenchyma.
- **Acoustic impedance matching** — essential in transducer and gel design to maximize energy transfer into tissue.
- **Clinical relevance:** Large $R$ at bone/tissue and air/tissue interfaces → strong echoes and acoustic shadowing. Small $R$ at blood/muscle interfaces → faint echoes, good penetration.
- **The single most important numerical** you must memorize: $c_{\text{soft tissue}} = 1540$ m/s.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scattering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scattering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In medical imaging, **Scattering** is defined as the physical phenomenon wherein an incident photon (X-ray or gamma ray) interacts with the atoms of a target medium and undergoes a change in its direction of propagation, with or without a concurrent change in its energy. It is one of the two principal categories of photon–matter interaction (the other being absorption), and it directly governs the **image quality**, **contrast**, and **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** of every projection-based imaging modality — from planar radiography to X-ray CT, and from SPECT to PET.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** Scattering is studied under the unit *"Interaction of Radiation with Matter"* and forms the basis for understanding **image degradation**, **grids**, and **collimation** in later modules.

## 1.2 Two Principal Scattering Modes

For diagnostic radiology (energies **20 keV – 150 keV**), the two relevant scattering modes are:

1. **Coherent (Rayleigh / Classical) Scattering** — photon is deflected with **no change in energy** (elastic).
2. **Compton (Incoherent) Scattering** — photon transfers part of its energy to a loosely-bound outer-shell electron (inelastic). This is the **dominant scattering process** in the diagnostic energy range.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bumper-Car Model

Imagine photons as tiny bumper-cars travelling through a tissue "fun-fair":

- **Coherent scattering** is like a glancing tap on a tightly-parked heavy truck — the bumper-car **bounces off** with the same speed, only its direction changes.
- **Compton scattering** is like colliding with a free-moving small car — the bumper-car **slows down** (loses energy) and transfers some kinetic energy to the other car (the recoil electron), while both cars scatter at different angles.
- **Photoelectric absorption** is like crashing head-on into a concrete wall — the car is **completely stopped** (photon annihilated, electron ejected).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** Scattered photons that reach the image receptor carry **no anatomical information** about the path they actually travelled. They only add a uniform haze, reducing **contrast** — analogous to trying to read a book through a foggy windscreen.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}}$ |
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}$ |
| Electron rest mass | $m_0$ | $\mathbf{9.109 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}}$ |
| Rest mass energy of electron | $m_0 c^2$ | $\mathbf{511\ \text{keV}}$ |
| Compton wavelength of electron | $\lambda_c$ | $\mathbf{2.426 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}}$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coherent (Rayleigh) Scattering

- Occurs predominantly at **low photon energies (< 30 keV)**.
- Photon interacts with **bound orbital electrons** of the atom as a whole.
- The atom is neither ionized nor excited; the photon emerges with the **same wavelength** but a different direction.
- Contribution to image degradation is small at diagnostic energies but becomes important in **mammography** (low kVp).
- Scattering angle is generally **small (< 5°)**; the angular distribution is strongly forward-peaked.

## 2.2 Compton (Incoherent) Scattering — The Dominant Process

Compton scattering is the **most clinically significant** interaction in the **diagnostic X-ray range (≈ 30 keV – 150 keV)** and in **nuclear medicine** (e.g., 140 keV Tc-99m gamma photons). It involves a photon and a *quasi-free* outer-shell electron.

### 2.2.1 Physical Setup

- **Initial state:** Photon of energy $E = h\nu$ strikes a stationary, free electron.
- **Final state:** Photon is scattered at angle $\theta$ with reduced energy $E' = h\nu'$; the recoil electron moves off at angle $\phi$ with kinetic energy $T_e = E - E'$.

### 2.2.2 The Compton Wavelength Shift (Headline Equation)

$$\Delta \lambda = \lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_0 c}\,(1 - \cos\theta)$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = incident wavelength, $\lambda'$ = scattered wavelength
- $\theta$ = scattering angle of the photon
- $\lambda_c = h / m_0 c = 2.426 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$ = **Compton wavelength of the electron**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wavelength shift $\Delta\lambda$ depends **only on $\theta$** — never on the incident wavelength itself. This was the brilliant experimental proof of the photon's quantum nature (A. H. Compton, Nobel Prize 1927).

### 2.2.3 Energy Form of the Compton Equation

Expressed in photon energies, the same relation becomes:

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 + \dfrac{E}{m_0 c^{2}}\,(1 - \cos\theta)}$$

The kinetic energy of the recoil electron is therefore:

$$T_e = E - E' = \frac{E \cdot \dfrac{E}{m_0 c^{2}}\,(1 - \cos\theta)}{1 + \dfrac{E}{m_0 c^{2}}\,(1 - \cos\theta)}$$

### 2.2.4 Three Limiting Cases (Extremely High-Yield for KTU)

| Case | Angle $\theta$ | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Forward grazing | $\theta \to 0°$ | $E' \to E$, $T_e \to 0$ (no interaction) |
| $90°$ scatter | $\theta = 90°$ | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_c$; $E'_{\max\ shift} = E/(1+E/511\text{ keV})$ |
| Direct backscatter | $\theta = 180°$ | $\Delta\lambda = 2\lambda_c$ (maximum shift); $E'_{\min} = E/(1 + 2E/511\text{ keV})$ |

### 2.2.5 Klein–Nishina Differential Cross-Section

The probability of scattering into a unit solid angle is given by:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} = \frac{r_e^{2}}{2}\left(\frac{E'}{E}\right)^{2}\!\left(\frac{E'}{E} + \frac{E}{E'} - \sin^{2}\theta\right)$$

where $r_e = e^{2}/(4\pi\varepsilon_{0}m_{0}c^{2}) = 2.818 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{m}$ is the **classical electron radius**. Integrating over the full sphere gives the **total Compton cross-section per electron** $\sigma_C$, which **decreases with increasing photon energy**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_c (1-\cos\theta)$ | Compton wavelength shift |
| 2 | $\lambda_c = h/m_0c = 2.426 \times 10^{-12}$ m | Compton wavelength |
| 3 | $E' = E/\bigl[1 + (E/511\text{ keV})(1-\cos\theta)\bigr]$ | Energy of scattered photon |
| 4 | $T_e = E - E'$ | Kinetic energy of recoil electron |
| 5 | $\mu_{tot} = \mu_{photo} + \mu_{Compton} + \mu_{pair}$ | Total linear attenuation coefficient |
| 6 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer–Lambert (includes scatter + absorption) |
| 7 | $\sigma_C \propto 1/E$ (approx.) | Energy dependence of Compton cross-section |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} = \dfrac{r_e^{2}}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{E'}{E}\right)^{\!2}\!\left(\dfrac{E'}{E} + \dfrac{E}{E'} - \sin^{2}\theta\right)$ | Klein–Nishina differential cross-section |
| 9 | $\text{Scatter fraction} = \dfrac{N_{scatter}}{N_{primary} + N_{scatter}}$ | Metric used in CT / nuclear medicine |
| 10 | $\text{Contrast reduction} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \text{SPR}}$ | SPR = Scatter-to-Primary Ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Quantitative understanding of scatter is the foundation for designing **anti-scatter grids** (radiography), **bow-tie filters** (CT), **energy-window discriminators** (SPECT), and **scatter-correction algorithms** (PET). Every modern scanner incorporates at least three scatter-mitigation strategies.

## 2.4 Effect of Scattering on the Medical Image

- **Loss of contrast** — the primary clinical concern.
- **Increased image noise** (quantum mottle from random scatter directions).
- **Reduced image SNR** — sometimes by a factor of 2 or more in thick body parts.
- **Cupping artefact in CT** — scatter raises the apparent attenuation in the centre of a body cross-section.
- **Degraded quantification** in SPECT and PET (overestimation of activity).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Compton Equation

We derive $\Delta\lambda = (h/m_0c)(1-\cos\theta)$ from the **conservation of total relativistic energy** and **conservation of linear momentum** (vector form).

### Step 0 — Define the Variables and the Geometry

- An incident photon of frequency $\nu$ moves along the +x axis, energy $E = h\nu$, momentum $p_\gamma = E/c = h\nu/c$.
- Target electron is at rest: rest energy $m_0c^2$, zero momentum.
- After the collision, the scattered photon travels at angle $\theta$ to the +x axis with energy $E' = h\nu'$ and momentum $p'_\gamma = E'/c$.
- The recoil electron leaves at angle $\phi$ to the +x axis with total relativistic energy $E_e$ and momentum $p_e$.

### Step 1 — Conservation of Total Relativistic Energy

The total energy is conserved:

$$h\nu + m_0 c^{2} = h\nu' + E_e$$

Solving for the electron's total energy:

$$E_e = h\nu - h\nu' + m_0 c^{2} = m_0 c^{2} + h(\nu - \nu')$$

### Step 2 — Relativistic Energy–Momentum Relation for the Electron

By Einstein's relativistic relation:

$$E_e^{2} = (p_e c)^{2} + (m_0 c^{2})^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Square the Energy Conservation Equation

$$\bigl(m_0 c^{2} + h(\nu - \nu')\bigr)^{2} = E_e^{2}$$

Substitute the relation from Step 2:

$$\bigl(m_0 c^{2} + h(\nu - \nu')\bigr)^{2} = (p_e c)^{2} + (m_0 c^{2})^{2}$$

Expanding the left side:

$$(m_0 c^{2})^{2} + 2 m_0 c^{2} h(\nu - \nu') + h^{2}(\nu - \nu')^{2} = (p_e c)^{2} + (m_0 c^{2})^{2}$$

Cancel $(m_0 c^{2})^{2}$ on both sides:

$$2 m_0 c^{2} h(\nu - \nu') + h^{2}(\nu - \nu')^{2} = (p_e c)^{2} \quad \text{...(I)}$$

### Step 4 — Conservation of Momentum (x and y Components)

x-component (horizontal):

$$\frac{h\nu}{c} = \frac{h\nu'}{c}\cos\theta + p_e \cos\phi \quad \text{...(II)}$$

y-component (vertical):

$$0 = \frac{h\nu'}{c}\sin\theta - p_e \sin\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_e \sin\phi = \frac{h\nu'}{c}\sin\theta \quad \text{...(III)}$$

### Step 5 — Eliminate $\phi$ by Squaring and Adding (II) and (III)

Rearrange (II) to isolate $p_e \cos\phi$:

$$p_e \cos\phi = \frac{h\nu}{c} - \frac{h\nu'}{c}\cos\theta$$

Now form $(p_e \cos\phi)^{2} + (p_e \sin\phi)^{2} = p_e^{2}$:

$$p_e^{2} = \left(\frac{h\nu}{c} - \frac{h\nu'}{c}\cos\theta\right)^{2} + \left(\frac{h\nu'}{c}\sin\theta\right)^{2}$$

Multiply both sides by $c^{2}$:

$$(p_e c)^{2} = h^{2}\nu^{2} - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu'\cos\theta + h^{2}\nu'^{2}\cos^{2}\theta + h^{2}\nu'^{2}\sin^{2}\theta$$

Using $\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta = 1$:

$$(p_e c)^{2} = h^{2}\nu^{2} + h^{2}\nu'^{2} - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu'\cos\theta \quad \text{...(IV)}$$

### Step 6 — Equate (I) and (IV)

$$2 m_0 c^{2} h(\nu - \nu') + h^{2}(\nu - \nu')^{2} = h^{2}\nu^{2} + h^{2}\nu'^{2} - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu'\cos\theta$$

Expand $h^{2}(\nu - \nu')^{2} = h^{2}\nu^{2} - 2h^{2}\nu\nu' + h^{2}\nu'^{2}$ and substitute:

$$2 m_0 c^{2} h(\nu - \nu') + h^{2}\nu^{2} - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu' + h^{2}\nu'^{2} = h^{2}\nu^{2} + h^{2}\nu'^{2} - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu'\cos\theta$$

Cancel $h^{2}\nu^{2}$ and $h^{2}\nu'^{2}$ from both sides:

$$2 m_0 c^{2} h(\nu - \nu') - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu' = - 2 h^{2}\nu\nu'\cos\theta$$

Divide by $2h$:

$$m_0 c^{2}(\nu - \nu') - h\nu\nu' = -h\nu\nu'\cos\theta$$

Rearrange:

$$m_0 c^{2}(\nu - \nu') = h\nu\nu'(1 - \cos\theta)$$

### Step 7 — Convert to Wavelengths Using $c = \nu\lambda = \nu'\lambda'$

From $c = \nu\lambda$ we have $\nu = c/\lambda$ and $\nu' = c/\lambda'$, so $\nu - \nu' = c(1/\lambda - 1/\lambda')$, and $\nu\nu' = c^{2}/(\lambda\lambda')$.

Substitute:

$$m_0 c^{2} \cdot c\!\left(\frac{1}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\lambda'}\right) = \frac{h\,c^{2}}{\lambda\lambda'}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Simplify (a factor of $c$ cancels from both sides):

$$m_0 c\!\left(\frac{\lambda' - \lambda}{\lambda\lambda'}\right) = \frac{h}{\lambda\lambda'}(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Multiply both sides by $\lambda\lambda'$:

$$m_0 c (\lambda' - \lambda) = h(1 - \cos\theta)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_0 c}(1 - \cos\theta)\;}$$

This is the **Compton scattering equation** in its most famous form. ∎

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 100 keV photon undergoes Compton scattering at an angle of $60°$. Calculate:
- (a) the wavelength of the incident photon,
- (b) the wavelength of the scattered photon,
- (c) the energy of the scattered photon, and
- (d) the kinetic energy of the recoil electron.

### (a) Incident Wavelength

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{100 \times 10^{3} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.6 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.242 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m} = 0.1242\ \text{Å}$$

### (b) Scattered Wavelength

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{h}{m_0 c}(1 - \cos 60°) = (2.426 \times 10^{-12})(1 - 0.5) = 1.213 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

$$\lambda' = \lambda + \Delta\lambda = 1.242 \times 10^{-11} + 0.1213 \times 10^{-11} = 1.363 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}$$

### (c) Scattered Photon Energy

$$E' = \frac{hc}{\lambda'} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.363 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.458 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{J}$$

Convert to keV:

$$E' = \frac{1.458 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^{-3} = 91.1\ \text{keV}$$

### (d) Recoil Electron Kinetic Energy

$$T_e = E - E' = 100 - 91.1 = 8.9\ \text{keV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cross-check using the energy form: $E' = E/[1 + (E/511)(1-\cos\theta)] = 100/[1 + (100/511)(0.5)] = 100/1.0978 = 91.1$ keV ✓

## 3.3 Energy-Form Derivation of the Recoil Electron

Starting from the energy-conservation result:

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 + \dfrac{E}{m_0 c^{2}}\,(1 - \cos\theta)}$$

The recoil electron's kinetic energy follows directly:

$$T_e = E - E' = E - \frac{E}{1 + \alpha(1-\cos\theta)}$$

where $\alpha = E/m_0c^{2}$. Combining over a common denominator:

$$T_e = \frac{E\bigl[1 + \alpha(1-\cos\theta)\bigr] - E}{1 + \alpha(1-\cos\theta)} = \frac{E\,\alpha(1-\cos\theta)}{1 + \alpha(1-\cos\theta)}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_e = \frac{E\,\alpha(1-\cos\theta)}{1 + \alpha(1-\cos\theta)}\;}$$

This is the form most often asked in KTU 14-mark problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compton Scattering Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Photon E, p, lambda] --> B[Stationary Electron at rest mass m0c2]
    B --> C[Interaction Vertex]
    C --> D[Scattered Photon Eprime, pprime, lambdaprime at angle theta]
    C --> E[Recoil Electron Te, pe at angle phi]
    F[Conservation of Energy] -.-> C
    G[Conservation of Momentum x] -.-> C
    H[Conservation of Momentum y] -.-> C
    I[Output Compton Equation] --> J["Wavelength shift Deltalambda = h over m0c times 1 minus cos theta"]
```

## 4.2 Photon–Matter Interaction Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Incident Photon] --> Q1{Energy Range}
    Q1 -->|Low less than 30 keV| Q2{Bound Electron?}
    Q1 -->|Diagnostic 30 to 150 keV| Q3[Compton Dominant]
    Q1 -->|High above 1.022 MeV| Q4[Pair Production]
    Q2 -->|Yes, atom as a whole| R1[Coherent Rayleigh Scatter]
    Q2 -->|No, inner shell| R2[Photoelectric Absorption]
    Q3 --> R3[Compton Incoherent Scatter]
    R1 --> Out1[No energy change, direction change]
    R2 --> Out2[Photon absorbed, electron ejected]
    R3 --> Out3[Energy and direction of photon both change]
    Q4 --> Out4[Electron positron pair created]
```

## 4.3 Image-Quality Impact Chain of Scattering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Scatter Photons reach detector] --> A[Add uniform haze to image]
    A --> B[Reduce subject contrast]
    A --> C[Increase image noise floor]
    A --> D[Lower SNR]
    B --> E[Misdiagnosis risk]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    M[Mitigation Strategies] --> S
    M1[Anti Scatter Grid] --> M
    M2[Bow Tie Filter] --> M
    M3[Collimation] --> M
    M4[Air Gap Technique] --> M
    M5[Software Scatter Correction] --> M
```

## 4.4 Energy Dependence of Major Interactions (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[10 keV] -->|Photoelectric dominates| B[30 keV]
    B -->|Compton rises| C[100 keV]
    C -->|Compton dominant diagnostic| D[150 keV]
    D -->|Pair production threshold| E[1022 keV]
    E -->|Pair production rises| F[5000 keV]
    G[Coherent scatter small contribution throughout] --> B
    G --> C
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
*Define Compton scattering. Why is it considered the dominant interaction in the diagnostic X-ray energy range?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Compton scattering is the inelastic collision between an incident photon and a *quasi-free* outer-shell electron, in which the photon is deflected from its original path with a **reduced energy** and an **outer-shell electron is ejected** from the atom (recoil electron).
- **Why dominant (1.5 marks):** In the **30 keV – 150 keV** range — the diagnostic X-ray band — the photon energy is far higher than the binding energy of outer-shell electrons, so the electron behaves as essentially free. The Compton mass attenuation coefficient is largest in this range, while photoelectric cross-section drops rapidly with energy ($\propto 1/E^{3}$) and pair production is energetically forbidden below 1022 keV. Therefore Compton scattering dominates tissue attenuation at typical clinical kVp settings (e.g., 80–120 kVp).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, Module 1]**
*State the Compton scattering equation and define the Compton wavelength. Why is $\Delta\lambda$ independent of the incident photon energy?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (1 mark):** $\lambda' - \lambda = (h/m_0c)(1 - \cos\theta)$.
- **Definition of Compton wavelength (1 mark):** $\lambda_c = h/m_0c = 2.426 \times 10^{-12}$ m, the wavelength shift corresponding to a 90° photon scatter.
- **Reason for independence (1 mark):** $\Delta\lambda$ depends only on the geometric factor $(1 - \cos\theta)$ and the fundamental constants $h$, $m_0$, and $c$. The derivation (see Section 3) shows that the incident wavelength cancels from the equation — the wavelength shift is a *kinematic* property of the electron–photon collision, not of the photon's initial state.

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Provide the answer for **Question A OR Question B** in the KTU ESE. Both options are given below for self-evaluation.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1]*

**(a) Derive the Compton scattering equation starting from conservation of energy and momentum. Show that the wavelength shift of the scattered photon depends only on the scattering angle. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The examiner expects:

1. Clear statement of the **three conservation laws** (energy + 2 components of momentum). **(1 mark)**
2. Writing the **relativistic energy–momentum** relation for the electron. **(1 mark)**
3. Setting up the **x- and y-momentum** equations. **(1 mark)**
4. Eliminating the recoil electron angle $\phi$ by squaring and adding. **(1 mark)**
5. Equating with the squared-energy relation and simplifying. **(1 mark)**
6. Final conversion to wavelength form and concluding $\Delta\lambda = (h/m_0c)(1-\cos\theta)$ is **independent of $\lambda$** (depends only on $\theta$). **(2 marks)**

**(b) A 150 keV photon undergoes Compton scattering through an angle of 90°. Calculate (i) the wavelength of the incident photon, (ii) the wavelength of the scattered photon, (iii) the energy of the scattered photon, and (iv) the kinetic energy of the recoil electron. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Incident wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E} = \frac{1240\ \text{eV·nm}}{150{,}000\ \text{eV}} = 8.27 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{nm} = 8.27 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 8.27 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}}$$

**(ii) Scattered wavelength:** For $\theta = 90°$, $\cos\theta = 0$:

$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_c = 2.426 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

$$\lambda' = \lambda + \Delta\lambda = 8.27 \times 10^{-12} + 2.426 \times 10^{-12} = 10.70 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda' = 1.07 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}}$$

**(iii) Scattered photon energy:**

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 + (E/m_0c^{2})(1-\cos\theta)} = \frac{150}{1 + (150/511)(1)} = \frac{150}{1.2936}$$

$$\boxed{E' = 115.95\ \text{keV}}$$

**(iv) Recoil electron kinetic energy:**

$$T_e = E - E' = 150 - 115.95 = 34.05\ \text{keV}$$

$$\boxed{T_e \approx 34.05\ \text{keV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students frequently forget that $hc = 1240\ \text{eV·nm}$ and unnecessarily convert everything to SI joules. A direct use of $hc = 1240\ \text{eV·nm}$ with energies in eV/keV saves **3–4 minutes per problem** and avoids arithmetic mistakes.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Do **not** confuse the *photon's total energy* $E'$ with the *recoil electron's kinetic energy* $T_e$. Several students write $T_e = E'$, losing **1 mark**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Alternative Choice]*

**(a) Differentiate between coherent (Rayleigh) and Compton scattering. In which energy range does each dominate, and what is the net effect on diagnostic image quality? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Feature | Coherent (Rayleigh) | Compton (Incoherent) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of electron | Tightly bound, atom reacts as a whole | Quasi-free outer-shell electron |
| Energy of scattered photon | **Unchanged** (elastic) | **Reduced** (inelastic) |
| Direction change | Yes (small angles, < 5°) | Yes (broad distribution) |
| Wavelength shift | **Zero** | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_c(1-\cos\theta)$ |
| Dominant energy range | < 30 keV (mammography) | 30 keV – 150 keV (diagnostic X-ray, Tc-99m) |
| Contribution to attenuation | Minor in general radiography | Major in diagnostic X-ray range |
| Effect on image | Slight blur; minimal contrast loss | Severe contrast loss; adds uniform haze; raises noise floor |

**Energy-range justification (2 marks):** Coherent scattering cross-section falls off rapidly with photon energy ($\propto 1/E^{2}$ in some regimes), becoming negligible above 50–60 keV. Compton scattering, governed by the Klein–Nishina formula, decreases more slowly ($\sigma_C \propto 1/E$) and is the largest single contributor to the mass attenuation coefficient in soft tissue over the entire 30–150 keV band.

**Image-quality effect (1 mark):** Both modes degrade image quality — coherent scatter produces mild blur (low-angle forward deflection), while Compton scatter, because of its broad angle distribution and energy loss, is the **primary cause of contrast degradation** and **cupping artefacts in CT**.

**(b) Explain with a labelled sketch the geometry of Compton scattering. A 200 keV photon is back-scattered ($\theta = 180°$). Find the energy of the scattered photon and the kinetic energy of the recoil electron. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Geometry (3 marks)** — The student must draw (or describe) the following:

- Incident photon approaches from the left along the +x axis with energy $E$ and momentum $E/c$.
- Stationary electron at the origin.
- Scattered photon leaves at angle $\theta$ to the +x axis with energy $E' < E$ and momentum $E'/c$.
- Recoil electron leaves at angle $\phi$ to the +x axis (where $\phi < 90°$ for a back-scattered photon) with kinetic energy $T_e$ and momentum $p_e$.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

For $\theta = 180°$, $\cos\theta = -1$, $1 - \cos\theta = 2$:

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 + (E/m_0c^{2})(1-\cos\theta)} = \frac{200}{1 + (200/511)(2)} = \frac{200}{1 + 0.7828} = \frac{200}{1.7828}$$

$$\boxed{E' = 112.2\ \text{keV}}$$

Recoil electron kinetic energy:

$$T_e = E - E' = 200 - 112.2 = 87.8\ \text{keV}$$

$$\boxed{T_e = 87.8\ \text{keV}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** In back-scattering, the recoil electron carries away a *large fraction* of the photon's energy ($\sim 44\%$ in this problem). Many students wrongly assume the photon loses only a small amount. The factor $(1 - \cos\theta) = 2$ at $\theta = 180°$ makes Compton back-scatter the **most energy-depositing** single interaction.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** If a sketch is required, forgetting to label *all four* vectors (incident photon, scattered photon, electron momentum, and the two angles $\theta$ and $\phi$) costs at least **1 mark**. Always show angles clearly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Scattering** is the redirection of a photon during its interaction with matter, **with or without** energy loss.
- ☐ **Coherent (Rayleigh) scattering** = no energy change; dominant **below 30 keV**; minor in clinical X-ray.
- ☐ **Compton scattering** = energy loss + direction change; **dominant 30–150 keV**; the single most important interaction in diagnostic radiology and nuclear medicine (Tc-99m).
- ☐ The **Compton equation** is $\boxed{\Delta\lambda = (h/m_0c)(1 - \cos\theta) = 2.426 \times 10^{-12}(1-\cos\theta)\ \text{m}}$.
- ☐ $\Delta\lambda$ depends **only on the scattering angle $\theta$**, not on the incident wavelength.
- ☐ Maximum wavelength shift occurs at **$\theta = 180°$** (back-scatter): $\Delta\lambda_{\max} = 2\lambda_c = 4.85 \times 10^{-12}$ m.
- ☐ Energy form: $E' = E/\bigl[1 + (E/511\ \text{keV})(1-\cos\theta)\bigr]$.
- ☐ Recoil electron energy: $T_e = E - E'$.
- ☐ Scattering **degrades image quality** by reducing contrast, adding noise, and lowering SNR.
- ☐ Clinical mitigations: **anti-scatter grids, collimation, air-gap technique, bow-tie filters, software correction**.
- ☐ Klein–Nishina formula gives the **angle-dependent** probability of Compton scatter.
- ☐ Total linear attenuation coefficient: $\mu_{tot} = \mu_{photo} + \mu_{Compton} + \mu_{pair}$.
- ☐ Memorize: $hc = 1240\ \text{eV·nm} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{J·m}$ and $m_0c^{2} = 511$ keV — these two constants solve **every** KTU numerical on this topic.
- ☐ For derivation questions, always state the **three conservation laws** first before writing equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Refraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Refraction** is the phenomenon of change in the path (bending) of a propagating wave as it passes obliquely from one transparent medium into another medium having a different optical density (different phase velocity). In the context of **Medical Imaging Techniques**, refraction governs how light, ultrasound, and electromagnetic waves travel through biological tissues, optical fibres of endoscopes, and lens systems of diagnostic cameras.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Refraction is the change in direction of a wave due to a change in its speed when it travels from one medium to another, mathematically governed by **Snell's Law**.

The quantitative measure of this bending is the **Refractive Index** (also called Index of Refraction), denoted by the symbol **n**.

$$n = \frac{c}{v}$$

Where:
- **c** = speed of light in vacuum = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **v** = speed of light in the given medium
- **n** is a **dimensionless quantity** (no units)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Convention:** The refractive index of vacuum is **n = 1**, and that of air is approximately **n ≈ 1.0003** (often taken as 1 for clinical calculations). All biological tissues have **n > 1**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a shopping cart across a parking lot (smooth surface) into a grassy field (rough surface).

- If you push the cart **straight on (perpendicular)** to the boundary, the cart simply slows down but keeps moving straight.
- If you push the cart at an **angle (oblique)**, the wheels that hit the grass first will slow down, while the wheels still on the pavement continue at the original speed. This causes the cart to **pivot or "bend"** its direction of travel.

This is exactly how light behaves! Light waves travel slower in **optically denser media** (like water, glass, or biological tissue) and faster in **optically rarer media** (like air). The change in speed at the interface causes the wavefront to change direction.

## 1.3 Refractive Indices of Common Biological Tissues (Medical Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table - Refractive Indices in Medical Imaging**

| Medium | Refractive Index (n) |
|---|---|
| Vacuum | 1.0000 |
| Air | 1.0003 |
| Water (Cerebrospinal Fluid) | 1.33 |
| Cornea (Human Eye) | 1.376 |
| Aqueous/Vitreous Humor | 1.336 |
| Lens (Crystalline) | 1.386 – 1.406 |
| Typical Soft Tissue | 1.38 – 1.41 |
| Bone | 1.55 – 1.65 |
| Glass (Optical Fibre Core) | 1.50 – 1.62 |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Board examinations, remember that **biological tissues have refractive indices close to water (≈1.33 to 1.55)**. This is why ultrasound coupling gels (n ≈ 1.3 – 1.5) are used — to eliminate air gaps and allow efficient wave transmission into the body.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Refraction

When a light ray strikes the interface between two transparent media, the following sequence of events occurs:

1. **Incident Ray Strikes Interface:** A light ray traveling in medium 1 strikes the boundary at an angle $\theta_1$ (measured from the **normal**, which is a line perpendicular to the surface).

2. **Partial Reflection & Partial Refraction:** At the interface, the wave splits into two components:
   - A **reflected ray** that bounces back into medium 1 (governed by the Law of Reflection: $\theta_r = \theta_1$).
   - A **refracted ray** that enters medium 2 at a new angle $\theta_2$.

3. **Speed Change:** The refracted wave's speed changes from $v_1$ to $v_2$. Since the **frequency (f) of the wave remains constant** (boundary condition), the wavelength changes: $\lambda = v/f$.

4. **Direction Change (Bending):** To keep the wavefront continuous across the boundary, the direction must change. This is described by **Snell's Law**.

5. **Huygens' Principle Explanation:** Each point on the wavefront at the interface acts as a secondary source of spherical wavelets. Because these wavelets travel at different speeds on either side, the new wavefront tilts, producing the bent refracted ray.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — every KTU Board exam on this module tests at least 2 of these.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Condition / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refractive Index | $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$ | c, v in m/s; n is dimensionless | Definition, basic calculation |
| 2 | Relative Refractive Index | $n_{21} = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{v_1}{v_2}$ | All dimensionless | Medium 2 w.r.t. medium 1 |
| 3 | **Snell's Law** | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | $\theta$ in degrees/radians | Core refraction equation |
| 4 | Critical Angle (TIR onset) | $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Requires $n_1 > n_2$ | Total Internal Reflection |
| 5 | Apparent Depth | $d_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{d_{\text{real}}}{n}$ | d in metres | Object viewed through a medium |
| 6 | Refraction at Curved Surface | $\dfrac{n_2}{v} - \dfrac{n_1}{u} = \dfrac{n_2 - n_1}{R}$ | u, v, R in metres; sign convention | Lens/mirror optics in endoscopes |
| 7 | Optical Fibre NA | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Dimensionless | Numerical Aperture (endoscopy) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Medical Imaging, refraction is the foundation of multiple diagnostic modalities:

- **Endoscopy:** Flexible endoscopes use bundles of optical fibres. Light entering the fibre at angles smaller than the critical angle undergoes **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** and propagates along the fibre with minimal loss, enabling internal visualization of the GI tract.

- **Ophthalmology (Slit Lamp & Retinoscopy):** Refraction through the cornea and lens forms the basis of vision correction. LASIK surgery reshapes the cornea to alter its refractive power.

- **Ultrasound Imaging:** When ultrasound crosses tissue boundaries (e.g., muscle-fat interface), refraction causes the beam to bend, potentially creating **artifacts** (misplaced structures) in the image. Understanding refraction allows sonographers to interpret these artifacts correctly.

- **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT):** Low-coherence interferometry relies on precise refraction calculations to image retinal layers with micrometre resolution.

- **Multimode Fibre Refraction:** Different modes in a fibre travel at slightly different angles, causing **modal dispersion** — a critical concept in designing fibre-optic imaging probes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Snell's Law (From Wave Theory / Huygens' Principle)

This is a guaranteed KTU Board question. Master the logic.

**Setup:** Consider a plane wavefront AB travelling from medium 1 (refractive index $n_1$) into medium 2 (refractive index $n_2$). The interface is horizontal.

**Step 1:** Let the time taken for the wavefront to fully cross the interface be **t**.

The distance travelled by point A in medium 2 during this time is:

$$AD = v_2 \cdot t$$

The distance travelled by point B in medium 1 during this time is:

$$BC = v_1 \cdot t$$

**Step 2:** From the geometry of the figure, $\angle BAC = \theta_1$ (angle of incidence) and $\angle ACD = \theta_2$ (angle of refraction). In right triangles:

$$\sin\theta_1 = \frac{BC}{AC} = \frac{v_1 \cdot t}{AC}$$

$$\sin\theta_2 = \frac{AD}{AC} = \frac{v_2 \cdot t}{AC}$$

**Step 3:** Divide the two equations:

$$\frac{\sin\theta_1}{\sin\theta_2} = \frac{v_1}{v_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $v = c/n$:

$$\frac{\sin\theta_1}{\sin\theta_2} = \frac{c/n_1}{c/n_2} = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 5:** Cross-multiply to obtain Snell's Law:

$$\boxed{n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** If $n_2 > n_1$ (denser medium), then $\sin\theta_2 < \sin\theta_1$, so $\theta_2 < \theta_1$. The ray bends **towards the normal**. This is consistent with the "shopping cart" analogy.

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Angle

The **critical angle** is the angle of incidence in the denser medium for which the angle of refraction equals exactly **90°** (the refracted ray grazes along the interface). Beyond this angle, **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** occurs.

**Step 1:** Start with Snell's Law:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the critical condition $\theta_2 = 90°$, hence $\sin\theta_2 = 1$. Let $\theta_1 = \theta_c$:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \cdot 1$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\sin\theta_c$:

$$\boxed{\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition:** TIR can ONLY occur when light travels from a **denser medium** to a **rarer medium**, i.e., $n_1 > n_2$. If $n_1 < n_2$, the equation has no real solution.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Light travels from glass ($n_1 = 1.5$) to air ($n_2 = 1.0$):

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.6667$$

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.6667) = 41.81°$$

Any incident ray in the glass with $\theta > 41.81°$ will undergo Total Internal Reflection.

## 3.3 Derivation of Apparent Depth

When an object is placed inside a medium of refractive index **n** and viewed from outside (air), the apparent depth is less than the real depth.

**Step 1:** Consider an object O at real depth **d** below the surface. A ray from O travels up and refracts into air. Using small-angle (paraxial) approximation:

$$\sin\theta_1 \approx \tan\theta_1 = \frac{x}{d_{\text{real}}}$$

$$\sin\theta_2 \approx \tan\theta_2 = \frac{x}{d_{\text{app}}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Snell's Law ($n_{\text{medium}} \sin\theta_1 = n_{\text{air}} \sin\theta_2$, with $n_{\text{air}} \approx 1$):

$$n \cdot \frac{x}{d_{\text{real}}} = 1 \cdot \frac{x}{d_{\text{app}}}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel x and rearrange:

$$\boxed{d_{\text{app}} = \frac{d_{\text{real}}}{n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Application:** This formula explains why a wound or surgical site viewed through saline solution appears shallower than its actual depth. It is also why fish in a pond appear closer to the surface than they actually are.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Refraction Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incident Ray Strikes Interface"] --> B{"Medium 1 Optical Density vs Medium 2"}
    B -->|n1 less than n2| C["Ray Bends Toward Normal"]
    B -->|n1 greater than n2| D["Ray Bends Away From Normal"]
    B -->|Incident Angle equals Critical| E["Refracted Ray Grazes Along Interface"]
    B -->|Incident Angle greater than Critical| F["Total Internal Reflection Occurs"]

    C --> G["Applications: Eye Cornea, Ultrasound into Tissue"]
    D --> H["Applications: Light from Water to Air, Endoscope Exits"]
    E --> I["Boundary Condition: NA Calculation"]
    F --> J["Applications: Optical Fibre Endoscopy, Slit Lamp"]

    G --> K["Medical Imaging Output"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L["Diagnostic Image Formation"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style K fill:#fff4e1
    style L fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Refraction in an Optical Fibre Endoscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Light Source at Endoscope Tip"] --> S2["Enters Core n1 Equals 1.5"]
    S2 --> S3{"Angle of Incidence at Core-Cladding Boundary"}
    S3 -->|Less than Critical Angle| S4["Refraction into Cladding: Signal Loss"]
    S3 -->|Greater than Critical Angle| S5["Total Internal Reflection Inside Core"]
    S5 --> S6["Multiple TIR Bounces Down Fibre"]
    S6 --> S7["Light Exits at Distal End"]
    S7 --> S8["Illuminates Internal Tissue Surface"]
    S8 --> S9["Reflected Light Returns Through Same Fibre"]
    S9 --> S10["Forms Image on Eyepiece or CCD"]
    S4 --> S11["Reduced Image Brightness and Clarity"]
    style S5 fill:#c8e6c9
    style S9 fill:#fff9c4
    style S10 fill:#ffccbc
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic: Bending of Light at Interface

```
                    Normal (N)
                      |
                      |
                      |       Medium 2 (n₂)
                      |     /
        Medium 1      |    / 
        (n₁)          |   /  Refracted Ray
       \      \       |  /  (Bends TOWARDS normal
        \      \      | /    if n₂ > n₁)
         \      \     |/
          \______\____|/________ Interface
                   \  |
                    \ |
                     \|
                      *  Point of incidence
                      |
                      | Incident Ray
```

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Board exams, always remember the **two golden rules** for sketching:
> 1. The **normal** is drawn with **dashed lines** (not solid).
> 2. Both angles ($\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$) are measured from the **normal**, never from the surface.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**Define refractive index. State Snell's Law of refraction.**

**Model Answer:**

The **refractive index (n)** of a medium is defined as the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum (**c**) to the speed of light in that medium (**v**):

$$n = \frac{c}{v}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity that measures how much the medium slows down light relative to vacuum.

**Snell's Law:** It states that the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is constant and equals the ratio of the refractive indices of the two media:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

This constant is also called the **relative refractive index** $n_{21}$ of medium 2 with respect to medium 1.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]`
**What is Total Internal Reflection? Mention any two conditions necessary for it to occur.**

**Model Answer:**

**Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** is the phenomenon in which a light ray travelling from a denser medium to a rarer medium is completely reflected back into the denser medium at the interface, with no light being refracted into the rarer medium.

**Two Essential Conditions:**

1. **Denser to Rarer Medium:** Light must travel from a medium of **higher refractive index** to a medium of **lower refractive index** (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$).

2. **Angle of Incidence Must Exceed Critical Angle:** The angle of incidence in the denser medium must be **greater than the critical angle** ($\theta_i > \theta_c$), where $\sin\theta_c = n_2 / n_1$.

**Application:** TIR is the working principle behind **optical fibres** used in medical endoscopy to transmit light inside the human body without leakage.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for Module 1]`

**(a)** State and explain Snell's Law of refraction. Derive the expression for the critical angle for total internal reflection. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A ray of light travels from water (n = 1.33) into glass (n = 1.50). If the angle of incidence is 40°, calculate: (i) the angle of refraction, and (ii) the critical angle if light now travels from glass back into water. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) State and Explain Snell's Law:**

**Statement:** Snell's Law states that for a light ray refracting at the interface of two media:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

where $n_1, n_2$ are the refractive indices of the two media, and $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are the angles measured from the normal.

**Explanation:** When light crosses a boundary, its frequency remains constant, but its speed and wavelength change. The change in speed causes the wavefront to pivot, leading to a change in direction. The sine of the angle is proportional to the wave speed in each medium.

**Derivation of Critical Angle:**

Refer to Section 3.2. The critical condition is $\theta_2 = 90°$. Substituting into Snell's Law:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \sin(90°) = n_2$$

$$\therefore \sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

This has a valid solution **only if** $n_1 > n_2$. Beyond this angle, the refracted ray ceases to exist and the entire light energy is reflected back — this is TIR.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.33$ (water), $n_2 = 1.50$ (glass), $\theta_1 = 40°$

**(i) Angle of Refraction:**

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

$$\sin\theta_2 = \frac{n_1 \sin\theta_1}{n_2} = \frac{1.33 \times \sin 40°}{1.50}$$

$$\sin 40° = 0.6428$$

$$\sin\theta_2 = \frac{1.33 \times 0.6428}{1.50} = \frac{0.8549}{1.50} = 0.5699$$

$$\theta_2 = \sin^{-1}(0.5699) = 34.73°$$

> The ray bends **towards the normal** because it enters a denser medium — consistent with our analysis.

**(ii) Critical Angle (Glass to Water):**

Now light travels from glass ($n_1 = 1.50$) to water ($n_2 = 1.33$). The condition $n_1 > n_2$ is satisfied, so TIR is possible.

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.33}{1.50} = 0.8867$$

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.8867) = 62.46°$$

> Any ray inside the glass striking the glass-water interface at an angle greater than **62.46°** will undergo Total Internal Reflection.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam - Internal Choice]`

**(a)** What is the apparent depth formula? Derive it with a neat ray diagram. A coin is placed at the bottom of a trough containing oil of refractive index 1.45. If the real depth is 8 cm, find the apparent depth when viewed from above. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the principle and working of an optical fibre. Derive an expression for the Numerical Aperture (NA). Why is NA important in medical endoscopy? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Apparent Depth:**

**Statement:** When an object is viewed through a transparent medium, it appears to be at a lesser depth than its actual depth due to refraction. The relationship is:

$$d_{\text{app}} = \frac{d_{\text{real}}}{n}$$

**Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.3, for a small-angle refraction at a flat interface, the lateral displacement of the ray can be cancelled, yielding the linear relationship between apparent and real depth.

**Given:** $n = 1.45$, $d_{\text{real}} = 8 \text{ cm}$

$$d_{\text{app}} = \frac{8 \text{ cm}}{1.45} = 5.52 \text{ cm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Many students incorrectly write $d_{\text{app}} = d_{\text{real}} \times n$. The correct relationship is **divided** by n. Losing 1 mark here is common.
> 2. Not mentioning that this formula applies only for **small angles** (paraxial rays) and viewing from **directly above**. If the angle is large, the formula becomes invalid.
> 3. Forgetting to convert units consistently (use cm or m throughout, not mixed).

---

**(b) Optical Fibre Principle and NA Derivation:**

**Principle:** An optical fibre consists of a **core** (refractive index $n_1$) surrounded by a **cladding** (refractive index $n_2 < n_1$). Light entering the fibre undergoes repeated **Total Internal Reflection** at the core-cladding interface and propagates along the fibre.

**Derivation of Numerical Aperture:**

The Numerical Aperture is defined as:

$$\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a$$

where $\theta_a$ is the **acceptance angle** (the maximum angle at which light can enter the fibre and still undergo TIR).

Apply Snell's Law at the air-core entry face:

$$n_{\text{air}} \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin(90° - \theta_c) = n_1 \cos\theta_c$$

Using the identity $\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c}$ and substituting $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$:

$$\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{NA} = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Importance in Medical Endoscopy:**
- A **higher NA** allows the fibre to collect more light from a wider cone angle, producing **brighter images** of internal organs.
- It determines the **resolution and field of view** of endoscopic instruments.
- Typical medical fibres have NA between **0.2 and 0.5**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** In curved-surface refraction problems (e.g., the lens formula $\frac{n_2}{v} - \frac{n_1}{u} = \frac{n_2 - n_1}{R}$), students often mix up the sign of R. **Always assume light travels left to right**, distances measured in the direction of light are **positive**, against the light are **negative**.
> 2. **Confusing Critical Angle and Brewster's Angle:** Critical angle is for TIR (no polarization involved). Brewster's angle involves **polarization** — a different topic. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch:** Refractive index has no unit. Writing "n = 1.5 m/s" is a guaranteed 0.5 mark cut.
> 4. **Forgetting the Validity Condition:** When asked for TIR, students sometimes forget to verify $n_1 > n_2$. KTU examiners specifically look for this statement.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** In derivations like Snell's Law, failing to draw the wavefront AB and CD leads to a direct 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these before the exam:**

- **Definition:** Refraction = bending of light due to speed change at a medium interface.
- **Refractive Index Formula:** $n = c/v$; dimensionless; $n_{\text{vacuum}} = 1$.
- **Snell's Law (Equation of the Module):** $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$.
- **Bending Direction:**
  - Denser to Rarer → bends **away** from normal.
  - Rarer to Denser → bends **towards** normal.
- **Critical Angle:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$; valid only if $n_1 > n_2$.
- **Total Internal Reflection:** Occurs when $\theta_i > \theta_c$; no refraction, full reflection.
- **Apparent Depth:** $d_{\text{app}} = d_{\text{real}} / n$.
- **Optical Fibre NA:** $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$.
- **Speed:** $v_{\text{medium}} = c/n$ — frequency never changes during refraction.
- **Medical Applications to Remember:** Endoscopy (TIR), Ophthalmology (cornea/lens), Ultrasound artifacts, OCT.
- **Units:** Angles in degrees, distances in metres/cm, n is dimensionless, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

**🔑 Key Mnemonic — "RARED bends AWAY, DENSE bends TOWARDS":**
- **R**arer to **D**enser = Towards Normal.
- **D**enser to **R**arer = Away from Normal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulsed Ultrasound and Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pulsed Ultrasound and Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Pulsed Ultrasound** refers to a non-continuous mode of acoustic wave emission in which the ultrasound transducer transmits short bursts (pulses) of high-frequency sound energy, typically comprising only **2 to 4 cycles**, separated by relatively long "listening" or "off" periods. Each pulse is followed by a quiescent interval during which the same transducer (or a paired one) functions as a receiver, capturing the echoes reflected from tissue interfaces within the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBMT503, Module 1):** The transition from Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler to **Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler** and **Pulse-Echo Imaging** is the foundational stepping stone to all modern B-mode, M-mode, and Color Doppler diagnostic modalities.

The governing framework is the **Pulse-Echo Principle**, which states that the *time delay* between transmission of an acoustic pulse and reception of its echo from a reflecting interface is directly proportional to the depth of that interface, while the *amplitude* of the returned echo encodes acoustic impedance mismatch information.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Bat Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a dark cave and you want to map its shape. You cannot use a flashlight (light gets absorbed instantly). Instead, you shout "ECHO!" and listen for the sound to bounce back.

- If the sound returns **quickly** → there is a wall very close to you (shallow depth).
- If the sound returns **slowly** → the wall is far away (deep structure).
- If the returning sound is **loud** → the wall is hard and dense (large impedance mismatch, e.g., bone, stone).
- If the sound is **soft and faint** → the surface is sound-absorbing (e.g., soft tissue, fat).

A medical ultrasound machine does *exactly* this, but it shouts **2 million times per second** at a frequency humans cannot hear (typically **2 MHz to 15 MHz**). Each "shout" is one *pulse*, and the listening window between shouts is what allows the system to build a 1-D depth profile. By sweeping this pulse across many lines, a 2-D image is constructed.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of sound in soft tissue (c):** **1540 m/s** (the universally adopted KTU / clinical reference value)
- **Diagnostic frequency range (f):** **2 MHz – 15 MHz**
- **Wavelength in soft tissue (λ):** 0.1 mm – 0.77 mm
- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF):** **1 kHz – 10 kHz** (typical diagnostic range)
- **Duty Factor (DF):** **0.1 % – 1 %** (pulsed mode is "on" less than 1% of the time)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a duty factor of less than 1%?** Because the transducer spends the vast majority of its time *listening* for returning echoes. If it were transmitting continuously (DF = 100%), there would be no silent interval in which to detect the returning echoes — making imaging impossible. This is the core difference between **CW (Continuous Wave)** and **PW (Pulsed Wave)** operation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Pulsed Ultrasound System Works

The imaging chain can be broken into six sequential logical steps. Understanding each is mandatory for KTU Part B questions on this topic.

**Step 1 — Pulse Generation (Transmit Phase).**
A high-voltage pulser (typically **100–300 V**) excites the piezoelectric crystal. The crystal resonates at its natural frequency $f_0$, emitting a short burst of 2–4 sinusoidal cycles. The duration of this burst is the **Pulse Duration (PD)**.

**Step 2 — Propagation into Tissue.**
The acoustic wave propagates at velocity $c = 1540$ m/s through soft tissue. The wave encounters tissue interfaces (boundaries between organs) that have differing **acoustic impedance (Z)**, where $Z = \rho c$ (density × speed of sound).

**Step 3 — Reflection (Echo Formation).**
At each interface, a portion of the incident energy is reflected. The **Reflection Coefficient (R)** is given by:

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

Only a fraction $R$ of the intensity returns; the remainder is transmitted deeper.

**Step 4 — Echo Reception (Listen Phase).**
The returning echo mechanically deforms the piezoelectric crystal, generating a small electrical signal (microvolts to millivolts). The pulser/receiver switch isolates the sensitive receiver from the destructive high-voltage pulser using **diodes** or **transistor switches** with a dead time of ~1 µs.

**Step 5 — Time-of-Flight Measurement.**
The system clock measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$ between transmit and echo reception. The depth $d$ of the reflector is computed using the **Range Equation**.

**Step 6 — Display and Scan Conversion.**
The echo is amplified with **Time Gain Compensation (TGC)** to compensate for attenuation (deeper echoes are weaker), then displayed as a function of time/depth. Sequential lines of sight are assembled into a 2-D image (B-mode).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Pulse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why pulses and not continuous waves?** Three engineering reasons:
> 1. **Depth selectivity:** A pulse allows time-of-flight measurement; a CW signal would have echoes arriving simultaneously with the transmission — impossible to separate.
> 2. **Patient safety:** Lower average acoustic power (lower duty factor → lower thermal/mechanical index).
> 3. **Single transducer operation:** One crystal can both transmit and receive — practical for handheld probes.

### 2.3 The "How" — Pulse Parameters That Govern Imaging

A pulsed ultrasound signal is defined by **three families of parameters**:

**(A) Temporal parameters (along the time axis):**
- **Pulse Duration (PD):** Time from start to end of one pulse burst. Depends on number of cycles $n$ and frequency $f$. Units: microseconds (µs).
- **Pulse Repetition Period (PRP):** Time from the start of one pulse to the start of the next. Units: milliseconds (ms).
- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF):** Inverse of PRP. Units: Hz or kHz.
- **Duty Factor (DF):** Fraction of time the system is actively transmitting.

**(B) Spatial parameters (along the beam axis):**
- **Spatial Pulse Length (SPL):** The physical length in tissue occupied by one pulse. Depends on cycles $n$ and wavelength $\lambda$.
- **Axial Resolution:** The minimum axial separation at which two reflectors can be distinguished. Governed by SPL.

**(C) Resolution parameters:**
- **Axial (Longitudinal) Resolution:** Determined by pulse length; shorter pulse → better resolution.
- **Lateral (Transverse) Resolution:** Determined by beam width and number of scan lines; narrower beam → better resolution.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — Essential Pulsed Ultrasound Formulas (KTU 2024, Module 1).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Pulse Duration | $PD = n / f = n \cdot \lambda / c$ | s | Determines axial resolution |
| 2 | Pulse Repetition Period | $PRP = 1 / PRF$ | s | Listen window length |
| 3 | Pulse Repetition Frequency | $PRF = 1 / PRP$ | Hz | Limits maximum imaging depth |
| 4 | Duty Factor | $DF = PD / PRP = PD \times PRF$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of "ON" time |
| 5 | Duty Factor (%) | $DF(\%) = (PD / PRP) \times 100$ | % | Clinical reporting |
| 6 | Spatial Pulse Length | $SPL = n \cdot \lambda$ | m | Physical pulse extent in tissue |
| 7 | Axial Resolution | $Ax_{res} = SPL / 2 = n \cdot \lambda / 2$ | m | Best case = SPL/2 |
| 8 | Range / Depth Equation | $d = (c \cdot \Delta t) / 2$ | m | Computes depth from echo time |
| 9 | Maximum Imaging Depth | $d_{max} = c \cdot PRP / 2$ | m | Set by PRF (aliasing limit) |
| 10 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) | Determines echo strength |
| 11 | Reflection Coefficient | $R = \left[(Z_2 - Z_1) / (Z_2 + Z_1)\right]^2$ | dimensionless | Fraction of intensity reflected |
| 12 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | m | Sets resolution limit |

> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all KTU answers, use $c = 1540$ m/s for soft tissue unless the question specifies another medium (e.g., bone ≈ 4080 m/s, fat ≈ 1450 m/s, blood ≈ 1570 m/s, air ≈ 330 m/s).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pulsed ultrasound is not a laboratory curiosity — it is the operating principle behind **every** clinical ultrasound scanner manufactured globally (GE, Philips, Siemens, Mindray, Samsung). Specifically, it enables:

- **Obstetric imaging** (fetal scanning) at 3–5 MHz.
- **Cardiac echocardiography** at 2–4 MHz (deep penetration required).
- **Vascular imaging** and Doppler flow quantification.
- **Intra-operative guidance** for biopsies and ablations.
- **Elastography** (pulsed shear waves for tissue stiffness mapping).

The same pulsed principle also underpins **sonar** for submarines and **non-destructive testing (NDT)** of materials and welds in the aerospace and petrochemical industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pulse Duration from Cycles and Frequency

A pulsed ultrasound burst is a finite sinusoid. If the transducer resonates at frequency $f$ (Hz) and emits exactly $n$ complete cycles within the pulse, the total time occupied by the pulse is the period of one cycle ($T = 1/f$) multiplied by the number of cycles $n$.

**Starting point:** One cycle has period $T = 1/f$ seconds.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{For } n \text{ cycles, total pulse duration } PD &= n \times T \\
&= n \times \frac{1}{f} \\
\therefore \quad PD &= \frac{n}{f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Alternative form using wavelength:** Since $\lambda = c / f$, it follows that $1/f = \lambda / c$, so

$$\begin{aligned}
PD &= n \times \frac{\lambda}{c} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{c}
\end{aligned}$$

**Unit check:** $n$ is dimensionless, $\lambda$ is in metres, $c$ is in m/s → result is in seconds. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duty Factor Relationship

The duty factor is defined as the fraction of the pulse repetition period during which the transducer is actively transmitting. By definition:

$$\begin{aligned}
DF &= \frac{\text{Time transmitter is ON}}{\text{Total time of one cycle}} \\
&= \frac{PD}{PRP}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $PRP = 1/PRF$, we can substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
DF &= \frac{PD}{1/PRF} = PD \times PRF
\end{aligned}$$

**Expressing as a percentage (more common in KTU answers):**

$$\begin{aligned}
DF(\%) &= \frac{PD}{PRP} \times 100 \\
&= PD \times PRF \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Range Equation (Most Important in KTU Exams)

Consider a transducer placed on the skin surface. It emits an acoustic pulse at time $t = 0$. The pulse travels through tissue at speed $c$ until it strikes a reflector at depth $d$, and the echo returns along the same path at speed $c$. The system records the round-trip time as $\Delta t$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

The pulse travels *outward* to the reflector: distance $d$ at speed $c$ → time taken = $d/c$.

The echo travels *backward* from the reflector: distance $d$ at speed $c$ → time taken = $d/c$.

Total round-trip time:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} \\
\Delta t &= \frac{2d}{c}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for depth $d$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2d &= c \cdot \Delta t \\
\therefore \quad d &= \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The factor of 2 is the most-skipped step in KTU scripts.** Examiners explicitly look for the round-trip reasoning. Marks are lost when students write $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Imaging Depth (PRF Limit)

For unambiguous imaging, all echoes from one pulse must return *before* the next pulse is transmitted. The deepest possible unambiguous depth corresponds to the round-trip time equalling the entire PRP.

**Setting the condition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Round-trip time to deepest unambiguous reflector} &= PRP \\
\frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{c} &= PRP = \frac{1}{PRF}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $d_{max}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot d_{max} &= \frac{c}{PRF} \\
\therefore \quad d_{max} &= \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} = \frac{c \cdot PRP}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Rule of Thumb:** A common KTU numerical is PRF = 5 kHz. Then $d_{max} = 1540 / (2 \times 5000) = 0.154$ m = **15.4 cm**. Going deeper than this produces **range ambiguity aliasing**, where the system misplaces deep echoes at shallow apparent depths.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Spatial Pulse Length and Axial Resolution

The spatial pulse length is the *physical length in tissue* that the pulse occupies at any instant. It is the number of cycles $n$ multiplied by the wavelength $\lambda$ in tissue.

$$\begin{aligned}
SPL &= n \cdot \lambda
\end{aligned}$$

**Why divide by 2 to get axial resolution?**

Axial resolution is the minimum distance between two reflectors lying *along the beam axis* that can be displayed as two separate echoes. Consider two closely spaced reflectors. The leading edge of the pulse must reach the *second* reflector before the trailing edge leaves the *first* reflector — otherwise their echoes merge.

The minimum separation satisfying this is half the spatial pulse length:

$$\begin{aligned}
Ax_{res} &= \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shorter pulse → better axial resolution.** A 2-cycle pulse at 5 MHz has $\lambda = 0.308$ mm, so $Ax_{res} = 2 \times 0.308 / 2 = 0.308$ mm. Excellent resolution. This is why modern scanners use **broadband short pulses** and **coded excitation** techniques.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A diagnostic ultrasound scanner uses a 5 MHz transducer, emits pulses containing 3 cycles each, with a PRF of 4 kHz. For soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s), calculate: (i) Pulse Duration, (ii) Duty Factor (in %), (iii) Maximum imaging depth, (iv) Spatial Pulse Length, (v) Axial Resolution.

**Given data:**
- $f = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $n = 3$ cycles
- $PRF = 4 \times 10^3$ Hz
- $c = 1540$ m/s

**Step (i) — Pulse Duration:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PD &= \frac{n}{f} = \frac{3}{5 \times 10^6} \\
&= 6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.6 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (ii) — Duty Factor (%):**

$$\begin{aligned}
DF(\%) &= PD \times PRF \times 100 \\
&= (6 \times 10^{-7}) \times (4 \times 10^3) \times 100 \\
&= 2.4 \times 10^{-1} \% \\
&= 0.24 \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iii) — Maximum imaging depth:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{max} &= \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 4000} \\
&= \frac{1540}{8000} \\
&= 0.1925 \text{ m} \\
&= 19.25 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (iv) — Spatial Pulse Length:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
SPL &= n \cdot \lambda = 3 \times 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 9.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.924 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (v) — Axial Resolution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Ax_{res} &= \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{0.924 \text{ mm}}{2} \\
&= 0.462 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sanity check:** All values are clinically realistic. A 5 MHz scanner imaging to ~19 cm depth with 0.46 mm axial resolution is typical of a general-purpose abdominal probe. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pulsed Ultrasound Imaging System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the full signal path from the operator's pulse trigger to the final displayed B-mode image. Each block represents a subsystem whose function must be understood for KTU Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Clock / Timing Generator] -->|Trigger pulse| B[Pulser 100-300V]
    B -->|High voltage spike| C[Transducer Piezoelectric Crystal]
    C -->|Acoustic pulse 2 to 4 cycles| D[Patient Body Tissue]
    D -->|Echoes return| C
    C -->|Microvolt receive signal| E[TR Switch Transmit Receive]
    E -->|Protect receiver during transmit| F[RF Amplifier Low Noise]
    F --> G[Time Gain Compensation TGC]
    G --> H[Bandpass Filter and Beamformer]
    H --> I[Envelope Detector and Log Compression]
    I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter]
    J --> K[Scan Converter Memory]
    K --> L[Display Monitor B mode image]

    M[PRF Control Unit] -.->|Sets pulse rate| A
    N[Depth Selection] -.->|Sets PRP and TGC slope| A
    O[Focusing Lens Array] -.->|Electronic delay control| H
```

**Reading the diagram for KTU answers:**

- **Master Clock** sets the **PRF** and the **PRP** (inverse of each other).
- **Pulser** energizes the **transducer**; the TR (Transmit-Receive) switch protects the sensitive front-end electronics from the destructive pulser voltage.
- **TGC** is *clinically essential* — without it, deep echoes would be invisible (attenuation in soft tissue is ~**0.5 dB/cm/MHz**, so a 10 cm deep 5 MHz echo is attenuated by 25 dB, i.e., weakened by a factor of ~316).
- **Scan Converter** aligns A-line data spatially so the rectangular pixel grid of the monitor matches the polar geometry of the scan.

### 4.2 Mermaid — Pulse Timing Diagram (As State Chart)

This chart visualizes the temporal relationship between Transmit, Listen, and Display phases within one Pulse Repetition Period.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Transmit
    Transmit: Transmit Phase
    note right of Transmit
        Duration equals PD
        2 to 4 cycles of f0
        DF percent of PRP
    end note
    Transmit --> Listen: Pulser OFF
    Listen: Listen Phase
    note right of Listen
        Echoes arrive here
        Time to depth d equals c into 2d
        Receiver active
    end note
    Listen --> Display: All echoes received
    Display: Display and Compute Phase
    note right of Display
        TGC applied
        Scan conversion
        Pixel intensity updated
    end note
    Display --> Transmit: Next PRP begins
    Transmit --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Diagram Questions:** When asked to "draw and label the pulse timing diagram," ensure you show: (1) two or more pulses with their short width PD, (2) the long interval PRP between them, (3) the echo returns positioned *within* the listen window at the correct time-of-flight positions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A comprises short-answer questions. Each answer should be **3 to 4 sentences** with one supporting equation or numerical example. Strict 3-mark valuation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for key equation, 1 mark for the application/significance.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Duty Factor. State its typical range in diagnostic pulsed ultrasound and explain why it is so small.**

**Model Answer:**
The Duty Factor (DF) is the fraction of the Pulse Repetition Period (PRP) during which the transducer is actively transmitting an acoustic pulse. It is defined as:

$$DF = \frac{PD}{PRP} = PD \times PRF$$

In clinical diagnostic pulsed ultrasound, the duty factor is typically **0.1 % to 1 %**, meaning the transducer is "on" less than 1 % of the time. This very small value exists because the transducer must spend the vast majority of its time in *listen mode* to receive returning echoes — a continuous wave (DF = 100 %) would make depth-selective imaging impossible, since echoes would arrive simultaneously with the transmission.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is Spatial Pulse Length? How does it determine axial resolution in ultrasound imaging?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Spatial Pulse Length (SPL)** is the physical length in tissue occupied by one transmitted acoustic pulse, given by the product of the number of cycles $n$ and the wavelength $\lambda$ in tissue:

$$SPL = n \cdot \lambda$$

It directly determines **axial resolution** — the minimum separation along the beam axis at which two reflectors can be displayed as distinct echoes. The axial resolution equals half the SPL:

$$Ax_{res} = \frac{SPL}{2} = \frac{n \cdot \lambda}{2}$$

Shorter pulses (fewer cycles, higher frequency → smaller $\lambda$) yield better axial resolution. For example, a 2-cycle pulse at 10 MHz gives $Ax_{res} \approx 0.15$ mm, allowing fine structural detail.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 1 Trap:** The single most common mark-loss in this module is *forgetting the factor of 2 in the range equation*. Any depth/time calculation without $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ (instead of $d = c \cdot \Delta t$) loses 2 to 3 marks immediately. The second most common error is using $c = 330$ m/s (air) instead of $c = 1540$ m/s (soft tissue) when not specified. Read the medium carefully.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)/(b)]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Pulse Duration, Pulse Repetition Period, Duty Factor, and Pulse Repetition Frequency. A 3.5 MHz transducer emits 4-cycle pulses at a PRF of 2 kHz. Calculate the Pulse Duration, Duty Factor (in %), and the Pulse Repetition Period for soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the Pulse-Echo principle used in diagnostic ultrasound imaging. Derive the range equation $d = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ and clearly justify why the factor of 2 appears. A reflector returns an echo 78 µs after transmission. Find the depth of the reflector and the maximum unambiguous imaging depth for a PRF of 5 kHz. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **Pulse Duration (PD)** is the time occupied by $n$ cycles of frequency $f$:

$$PD = \frac{n}{f} = \frac{4}{3.5 \times 10^6} = 1.143 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.143 \text{ µs}$$

The **Pulse Repetition Period (PRP)** is the inverse of PRF:

$$PRP = \frac{1}{PRF} = \frac{1}{2000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 500 \text{ µs}$$

The **Duty Factor** is the ratio of ON-time to total period:

$$DF = \frac{PD}{PRP} = \frac{1.143 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.286 \times 10^{-3}$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$DF(\%) = 0.2286 \% \approx 0.23\%$$

This confirms DF is far less than 1 % — the transducer is silent for over 99.77 % of the time, dedicated to echo reception.

**Model Solution to (b):**

In the **Pulse-Echo principle**, a short ultrasound pulse is transmitted into the body. When the pulse strikes a tissue interface, part of its energy is reflected back as an echo. The depth of the reflector is determined by measuring the round-trip time $\Delta t$.

**Derivation:** The pulse travels *outward* to the reflector at depth $d$ in time $d/c$. The echo travels *back* the same distance in another $d/c$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= \frac{d}{c} + \frac{d}{c} = \frac{2d}{c} \\
\therefore \quad d &= \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

The factor of 2 exists because the wave makes a complete round trip — once going to the reflector, and once returning.

**Numerical part:** For $\Delta t = 78$ µs:

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 78 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.12012}{2} = 0.06006 \text{ m} \approx 6.0 \text{ cm}$$

For $PRF = 5$ kHz, the maximum unambiguous depth is:

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 5000} = 0.154 \text{ m} = 15.4 \text{ cm}$$

Since 6.0 cm < 15.4 cm, the echo is unambiguously located. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q2(a)/(b)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a pulsed ultrasound imaging system. Briefly state the function of each block, with special emphasis on the role of the TGC (Time Gain Compensation) amplifier. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Define Spatial Pulse Length and Axial Resolution. A 7 MHz pulsed ultrasound system uses 2-cycle pulses. Calculate: (i) the wavelength in soft tissue, (ii) the Spatial Pulse Length, and (iii) the Axial Resolution. Comment on how these values would change if the system were re-tuned to 10 MHz with the same number of cycles. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The functional architecture consists of the following blocks (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4):

1. **Master Clock / Timing Generator** — sets the Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) and synchronizes transmit/receive events.
2. **Pulser (High-Voltage Excitation)** — applies a 100–300 V spike to the piezoelectric crystal, launching an acoustic pulse.
3. **Transducer (Piezoelectric Crystal)** — converts electrical energy to acoustic energy on transmit, and vice versa on receive.
