By measuring the **time delay** $t$ between transmission and reception of the echo, and knowing the **velocity of sound** $c$ in the medium, the **depth** of the reflector can be determined. Since the same transducer acts as transmitter and receiver, the pulse traverses the depth $d$ **twice** (once down, once up). If the round-trip time is $t$, the time to traverse $d$ once is $t/2$. By the fundamental kinematic relation $d = \text{speed} \times \text{time}$: $$ d = c \times \frac{t}{2} = \frac{c t}{2} $$ This is the **master equation** of all pulse-echo modalities. For soft tissue, $c = 1540$ m/s, so a 1 µs round-trip delay corresponds to a depth of 0.77 mm. The strength of each echo depends on the **acoustic impedance mismatch** at the interface. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** $c = 1540$ m/s; $t_1 = 52\;\mu s$; $t_2 = 104\;\mu s$; Bandwidth = 3 MHz. **(1) Depth of first structure:** $$ d_1 = \frac{c \, t_1}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 52 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.08008}{2} = 0.04004 \;\text{m} = \mathbf{4.0 \; cm} $$ **(2) Depth of second structure:** $$ d_2 = \frac{c \, t_2}{2} = \frac{1540 \times 104 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.16016}{2} = 0.08008 \;\text{m} = \mathbf{8.0 \; cm} $$ **(3) Separation between the two structures:** $$ \Delta d = d_2 - d_1 = 8.0 - 4.0 = \mathbf{4.0 \; cm} $$ **(4) Axial Resolution:** $$ \Delta d_{\min} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{BW}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^6} = \frac{1540}{6 \times 10^6} = 2.567 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} \approx \mathbf{0.257 \; mm} $$ **Conclusion:** The system can distinguish reflectors separated by as little as **0.26 mm** along the beam axis — well within the 4 cm separation of the two structures, so they will appear as **two distinct spikes** on the A-mode display. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in A-Mode Questions (read this twice before the exam):** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of ½** in $d = ct/2$. The examiner explicitly checks for this — losing the factor doubles your answer and **costs 2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Mixing up $R_p$ and $R_I$** in the reflection coefficient question. The pressure reflection coefficient is $R_p = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$, while the **intensity** reflection coefficient is its **square**. If the question says "percentage of intensity reflected," you **must square it**. > 3. **Using the wrong unit of acoustic impedance.** Always express in **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) or MRayl (×10⁶)**. Mixing CGS and SI loses marks. > 4. **Confusing axial and lateral resolution.** A-mode is a 1-D technique — there is no lateral resolution to compute, only **axial resolution** = $c/(2 \cdot BW)$. > 5. **Omitting the TR switch** in the block diagram. This is a "must-draw" block; skipping it costs at least 1 mark. > 6. **Forgetting the assumption $c = 1540$ m/s** in numericals when not given. State it explicitly: *"Assuming soft tissue, $c = 1540$ m/s."* > 7. **Saying "ultrasound is sound above 20 kHz"** in the introduction — this is technically correct but examiners want the *clinical* definition: *"non-ionizing acoustic radiation in the 1–15 MHz range used for medical diagnosis."* --- ## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **A-Mode = Amplitude Mode**, the **1-D grandfather** of all ultrasound imaging. - Built on the **pulse-echo principle**: same transducer transmits and receives. - **Master equation:** $d = ct/2$, with $c = 1540$ m/s in soft tissue. - **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$. Reflection at an interface: $R_I = \left[(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)\right]^2$. - **Block diagram (7 blocks):** Pulser → TR Switch → Transducer → Tissue → TR Switch → TGC Amplifier → Detector → Time-Base → CRT. - **Spike height ∝ echo strength ∝ impedance mismatch.** - **Spike X-position ∝ depth ∝ time of flight.** - **Axial resolution** $= c/(2 \cdot \text{Bandwidth})$. - **Clinical applications:** ophthalmic biometry, echoencephalography (midline shift detection), breast tumour characterization, biopsy needle guidance. - **Key limitation:** Only 1-D — no anatomical image; largely **superseded by B-mode** in modern practice, but still used where **sub-millimetre precision** matters. - **Frequencies used:** **1–15 MHz** (low for deep organs like liver, high for superficial structures like the eye). - **Attenuation in soft tissue:** roughly **0.5 dB/cm/MHz** (round-trip gives **1 dB/cm/MHz**), justifying the need for TGC. - **Cannot image through air or bone** because of huge impedance mismatch — this is why an **acoustic coupling gel** is mandatory. - **One sentence answer for 3-mark question:** *"A-mode is a one-dimensional pulse-echo ultrasound display in which echo amplitude is plotted against depth (time-of-flight)."* - **Number to remember:** **$c = 1540$ m/s** in soft tissue; **1 µs ↔ 0.77 mm** depth (round-trip). --- > *End of Module 1 — A-Mode Notes | PCBMT503 | KTU 2024 Scheme* ✅

topics.title: B-Mode and M-Mode Scanning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** B-Mode and M-Mode Scanning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are B-Mode and M-Mode?

In diagnostic ultrasound, the raw radio-frequency (RF) echo received from tissues carries three pieces of information for every sample point along the beam axis: **(i) Time-of-flight (depth), (ii) Amplitude (reflectivity), and (iii) Phase / Frequency (Doppler shift)**. The *display mode* decides which of these properties is mapped to a visible pixel on the monitor.

| Mode | Full Name | What is displayed? | Dimensionality |
|------|-----------|--------------------|----------------|
| **A-Mode** | Amplitude Mode | Echo amplitude as vertical spikes on a baseline | 1-D (depth) |
| **B-Mode** | Brightness Mode | Echo amplitude mapped to **grayscale brightness** of a 2-D pixel | 2-D (spatial) |
| **M-Mode** | Motion Mode | B-mode brightness line plotted as a function of time | 1-D (depth) × Time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** B-Mode and M-Mode are the two fundamental *real-time* pulse-echo imaging modes forming the backbone of every commercial ultrasound scanner. Every other advanced mode (Color Doppler, Elastography, 3-D/4-D) is built on top of the B-Mode pixel pipeline.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of B-Mode (Brightness Mode)

**B-Mode imaging** is a two-dimensional, real-time, pulse-echo ultrasonographic technique in which the amplitude of the returning echo from each tissue interface is encoded as the **luminance (grayscale intensity)** of a corresponding pixel, and the position of that pixel is determined by the **range (depth) and the angular/azimuthal orientation** of the transmitted ultrasound beam. A complete 2-D image (called a *B-scan*) is built by sequentially scanning the beam across the tissue and stacking successive range-gated brightness lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Grayscale Mapping:** Echo amplitudes in clinical scanners span a dynamic range of approximately **60–100 dB**. This is compressed logarithmically and mapped onto a displayable grayscale of **256 levels (8-bit)**, with the strongest reflectors (bone, gas) appearing *white* and anechoic regions (urine, cyst fluid) appearing *black*.

## 1.3 Formal Definition of M-Mode (Motion Mode)

**M-Mode imaging** is a one-dimensional, time-resolved pulse-echo display mode in which the ultrasound beam is held stationary along a single, user-selected axis (usually the *M-line* drawn across a structure of interest, e.g., a heart valve), and the resulting depth-vs-amplitude B-line is **swept horizontally across the screen at a constant velocity**. The horizontal axis therefore represents **time**, and the vertical axis represents **depth**. The motion of moving structures (cardiac walls, valves, fetal heart) is recorded as undulating bright/dark patterns — the **"echocardiographic signature"** of that structure.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

### The Bat-and-Cave Analogy for B-Mode
Imagine you are standing in a dark cave holding a flashlight that you can rotate left–right and tilt up–down. Each flash sends out a sound wave, and you *listen* for the echo. The **louder the echo**, the closer a wall is. Now imagine you record on a piece of paper: for each direction, you draw a dot, and the **darker the dot, the louder the echo**. Sweep the flashlight across the cave and you have drawn a 2-D map of the cave walls. That is exactly what a B-Mode scanner does — the *flashlight* is the **phased/linear array transducer**, the *echo* is the reflected acoustic wave, and the *dotted paper* is the **scan-converted B-image** on the LCD.

### The Heartbeat-Strip-Chart Analogy for M-Mode
Now imagine you fix the flashlight on **one single direction** through the cave, pointing at a swinging pendulum. Instead of drawing dots, you drag a piece of **moving chart paper horizontally past the beam** at a steady speed. Every echo becomes a vertical smudge on the paper. As time passes, the pendulum moves up and down, and the smudge traces a wavy line. That wavy line is the **M-Mode trace** — *depth on the vertical axis, time on the horizontal axis*, with brightness showing echo strength. Cardiologists literally read the M-mode trace like reading a heart's signature.

---

## 1.5 Why Two Modes? — The Clinical Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **B-Mode** answers the question: *"Where is the lesion, and what does it look like?"* — used for **anatomy, morphology, and static diagnosis**.
>
> **M-Mode** answers the question: *"How fast and how far is the boundary moving?"* — used for **quantitative motion analysis**, especially cardiac valve timing, wall-thickness changes through the cardiac cycle, and fetal heart rate.

| Feature | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Best for | Anatomy / static structure | Dynamic motion / timing |
| Frame rate | 15–60 fps (real-time) | **> 1000 fps** (very high temporal resolution) |
| Image format | 2-D fan / rectangle | 1-D horizontal strip |
| Typical use | Abdomen, obstetrics, vascular | Echocardiography, fetal heart |
| Operator input | Sweep beam automatically | Beam held on chosen axis |
| Data rate | High (entire frame each cycle) | Low (single line) |

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Speed of sound in soft tissue: c ≈ 1540 m/s** (this is the universal value used by every commercial scanner; deviates < 5% across most soft tissues).
- **Diagnostic ultrasound frequency range: 2 – 15 MHz** (higher = better resolution, lower penetration).
- **Axial resolution: ≈ 0.5 mm at 5 MHz**, scales as c/(2 × Δf) where Δf is transducer bandwidth.
- **Standard B-Mode display depth: 256 grayscale levels (8-bit)**, dynamic range compression ≈ 50–60 dB applied to the raw ~100 dB RF echo range.
- **Clinical M-Mode sweep speed: 25, 50, 100 mm/s** on the time axis.
- **Real-time B-Mode frame rate: 15–60 frames/s** (limited by depth × lines per frame × c/2).
- **M-Mode effective frame/line rate: 1000–2000 lines/s** (limited only by depth and PRF).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The B-Mode Imaging Pipeline — Step by Step

The B-Mode image is *not* a single physical event but a tightly synchronized **digital signal-processing chain**. Let us walk through it layer by layer.

### Step 1 — Beamforming (Transmit + Receive)
The system fires a focused, short, ~2-cycle acoustic pulse along one scan line using either:
- A **linear array** (rectangular image, beam steered/electronically focused in azimuth),
- A **curvilinear / convex array** (trapezoidal/wedge image, used in abdominal scanning), or
- A **phased array** (sector image, beam steered ± 45°, used in cardiac and intercostal scanning).

On receive, **dynamic receive focusing** is applied: time-varying analog/digital delays are added to each array element so that echoes from the current range depth add constructively — this yields a sharp, in-focus A-line.

### Step 2 — Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)
Because deeper echoes have travelled farther and suffered **attenuation** (≈ 0.5–1 dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue), the system applies depth-dependent amplification — typically 0.3 dB/cm/MHz up to a depth-dependent maximum — so that equal reflectors at different depths produce equal brightness.

### Step 3 — Envelope Detection (Rectification + Low-Pass Filtering)
The high-frequency (MHz) RF echo is passed through a **rectifier + low-pass filter** to extract the slowly varying *envelope* — i.e., the echo amplitude vs. time. This is essential because the monitor cannot display a 5 MHz signal directly.

### Step 4 — Logarithmic Compression
The envelope is compressed logarithmically (typically 40–60 dB compressed to 20–30 dB display range) so that the eye can perceive both weak and strong reflectors on the same 8-bit grayscale.

### Step 5 — Scan Conversion
Polar-coordinate echo data (range along beam, beam angle across the frame) is interpolated onto a **Cartesian (x, y) pixel grid** of typically 512 × 512 or 1024 × 1024 pixels. This is done in a **scan-converter memory** (originally analog, now fully digital). Without scan conversion, the B-image would appear as a raw fan/sector with non-uniform pixel density.

### Step 6 — Persistence & Frame Averaging
To reduce speckle noise, several successive frames (typically 2–4) are temporally averaged ("persistence") before display.

### Step 7 — Display
The final 8-bit pixel array is written to the LCD at the chosen frame rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept — Polar-to-Cartesian Scan Conversion:** The raw echo is in *polar* form (r, θ). The display is in *Cartesian* form (x, y). The conversion is x = r·sin(θ), y = r·cos(θ). Modern systems use a *digital scan converter (DSC)* implemented in FPGA/DSP/ASIC hardware to do this in real time.

---

## 2.2 The M-Mode Imaging Pipeline — Step by Step

M-Mode is conceptually a *one-line, time-stretched* B-Mode.

### Step 1 — Beam Fixation
The operator places an **M-line cursor** on the B-Mode image along which the M-trace is desired. The transducer's beamforming is electronically locked to that single line.

### Step 2 — PRF-Driven Repeated A-Lines
The system fires a transmit pulse at the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** along this single line. For a 15 cm depth, the round-trip time is:

$$
t_{RT} = \frac{2 d}{c} = \frac{2 \times 0.15}{1540} \approx 195 \, \mu s
$$

so the maximum PRF (and hence the A-line sampling rate) is:

$$
\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{RT}} = \frac{c}{2 d} \approx 5130 \, \text{Hz}
$$

i.e., **~5000 A-lines per second** — that becomes the M-Mode temporal sampling rate.

### Step 3 — Envelope Detection (Same as B-Mode)
Each returning A-line is envelope-detected, log-compressed, and converted to a single vertical column of brightness values (depth axis).

### Step 4 — Horizontal Time-Sweep
Successive columns are written into scan-converter memory so that column 1 is at the left edge, column 2 just to the right, etc. The horizontal scroll rate (sweep speed) is user-selectable (25, 50, 100 mm/s).

### Step 5 — Display
The accumulated columns form the M-Mode "strip-chart." Bright = reflective interface, Dark = anechoic region. Motion of structures (e.g., mitral valve leaflets) is seen as undulating bright lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** M-Mode is essentially a *one-dimensional, very high-frame-rate B-Mode*. The reason cardiologists still use it (despite the availability of 2-D B-Mode and Doppler) is its **superior temporal resolution** (>1000 samples/s vs. 30–60 fps for B-Mode), which is essential for measuring rapid valve events like mitral leaflet opening in early diastole.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of B-Mode | Use of M-Mode |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| **Obstetrics** | Fetal biometry, anomaly scan | Fetal heart rate, cardiac rhythm |
| **Cardiology** | Wall-motion analysis, EF estimation | Valve timing, wall-thickness vs time |
| **Abdominal** | Liver/kidney lesion characterization | Rarely used |
| **Vascular** | Plaque detection, intima-media thickness | (Limited; Doppler preferred) |
| **Emergency / POCUS** | FAST scan, eFAST, line placement | Cardiac tamponade, RV collapse |
| **MSK** | Tendon tears, joint effusion | Tendon movement under dynamic load |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📐

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know for KTU ESE** (End Semester Examination). Memory-tip given in column 4.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | KTU Memory Tip |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Round-trip time | $t_{RT} = \dfrac{2d}{c}$ | seconds | "There-and-back" |
| 2 | Maximum depth (unambiguous) | $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | metres | "Half-PRF, full c" |
| 3 | Pulse Repetition Frequency (max) | $\text{PRF}_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{max}}$ | Hz | Inverse of round-trip |
| 4 | Frame rate (B-Mode) | $F_r = \dfrac{\text{PRF}}{N_L}$ | frames/s | "PRF divided by lines" |
| 5 | Lines per frame | $N_L = F_r \cdot t_{RT} \cdot \text{PRF}/F_r$ | lines | $N_L = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{max} F_r}$ |
| 6 | Axial resolution | $\delta_z = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | metres | c over 2×bandwidth |
| 7 | Lateral resolution | $\delta_x \approx \dfrac{\lambda z}{D}$ | metres | λ·depth/aperture |
| 8 | Attenuation in tissue | $A = \alpha \cdot f \cdot d$ | dB | α ≈ 0.5–1 dB/cm/MHz |
| 9 | M-Mode sweep rate | $v_{sweep} = \dfrac{\Delta x_{display}}{\Delta t_{real}}$ | mm/s | Display speed |
| 10 | M-Mode time per cm screen | $t = \dfrac{10}{v_{sweep}}$ | s/cm | Inverse of sweep rate |
| 11 | M-Mode temporal samples | $N_M = \text{PRF} \cdot T_{total}$ | samples | PRF × duration |
| 12 | Speckle (Rayleigh) mean brightness | $\langle I \rangle = \sigma^2$ (Rayleigh envelope²) | — | "Mean = variance" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Always use **c = 1540 m/s** for soft tissue in numerical problems unless explicitly stated otherwise. Using c = 1500 m/s or 340 m/s (air) will be marked **wrong** in board valuation.

---

## 2.5 Physical / Mathematical Intuition for the Frame-Rate Trade-off

The three quantities **d_max (depth), N_L (lines/frame), F_r (frame rate)** form an unbreakable triangle. Combining them:

$$
F_r = \frac{\text{PRF}_{max}}{N_L} = \frac{c}{2 \, d_{max} \, N_L}
$$

This is the **fundamental B-Mode trade-off equation**. Reading it aloud:

> *"You cannot have all three at once. If you increase the imaging depth, you must reduce either the line density, the frame rate, or both."*

**Example reading:** For a 15 cm deep cardiac image at 60 fps, lines per frame = $\frac{1540}{2 \times 0.15 \times 60} \approx 85$ lines — perfectly adequate for a sector scan. For a 20 cm abdominal scan at 30 fps, lines per frame ≈ 128.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Why phased arrays win for cardiac imaging:** A phased array with 64–128 elements can produce a 90° sector with 128–256 lines, but its small footprint (~2 cm) fits between the ribs. A linear array would need a footprint of 4–6 cm — impossible intercostally. The trade-off: phased arrays have worse lateral resolution away from the apex, but this is the price of fitting into the intercostal window.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU derivation list for Module 1:** (i) Round-trip time and maximum unambiguous depth, (ii) Frame-rate–line-density–depth relationship, (iii) Axial resolution from pulse bandwidth, (iv) Scan-conversion geometry. These are derived below *in full*, with no skipped steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Round-Trip Time and Maximum Unambiguous Depth

### Goal
Show that the maximum depth from which a pulse can return and be unambiguously assigned is $d_{max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$.

### Step 1 — Define the round-trip time
Let a pulse be transmitted at $t = 0$. It travels to a reflector at axial depth $d$ and back. The total path length is $2d$.

The travel time for the pulse is, by definition of speed $c$:

$$
t_{RT} = \frac{\text{total path}}{\text{speed}} = \frac{2 d}{c}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* We use the simple kinematic relation time = distance / speed, but applied to the **total** path (there and back), which is why the factor of 2 appears in the numerator.

### Step 2 — Define the Pulse Repetition Interval
The transmitter cannot fire a new pulse until the previous pulse's echo from the *deepest* reflector has returned. If the pulse repetition interval is $T_{PRF} = 1/\text{PRF}$, then we *must* have:

$$
T_{PRF} \geq t_{RT,\text{max}} = \frac{2 d_{max}}{c}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is a causality constraint — the system is "blind" while a pulse is in flight, so the next pulse must wait for the last echo to return or it will create a *range ambiguity* (the new pulse's echo would overlap with the previous pulse's deep echo).

### Step 3 — Solve for the maximum depth
Rearranging the inequality (using the maximum-PRF case where $T_{PRF} = t_{RT,\text{max}}$):

$$
d_{max} = \frac{c \cdot T_{PRF}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* We just inverted $T_{PRF} = 1/\text{PRF}$ and substituted back. The factor 2 in the denominator is the hallmark of pulse-echo ranging — **always remember the "2"**.

### Step 4 — Substituting standard tissue speed c = 1540 m/s
For a typical abdominal exam at $d_{max} = 20$ cm:

$$
\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.20} = \frac{1540}{0.40} = 3850 \text{ Hz}
$$

For a cardiac exam at $d_{max} = 15$ cm:

$$
\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.15} \approx 5133 \text{ Hz}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frame-Rate / Line-Density / Depth Triangle

### Goal
Derive the relation $F_r = \dfrac{c}{2 d_{max} N_L}$ that constrains any real-time B-Mode scanner.

### Step 1 — Total time to build one frame
To form one B-frame, the scanner must fire $N_L$ pulses (one per scan line) and wait for each echo. Assuming the system is PRF-limited (i.e., we use the maximum PRF for the chosen depth), the time to build one frame is:

$$
T_{frame} = N_L \cdot T_{PRF,max} = N_L \cdot \frac{2 d_{max}}{c}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Each scan line is independent and must wait its full round-trip; we just sum $N_L$ such waits. This is the "worst case" — modern scanners can interleave lines, but the time budget is fundamentally the same.

### Step 2 — Frame rate is the inverse
By definition, the frame rate in frames per second is:

$$
F_r = \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{c}{2 d_{max} N_L}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $N_L$ given $F_r$ and $d_{max}$ (typical KTU numerical)
Rearranging:

$$
N_L = \frac{c}{2 d_{max} F_r}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked numerical (typical 7-mark KTU part-(a))
**Problem:** A B-Mode scanner images to a maximum depth of **18 cm** at a frame rate of **40 fps** in soft tissue. Find the number of scan lines per frame.

**Solution:**

$$
N_L = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.18 \times 40} = \frac{1540}{14.4} \approx 106.94
$$

Rounding down (we cannot have a fractional scan line):

$$
N_L \approx 106 \text{ lines/frame}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The unrounded value is the **theoretical** maximum; in practice the system uses the floor of this number. The 0.06 lines/frame "loss" is real-world overhead that the scan converter absorbs.

### Step 5 — Cross-check via PRF
The maximum PRF at 18 cm depth is $\text{PRF} = c/(2d) = 1540/(0.36) \approx 4278$ Hz. The product $\text{PRF} \times T_{frame} = 4278 \times 1/40 \approx 107$ lines. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Axial Resolution from Pulse Bandwidth

### Goal
Prove that the axial (range) resolution of pulse-echo ultrasound is $\delta_z = \dfrac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ where $\Delta f$ is the transducer's −6 dB bandwidth.

### Step 1 — Axial resolution definition
Two reflectors are *just resolvable* axially when their echo pulses do not overlap in time. The minimum time separation $\Delta t$ between two such pulses is set by the **pulse duration**.

### Step 2 — Pulse duration in terms of bandwidth
A short pulse in time has a broad bandwidth in frequency. For a Gaussian pulse, the time-bandwidth product is:

$$
\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \approx 0.44
$$

For ultrasound we use the engineering approximation:

$$
\Delta t \approx \frac{1}{\Delta f}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The bandwidth $\Delta f$ is the −6 dB width of the transducer's frequency response, and the pulse duration is approximately the inverse — a fundamental result from Fourier theory. We drop the 0.44 constant because KTU problems always use the engineering form.

### Step 3 — Convert time separation to spatial separation
A time separation $\Delta t$ in the echo corresponds to a spatial separation along the beam of:

$$
\Delta z = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The factor 2 is the *round-trip* factor: the time difference between two echoes corresponds to twice the spatial separation between the two reflectors (one extra round trip).

### Step 4 — Numerical
For a 5 MHz probe with 60% fractional bandwidth ($\Delta f = 3$ MHz):

$$
\delta_z = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^6} = \frac{1540}{6 \times 10^6} \approx 2.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.257 \text{ mm}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is *excellent* resolution — about 4× finer than typical clinical needs (≈ 1 mm). The cost of high resolution is reduced penetration (higher attenuation at higher f).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scan-Conversion Geometry (Polar to Cartesian)

### Goal
Show how a polar-coordinate B-mode echo (range r along a beam at angle θ) is mapped to a Cartesian pixel (x, y).

### Step 1 — Define the polar echo position
A sample is acquired at range r along a beam whose orientation is θ (measured from the transducer's central axis).

### Step 2 — Standard polar-to-Cartesian transform

$$
x = r \sin\theta
$$

$$
y = r \cos\theta
$$

> *Conversion logic:* This is the standard rotation/extension of a polar vector to a 2-D Cartesian frame. The y-axis is along the central beam, x is lateral.

### Step 3 — Pixel addressing
In a digital scan converter, the integer pixel index is:

$$
i = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{x}{\Delta x} + \frac{N_x}{2}\right), \quad j = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{y}{\Delta y}\right)
$$

where $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are pixel pitches and $N_x$ is the display width in pixels.

### Step 4 — Bi-linear interpolation
Because the polar samples do not fall on a regular Cartesian grid, the scan converter performs **bi-linear interpolation** between the four nearest polar samples to assign a single Cartesian pixel value. This is what gives the B-image its smooth (rather than pixelated) appearance.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Exam Use

| Property | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|----------|--------|--------|--------|
| Display dimension | 1-D (depth only) | 2-D (depth × lateral) | 1-D (depth) × Time |
| Pixel information | Spikes (amplitude) | Grayscale pixel (brightness) | Grayscale pixel scrolling |
| Beam motion | Fixed | Scanned | Fixed on one line |
| Frame rate / sample rate | One pulse = one line | 15–60 fps | 1000–5000 lines/s |
| Data per acquisition | ~1 KB | ~250 KB (512² × 1 byte) | ~10 KB / second |
| Best for | Biometry, eye | Anatomy, lesion detection | Cardiac motion |
| Limitation | No spatial info | Limited temporal resolution | 1-D only |
| KTU weight (typical) | Low | **High** | **High** |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — M-Mode Time Scaling

**Problem:** A cardiologist uses M-Mode to record a heart trace at 50 mm/s sweep speed. The mitral valve opening (E-point) and closure (A-point) appear separated horizontally by 18 mm on the screen. What is the time interval between these events?

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta t = \frac{\text{horizontal distance}}{\text{sweep speed}} = \frac{18 \text{ mm}}{50 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.36 \text{ s} = 360 \text{ ms}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The "sweep speed" is literally a velocity — distance on the time axis per unit real time. So we just use $t = d/v$. This is how M-Mode reports are *time-stamped*.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 B-Mode Real-Time Imaging Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pulser and TGC Control] --> TX[Transmit Beamformer<br>Phased or Linear Array]
    TX -->|Focused pulse| TIS[Tissue Volume<br>Acoustic Path]
    TIS -->|Echoes return| RX[Receive Beamformer<br>Dynamic Focusing]
    RX --> TGC[Time Gain Compensation<br>Depth dependent amplification]
    TGC --> ENV[Envelope Detector<br>Rectifier and LPF]
    ENV --> LOG[Logarithmic Compression<br>60 dB to 30 dB]
    LOG --> SC[Digital Scan Converter<br>Polar to Cartesian Interpolation]
    SC --> PERS[Persistence Filter<br>Frame Averaging 2 to 4]
    PERS --> DISP[LCD Monitor<br>512 x 512 grayscale]
    DISP -->|Operator places M line| MLINE[M Mode Cursor]
    MLINE --> MMODE[M Mode Processing]
    MMODE --> MDISP[M Mode Strip Display]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The horizontal centre row (TX → TIS → RX → TGC → ENV → LOG → SC → PERS → DISP) is the **complete B-Mode pipeline**.
- The M-Mode branch (DISP → MLINE → MMODE → MDISP) is *downstream* of B-Mode — M-Mode is essentially a "side channel" that re-uses the B-Mode single-line processing at a much higher repetition rate.

---

## 4.2 B-Mode vs M-Mode Information Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPATIAL[B Mode Spatial Domain]
        BX[Beam axis y] --> BY[Lateral axis x]
    end
    subgraph TEMPORAL[M Mode Time Domain]
        MX[Depth axis y] --> MT[Time axis t scrolling]
    end
    SPATIAL -.One scan line held fixed.-> TEMPORAL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- B-Mode occupies the **(x, y)** spatial plane — lateral × depth.
- M-Mode occupies the **(t, y)** plane — time × depth.
- The arrow shows the conceptual "rotation": if you freeze B-Mode's lateral axis and stretch it into a time axis, you get M-Mode.

---

## 4.3 Scan Conversion Geometry (Polar → Cartesian)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    POL[Polar echo sample<br>range r at angle theta] --> CALC[Apply transform<br>x equals r sin theta<br>y equals r cos theta]
    CALC --> NEAR[Identify four nearest polar neighbours]
    NEAR --> BILIN[Bi linear interpolation<br>weight by 1 over d]
    BILIN --> PIX[Write pixel to DSC memory]
    PIX --> DISP[Display in real time at frame rate F_r]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each polar sample is mapped to (x, y) coordinates.
- Because polar samples don't align with the Cartesian pixel grid, the scan converter finds the **four closest polar samples** and **interpolates** their amplitudes using bi-linear (distance-weighted) averaging.
- The result is a smooth Cartesian image suitable for human viewing.

---

## 4.4 Frame-Rate / Line-Density / Depth Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Max Depth d_max] -->|inversely| FR[Frame Rate F_r]
    D -->|inversely| NL[Lines per Frame N_L]
    FR -->|inversely| NL
    D -.Constraint.-> EQ[Equation<br>F_r equals c over 2 d_max N_L]
    FR -.Constraint.-> EQ
    NL -.Constraint.-> EQ
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All three vertices (D, FR, NL) are linked by inverse relationships, unified by the **fundamental trade-off equation** at the centre.
- Increasing any one *must* decrease at least one of the others.

---

## 4.5 M-Mode Cardiac Trace Schematic (Block Diagram of Trace Formation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROBE[Phased Array Probe<br>Locked on M line] --> PRFGEN[PRF Generator<br>e.g. 5 kHz]
    PRFGEN --> BEAM[Single Line Beamformer]
    BEAM --> ENV2[Envelope Detector]
    ENV2 --> COL[One Column of Brightness<br>depth versus amplitude]
    COL --> MEM[Scan Converter Memory<br>Columns shift left to right]
    MEM --> SWEEP[Sweep at 25 to 100 mm per s]
    SWEEP --> LCD[M Mode Strip<br>mitral valve undulation visible]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The probe stays put; the **PRF generator** dictates the rate of A-line acquisition.
- Each A-line becomes a **column** in scan-converter memory.
- The columns are continuously **scrolled** at a user-selected sweep speed, producing the characteristic M-Mode strip-chart display.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode ultrasound display. Mention one clinical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx 60–80 words):**

- **A-Mode (Amplitude):** Echo amplitude plotted as vertical spikes against depth (time) on a horizontal baseline. Echoes appear as upward deflections whose height is proportional to reflection strength. **Application:** Ophthalmology for axial length measurement of the eye (A-scan biometry for IOL power calculation).
- **B-Mode (Brightness):** Echo amplitude is encoded as the **brightness (luminance)** of a 2-D pixel. Successive scan lines form a real-time **2-D grayscale anatomical image**. **Application:** Obstetric scanning for fetal biometry and anomaly detection.
- **M-Mode (Motion):** A single B-line is swept horizontally with time. The vertical axis is depth, the horizontal axis is time. **Application:** Echocardiography for measuring mitral valve E-point septal separation (EPSS) and ventricular wall thickening.

> **[1 mark for each mode's correct description + 0.5 mark per correct application × 2 = total 3]**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is M-Mode preferred over B-Mode for measuring rapid cardiac events such as mitral valve opening, even though B-Mode is a 2-D image?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx 60–80 words):**

The reason is **temporal resolution**. B-Mode has to fire $N_L$ pulses (typically 64–256) per frame to build the 2-D image, so its effective frame rate is limited to **15–60 fps**. A cardiac event like mitral valve opening lasts only 30–60 ms, so a 30 fps B-Mode samples it just once or twice — too coarse.

M-Mode fires only one line per pulse, so its A-line rate is limited only by depth, giving **1000–5000 samples/s** — orders of magnitude higher. This lets the cardiologist capture the entire valve motion curve with sub-millisecond precision, enabling accurate timing measurements. **[3 marks: 1 for stating temporal resolution, 1 for the numerical comparison, 1 for the clinical implication.]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B (14 marks) is split as **Part (a) = 7 marks, Part (b) = 7 marks**. Either sub-part may carry an internal sub-choice. The two alternatives below are **independent** — students answer either Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**A(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **B-Mode ultrasound imaging system**. Discuss the role of:
- (i) Transmit beamformer
- (ii) Time Gain Compensation (TGC)
- (iii) Envelope detector
- (iv) Digital scan converter

**A(b) [7 marks]** A B-Mode scanner is used to image soft tissue to a maximum depth of **20 cm** at a frame rate of **25 fps**. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum pulse repetition frequency (PRF)
- (ii) Number of scan lines per frame
- (iii) The maximum depth if the frame rate is increased to 50 fps while keeping the lines per frame unchanged.

Take $c = 1540$ m/s for soft tissue.

---

#### Model Solution — A(a) [7 marks]

**Block diagram:** (Re-draw or refer to Section 4.1)

```
Pulser → Transmit Beamformer → Tissue → Receive Beamformer
       → TGC → Envelope Detector → Log Compression
       → Digital Scan Converter → Persistence → Display
```

**Explanation of each block:**

1. **Transmit Beamformer [1.5 marks]:** Generates properly delayed, focused, short (~2 cycle) high-voltage pulses to each element of the array transducer. The pattern of delays determines the **azimuth and depth of focus** of the transmit beam. Sequential firing of different delay patterns electronically steers the beam across the image plane.

2. **Time Gain Compensation (TGC) [1.5 marks]:** Because ultrasound attenuates in tissue at ≈ 0.5 dB/cm/MHz, deeper echoes are weaker. TGC applies **depth-dependent amplification** that increases with range, restoring equal-amplitude display of equal-reflectivity tissues at different depths. Operator-adjustable "TGC sliders" on the console allow fine-tuning.

3. **Envelope Detector [1.5 marks]:** The RF echo is a high-frequency (MHz) oscillating signal. The monitor cannot display this directly. The envelope detector is a **rectifier + low-pass filter** that extracts the slowly varying *envelope* (amplitude vs. time), which represents the actual echo strength at each depth.

4. **Digital Scan Converter [2.5 marks]:** Converts the polar-coordinate echo data (range, angle) into a **Cartesian pixel grid** suitable for the rectangular LCD. Uses the transform $x = r\sin\theta$, $y = r\cos\theta$, and **bi-linear interpolation** between neighbouring polar samples. Stores the result in frame memory at the chosen frame rate. This is what makes the image look like a clean 2-D picture rather than a raw sector.

---

#### Model Solution — A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $d_{max} = 20$ cm $= 0.20$ m, $F_r = 25$ fps, $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Part (i) — Maximum PRF [2 marks]:**

$$
\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{c}{2 d_{max}} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 0.20} = \frac{1540}{0.40} = 3850 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Part (ii) — Lines per frame [3 marks]:**

$$
N_L = \frac{\text{PRF}_{max}}{F_r} = \frac{3850}{25} = 154 \text{ lines/frame}
$$

**Part (iii) — New max depth at 50 fps with $N_L$ unchanged [2 marks]:**

Since $N_L$ is unchanged, using the trade-off equation:

$$
F_r = \frac{c}{2 d_{max} N_L} \Rightarrow d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 F_r N_L} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 50 \times 154}
$$

Computing denominator: $2 \times 50 \times 154 = 15400$

$$
d_{max} = \frac{1540}{15400} = 0.10 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ cm}
$$

> *Verification:* PRF at 10 cm is $1540/(0.20) = 7700$ Hz; lines per frame = $7700/50 = 154$ ✓.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**B(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle of M-Mode imaging** with a neat diagram. How is M-Mode display generated from the B-Mode signal? Discuss its **clinical applications in echocardiography** with at least two specific measurements.

**B(b) [7 marks]** A 5 MHz ultrasound transducer has a fractional bandwidth of 60% in soft tissue. Calculate:
- (i) Axial resolution
- (ii) Wavelength in tissue
- (iii) The maximum axial resolution attainable if the same transducer is operated at its second harmonic (10 MHz) with 80% bandwidth.

Take $c = 1540$ m/s.

---

#### Model Solution — B(a) [7 marks]

**Principle of M-Mode [3 marks]:**

In M-Mode, the ultrasound beam is held stationary along a single user-selected axis (the *M-line*). The system repeatedly fires pulses at the PRF along this line, and each returning A-line is envelope-detected and log-compressed, producing a 1-D **depth-vs-brightness** column. Successive columns are written into scan-converter memory and scrolled horizontally at a user-selected sweep speed (25, 50, or 100 mm/s).

The result is a strip-chart with:
- **Vertical axis** = depth into tissue
- **Horizontal axis** = time
- **Brightness** = echo strength

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.5)

**Generation from B-Mode signal [2 marks]:**

The B-Mode signal (after envelope detection, TGC, log compression) is routed to a *separate M-Mode processing channel*. Instead of scan-converting onto a 2-D Cartesian grid, each A-line is written as one **column** of the M-Mode memory. The M-line cursor is placed by the operator on the B-image. The columns are continuously shifted, producing the scrolling display. M-Mode re-uses the B-Mode single-line processing — only the display path differs.

**Clinical applications in echocardiography [2 marks]:**

1. **Mitral valve E-point septal separation (EPSS):** A specific M-mode measurement of the distance between the anterior mitral valve leaflet's maximum opening point (E-point) and the interventricular septum. Normal < 7 mm; > 7 mm suggests LV dysfunction.

2. **LV wall thickening fraction:** M-mode across the LV posterior wall shows end-diastolic and end-systolic thickness. Thickening fraction = (PWs − PWd) / PWd. Normal ≈ 30–40%.

3. *(Optional 3rd)* **Aortic root motion** for timing of valve events, **pericardial effusion** (echo-free space), **tamponade** (RV diastolic collapse visible on M-mode before B-mode).

---

#### Model Solution — B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $f = 5$ MHz, fractional bandwidth = 60%, $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Part (i) — Axial resolution [3 marks]:**

Bandwidth $\Delta f = 0.60 \times 5 = 3$ MHz.

$$
\delta_z = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 3 \times 10^6} = \frac{1540}{6 \times 10^6} \approx 2.57 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.257 \text{ mm}
$$

**Part (ii) — Wavelength in tissue [2 marks]:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.308 \text{ mm}
$$

**Part (iii) — Axial resolution at 2nd harmonic (10 MHz, 80% bandwidth) [2 marks]:**

$\Delta f = 0.80 \times 10 = 8$ MHz.

$$
\delta_z = \frac{1540}{2 \times 8 \times 10^6} = \frac{1540}{16 \times 10^6} = 9.625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 0.096 \text{ mm}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Higher frequency → shorter wavelength and wider bandwidth → finer axial resolution. The harmonic image is ~2.7× sharper but at the cost of lower penetration (depth).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points for B-Mode / M-Mode Questions (KTU ESE):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $d_{max} = c/(2 \cdot \text{PRF})$** — this alone costs 1–2 marks. The "2" comes from round-trip travel. Even top students write $c/\text{PRF}$ in the heat of the exam.
> 
> 2. **Using c = 1500 m/s or 340 m/s** — Board examiners deduct marks if you do not explicitly state **"c = 1540 m/s for soft tissue"** in your solution. Always write the assumed value.
> 
> 3. **Conflating B-Mode and M-Mode displays** — B-Mode is 2-D (x × y spatial), M-Mode is 1-D (depth × time). Writing "M-Mode shows a 2-D image" or "B-Mode is 1-D" is a **3-mark killer**.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the role of the scan converter** — When asked to explain B-Mode, students often describe up to envelope detection and stop. The *scan converter* is a distinct, mandatory block worth at least 1–2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — Always write units (Hz, fps, m, mm) explicitly. A correct number with no unit may lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 
> 6. **M-Mode clinical application oversimplification** — Just saying "M-Mode is for cardiac imaging" is too vague. Always name **at least one specific measurement** (EPSS, LV wall thickening, aortic root diameter).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **B-Mode** = 2-D grayscale real-time anatomical imaging. Echo amplitude → pixel brightness; beam position → pixel location.
- **M-Mode** = 1-D time-strip display. Single beam axis; depth vs. time scrolling.
- **Both modes** use the same physical principle: pulse-echo ranging with **c = 1540 m/s** in soft tissue.
- **Fundamental B-Mode trade-off equation:** $F_r = c / (2 d_{max} N_L)$ — increase any one, decrease another.
- **Maximum unambiguous depth:** $d_{max} = c / (2 \cdot \text{PRF})$ — the factor **2 is non-negotiable**.
- **Axial resolution:** $\delta_z = c / (2 \Delta f)$ — finer for higher bandwidth probes.
- **Scan conversion** (polar → Cartesian) is the digital step that turns raw sector echoes into a clean rectangular image.
- **B-Mode frame rate:** 15–60 fps. **M-Mode sample rate:** 1000–5000 lines/s. The huge M-Mode temporal resolution is its key clinical advantage for cardiac motion.
- **TGC** compensates for depth-dependent attenuation; **envelope detection** rectifies and smooths the RF; **log compression** maps the wide dynamic range to a viewable 8-bit grayscale.
- **KTU clinical favourites for M-Mode:** EPSS, LV wall thickening, aortic root diameter, pericardial effusion, fetal heart rate.
- **Phased array** = small footprint → cardiac / intercostal; **linear array** = rectangular → vascular/MSK; **curvilinear** = wedge → abdominal/OB.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — B-Mode and M-Mode Scanning** | *Next topic in Module 1: Doppler Ultrasound Principles*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transducer Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transducer Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Transducer

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of physical energy (such as mechanical, thermal, electrical, acoustic, or optical energy) into another form of energy. In the context of **Medical Imaging Techniques**, a transducer is the critical interface element that converts electrical signals into acoustic (ultrasound) waves and, conversely, converts returning acoustic echoes back into electrical signals for image reconstruction.

In medical imaging, the term *transducer* most commonly refers to the **ultrasound (US) probe** — a hand-held device placed on the patient's skin that both **transmits** high-frequency sound waves into tissue and **receives** the reflected echoes from internal anatomical structures.

### 1.2 Classification of Transducers in Medical Imaging

| Type | Energy Conversion | Example in Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric Transducer** | Electrical ⇌ Acoustic | Ultrasound probe, IVUS catheter |
| **Photodetector / Scintillator** | Optical ⇌ Electrical | X-ray detector, Gamma camera |
| **RF Coil** | Electromagnetic ⇌ Electrical | MRI receiver/transmitter coil |
| **Solid-State Detector** | Ionizing Radiation ⇌ Electrical | Digital mammography, CT detector array |
| **Fiber-Optic Sensor** | Light ⇌ Light (modulated) | Endoscopic imaging, optical biopsy |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Microphone and Speaker"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**
> Think of an ultrasound transducer as a **combination of a speaker and a microphone** packed into a single crystal:
> - **Speaker mode (Transmission):** Electrical pulses from the scanner vibrate the crystal → crystal pushes surrounding air/tissue and produces an **ultrasound wave** that travels into the body.
> - **Microphone mode (Reception):** Returning echoes vibrate the crystal → the crystal generates a tiny **electrical voltage** that the scanner amplifies and processes into an image.
>
> The same "speaker-microphone crystal" does both jobs by exploiting the reversible **piezoelectric effect**.

### 1.4 Why the Piezoelectric Effect is Central

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Medical ultrasound transducers rely almost exclusively on the **piezoelectric effect** — discovered by **Pierre and Jacques Curie in 1880**. Certain crystalline materials (e.g., quartz, lead zirconate titanate — PZT, polyvinylidene fluoride — PVDF) develop an electrical charge on their surfaces when mechanically stressed, and conversely, they physically deform when an electric field is applied.

**Standard Metrics at a Glance:**
- Typical diagnostic ultrasound frequency: **2 MHz – 15 MHz**
- Speed of sound in soft tissue: **c ≈ 1540 m/s**
- Wavelength range in tissue: **λ ≈ 0.1 mm – 0.77 mm**
- Piezoelectric material of choice: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** due to high electromechanical coupling coefficient *k* ≈ **0.5 – 0.6**.

---

### 1.5 Block Diagram — Role of Transducer in Imaging Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pulser / Tx Beamformer] --> B[Transducer Probe]
    B --> C[Patient Tissue]
    C --> D[Reflected Echoes]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Receiver / Rx Beamformer]
    E --> F[Display / Image]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pulser excites the transducer, which sends acoustic pulses into the body. Echoes return to the *same* transducer, which converts them into electrical signals processed by the receiver and displayed as an image.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Piezoelectric Effect — Operational Logic

The piezoelectric effect operates in **two reversible modes**, which together form the foundation of ultrasound imaging.

#### 2.1.1 Direct Piezoelectric Effect (Used in Reception)

> **Operational Logic:**
> 1. A returning acoustic echo exerts a **mechanical force (stress, *T*)** on the piezoelectric crystal.
> 2. The internal **dipoles** of the crystal are disturbed, producing a measurable **charge (D)** on the electrodes.
> 3. The resulting **voltage (V)** is amplified and processed to form the image.
> 4. The relationship is **linear** in the small-signal regime.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$
D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^{T} \cdot E
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Electric displacement (C/m²)
- $d$ = Piezoelectric charge constant (C/N or m/V)
- $T$ = Mechanical stress (N/m²)
- $\varepsilon^{T}$ = Permittivity at constant stress (F/m)
- $E$ = Electric field (V/m)

#### 2.1.2 Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect (Used in Transmission)

> **Operational Logic:**
> 1. The scanner applies a **pulsed high-voltage (E)** across electrodes of the crystal.
> 2. The internal dipoles align, and the crystal **mechanically strains (S)**.
> 3. This mechanical strain **launches an acoustic wave** into the tissue via the matching layer.
> 4. When the pulse ends, the crystal returns to its original shape.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$
S = s^{E} \cdot T + d \cdot E
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Mechanical strain (dimensionless, m/m)
- $s^{E}$ = Elastic compliance at constant electric field (m²/N)
- $d$ = Same piezoelectric constant as above (reciprocity of the effect)

#### 2.1.3 The Piezoelectric Coupling Coefficient *k*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **electromechanical coupling coefficient *k*** is the single most important figure-of-merit for a transducer material. It quantifies **how efficiently electrical energy is converted into mechanical energy (and vice versa).**

$$
k^{2} = \frac{\text{Mechanical energy stored}}{\text{Electrical energy applied}} = \frac{\text{Electrical energy stored}}{\text{Mechanical energy applied}}
$$

Typical values:
- Quartz: *k* ≈ 0.10
- PZT (PZT-5H): *k* ≈ **0.50 – 0.60**
- PVDF polymer: *k* ≈ 0.20

### 2.2 Construction of an Ultrasound Transducer

A modern diagnostic ultrasound probe is a **multi-layer precision-engineered assembly**:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Backing Layer / Damping Material] --> L2[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    L2 --> L3[Matching Layer 1]
    L3 --> L4[Matching Layer 2]
    L4 --> L5[Lens / Patient Contact Surface]
```

| Layer | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Backing / Damper** | Tungsten-epoxy composite, rubber | Absorbs backward radiation → shortens pulse, improves axial resolution |
| **Piezoelectric Element** | PZT ceramic (most common), PVDF, composite | The active element — converts E ⇌ acoustic |
| **Matching Layer(s)** | Epoxy loaded with metal powder (Z ≈ √(Z_piezo · Z_tissue)) | Reduces acoustic impedance mismatch → maximizes energy transmission into tissue |
| **Acoustic Lens** | RTV silicone, plastic | Focuses the beam in the elevation (out-of-plane) direction |

### 2.3 Resonant Frequency of the Crystal

The piezoelectric crystal resonates when its **thickness equals half a wavelength** of the acoustic wave inside the crystal material. This is the **thickness-mode resonance**.

**Derivation (from wave equation with fixed-free or free-free boundary):**

A crystal of thickness $t$ supports a standing wave when:

$$
t = n \cdot \frac{\lambda_c}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

For the **fundamental mode (n = 1)**:

$$
t = \frac{\lambda_c}{2} = \frac{v_c}{2 f_r}
$$

Solving for the **resonant frequency $f_r$:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_r = \frac{v_c}{2 t}\,}
$$

Where:
- $v_c$ = velocity of sound *inside the piezoelectric crystal* (m/s)
- $t$ = thickness of the crystal (m)
- $f_r$ = fundamental resonant frequency (Hz)

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> To make a **5 MHz probe**, the PZT crystal must be milled to a thickness of $t = v_c / (2 f_r)$. For PZT ($v_c \approx 4000$ m/s), this gives $t \approx 0.4$ mm — an extremely thin, fragile wafer that requires precision manufacturing.

### 2.4 Quality Factor (Q) and Bandwidth

The **Quality Factor Q** measures the sharpness of the resonance peak:

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \frac{f_r}{f_2 - f_1}\,}
$$

Where $f_1$ and $f_2$ are the **−3 dB (half-power) frequencies** on either side of $f_r$.

| Probe Type | Q-factor | Bandwidth | Pulse Length | Axial Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Wave (CW)** | High (~50–100) | Narrow | Long | Poor |
| **Pulsed Diagnostic (Short Pulse)** | Low (~2–5) | Wide (~60–80% of $f_r$) | Short (~1–2 cycles) | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Q → narrow bandwidth → poor axial resolution (long spatial pulse length).**
> **Low Q → wide bandwidth → excellent axial resolution.**
> Medical imaging requires **broadband (low-Q)** transducers, hence the **damping/backing layer** is essential.

### 2.5 Acoustic Impedance and Transmission Coefficient

When ultrasound crosses a boundary, the fraction of intensity transmitted depends on the **acoustic impedance mismatch**:

$$
Z = \rho \cdot c
$$

Where $Z$ = acoustic impedance (Rayl = kg/m²·s), $\rho$ = density, $c$ = speed of sound.

| Material | Density (kg/m³) | c (m/s) | Z (Mrayl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air** | 1.2 | 330 | 0.0004 |
| **Fat** | 920 | 1450 | 1.33 |
| **Water / Soft Tissue** | 1000 | 1540 | 1.54 |
| **PZT (PZT-5H)** | 7500 | 4000 | 30.0 |
| **Bone** | 1900 | 4080 | 7.75 |
| **Steel** | 7800 | 5800 | 45.2 |

**Intensity Transmission Coefficient (normal incidence):**

$$
\boxed{\,T_I = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{\left(Z_1 + Z_2\right)^{2}}\,}
$$

**Intensity Reflection Coefficient:**

$$
\boxed{\,R_I = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^{2}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If no matching layer is used, **PZT → soft tissue** would reflect back **≈ 88%** of the acoustic energy. With a properly designed **quarter-wavelength matching layer** of impedance $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{tissue}}}$, reflection drops to **< 1%** and transmission approaches 100%.

### 2.6 Beam Characteristics — Near Field and Far Field

For a **circular piston transducer of diameter $D$** vibrating at frequency $f$ in a medium of sound speed $c$:

**Wavelength in tissue:**

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda = \frac{c}{f}\,}
$$

**Fresnel (Near) Field Length — distance from transducer to the last axial maximum:**

$$
\boxed{\,N = \frac{D^{2}}{4 \lambda} = \frac{D^{2} f}{4 c}\,}
$$

**Fraunhofer (Far) Field Distance:**

$$
\boxed{\,F = \frac{D^{2}}{4 \lambda} = N\,}
$$

(For a circular piston, the **last axial maximum** and the **start of the far field** coincide numerically.)

**Half-angle of beam divergence in the far field:**

$$
\boxed{\,\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \,\lambda}{D}\,}
$$

(The factor 1.22 comes from the first zero of the Bessel function $J_1(x)$.)

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Utility in Engineering**
> In **intracardiac echocardiography (ICE)** and **endoscopic ultrasound**, where the probe aperture is only a few millimeters, the **near-field length becomes very short** and the beam diverges rapidly. This is why **phased-array and convex-array** designs dominate in such applications — electronic focusing is used to maintain resolution at depth.

### 2.7 Complete KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant frequency (thickness mode) | $f_r = v_c / (2 t)$ | Hz |
| 2 | Wavelength in tissue | $\lambda = c / f$ | m |
| 3 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Rayl (kg/m²·s) |
| 4 | Reflection coefficient (intensity) | $R_I = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Transmission coefficient (intensity) | $T_I = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^{2}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Quality factor | $Q = f_r / \Delta f$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Near-field length | $N = D^{2} / (4 \lambda)$ | m |
| 8 | Far-field divergence half-angle | $\sin \theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$ | rad |
| 9 | Direct piezoelectric eqn. | $D = d T + \varepsilon^{T} E$ | C/m² |
| 10 | Converse piezoelectric eqn. | $S = s^{E} T + d E$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Coupling coefficient | $k^{2} = W_{\text{mech}} / W_{\text{elec}}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Axial resolution | $\Delta z = c \cdot \tau / 2$ (τ = pulse duration) | m |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resonant Frequency of Thickness-Mode Piezoelectric Crystal

**Goal:** Show that the fundamental thickness-mode resonant frequency of a piezoelectric disk is $f_r = v_c / (2 t)$.

**Setup:** Consider a thin piezoelectric plate of thickness $t$ with both faces **electrically free** (electroded but unconstrained mechanically). A longitudinal acoustic wave propagates along the thickness (x-axis) with velocity $v_c$ inside the crystal.

**Step 1 — One-Dimensional Wave Equation:**

The displacement $\xi(x,t)$ of a particle inside the crystal satisfies:

$$
\frac{\partial^{2} \xi}{\partial t^{2}} = v_c^{2} \, \frac{\partial^{2} \xi}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — General Travelling-Wave Solution:**

The general solution is a superposition of forward and backward waves:

$$
\xi(x, t) = A \cos\!\left(\omega t - kx\right) + B \cos\!\left(\omega t + kx\right)
$$

where $k = \omega / v_c$ is the wave number.

**Step 3 — Apply Stress-Free Boundary Conditions:**

The stress at each face of the crystal must be zero (mechanically free surface). The stress is $T = -Y \cdot \partial \xi / \partial x$ (where $Y$ is Young's modulus), so:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \left.\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x}\right|_{x=t} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Spatial Derivative:**

$$
\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = k A \sin\!\left(\omega t - kx\right) - k B \sin\!\left(\omega t + kx\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Condition at x = 0:**

$$
k A \sin(\omega t) - k B \sin(\omega t) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = B
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Apply Boundary Condition at x = t:**

Using $A = B$:

$$
\frac{\partial \xi}{\partial x} = k A \big[\sin(\omega t - kx) - \sin(\omega t + kx)\big] = -2 k A \cos(\omega t)\sin(kx)
$$

Setting this to zero at $x = t$:

$$
\sin(kt) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; kt = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for Frequency:**

$$
k = \frac{\omega}{v_c} = \frac{2 \pi f}{v_c} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{2 \pi f}{v_c} \cdot t = n \pi
$$

$$
\Rightarrow f_n = \frac{n \cdot v_c}{2 t}
$$

**Step 8 — Fundamental Mode (n = 1):**

$$
\boxed{\,f_r = \frac{v_c}{2 t}\,}
$$

**Verification of Units:**

$$
[v_c] = \text{m/s}, \quad [t] = \text{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; [f_r] = \frac{\text{m/s}}{\text{m}} = \text{s}^{-1} = \text{Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Near-Field (Fresnel) Length

**Goal:** Derive the expression for the axial distance to the **last on-axis pressure maximum** of a circular piston transducer.

**Setup:** A circular piston of radius $a$ (so $D = 2a$) vibrates with uniform velocity in a rigid infinite baffle. The on-axis pressure field at distance $z$ from the piston is obtained by integrating the Rayleigh–Sommerfeld integral over the piston face.

**Step 1 — On-Axis Pressure Integral:**

For a point on the axis at distance $z$:

$$
p(z) = \frac{j \omega \rho_0 U_0}{2 \pi} \int_{0}^{a} \frac{e^{-j k R}}{R} \, 2 \pi r \, dr
$$

where $R = \sqrt{r^{2} + z^{2}}$ is the distance from a ring element to the field point, and $U_0$ is the piston velocity amplitude.

**Step 2 — Simplify the Integral:**

$$
p(z) = j \omega \rho_0 U_0 \int_{0}^{a} \frac{e^{-j k \sqrt{r^{2}+z^{2}}}}{\sqrt{r^{2}+z^{2}}} \, r \, dr
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R = \sqrt{r^{2}+z^{2}}$, so $r \, dr = R \, dR$:**

When $r = 0$, $R = z$; when $r = a$, $R = \sqrt{a^{2} + z^{2}}$.

$$
p(z) = j \omega \rho_0 U_0 \int_{z}^{\sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}}} e^{-j k R} \, dR
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the Integration:**

$$
p(z) = j \omega \rho_0 U_0 \left[\frac{e^{-j k R}}{-j k}\right]_{z}^{\sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}}} = \rho_0 c \, U_0 \left[e^{-j k z} - e^{-j k \sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}}}\right]
$$

where $c = \omega / k$ is the speed of sound in the medium.

**Step 5 — Phase Difference Between the Two Rays:**

The two terms represent the wave from the centre of the piston and the wave from the rim. The phase difference is:

$$
\Delta \phi = k \left(\sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}} - z\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Condition for Last Axial Maximum:**

At the **last axial maximum**, the phase difference equals $\pi$ (the two contributions are **in phase again** for the last time before destructive interference dominates):

$$
k \left(\sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}} - z\right) = \pi
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for z:**

$$
\sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}} - z = \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

$$
\sqrt{a^{2}+z^{2}} = z + \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

Square both sides:

$$
a^{2} + z^{2} = z^{2} + \lambda z + \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4}
$$

$$
a^{2} = \lambda z + \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply the Far-Field Approximation (z ≫ λ):**

In the regime $z \gg \lambda$ (always true for practical imaging), $\lambda^{2}/4 \ll a^{2}$, so:

$$
z \approx \frac{a^{2}}{\lambda} = \frac{(D/2)^{2}}{\lambda} = \frac{D^{2}}{4\lambda}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,N = \frac{D^{2}}{4 \lambda} = \frac{D^{2} f}{4 c}\,}
$$

**Verification of Units:**

$$
[D^{2} f] = \text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}, \quad [c] = \text{m/s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; [N] = \frac{\text{m}^{2}/\text{s}}{\text{m}/\text{s}} = \text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quarter-Wave Matching Layer Impedance

**Goal:** Show that the **optimal acoustic impedance** of a single quarter-wavelength matching layer is the **geometric mean** of the source and load impedances.

**Setup:** A plane acoustic wave travels from medium 1 (piezo crystal, $Z_1$) into medium 2 (matching layer, $Z_2$) of thickness $\lambda_2/4$ and then into medium 3 (tissue, $Z_3$). We need the input impedance seen looking into the matching layer.

**Step 1 — Input Impedance of a Transmission Line of Length $L = \lambda_2/4$:**

For a lossless layer of characteristic impedance $Z_2$ and length $L$ terminated in $Z_3$:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = Z_2 \cdot \frac{Z_3 + j Z_2 \tan(k_2 L)}{Z_2 + j Z_3 \tan(k_2 L)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $L = \lambda_2/4$:**

Since $k_2 L = (2 \pi / \lambda_2)(\lambda_2/4) = \pi/2$, we have $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Standard Limit:**

As $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$, divide numerator and denominator by $\tan(k_2 L)$:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = Z_2 \cdot \frac{j Z_2 + Z_3 \cot(k_2 L)}{j Z_3 + Z_2 \cot(k_2 L)} \;\bigg|_{\;k_2 L = \pi/2}
$$

Since $\cot(\pi/2) = 0$:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = Z_2 \cdot \frac{j Z_2}{j Z_3} = \frac{Z_2^{2}}{Z_3}
$$

**Step 4 — Impedance Matching Condition:**

For **no reflection at the layer 1 → layer 2 interface**, we need $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_1$:

$$
Z_1 = \frac{Z_2^{2}}{Z_3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Z_2^{2} = Z_1 Z_3
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,Z_{\text{match}} = \sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{tissue}}}\,}
$$

**Numerical Verification:**

For PZT ($Z_1 = 30$ Mrayl) into soft tissue ($Z_3 = 1.54$ Mrayl):

$$
Z_2 = \sqrt{30 \times 1.54} = \sqrt{46.2} \approx 6.8 \text{ Mrayl}
$$

This is exactly the impedance range achievable with metal-loaded epoxies — confirming the design principle.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (Typical KTU-style):** A diagnostic ultrasound probe uses a PZT-5H crystal with $v_c = 4000$ m/s, diameter $D = 12$ mm, and operates at $f = 5$ MHz in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³).

**Find:**
(a) The required crystal thickness
(b) The wavelength in tissue
(c) The near-field length
(d) The half-angle of beam divergence
(e) The intensity reflection coefficient at the probe–tissue interface (without matching layer)
(f) The required matching-layer impedance

**Solution:**

**(a) Crystal thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{v_c}{2 f_r} = \frac{4000}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{6}} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**(b) Wavelength in tissue:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.308 \text{ mm}
$$

**(c) Near-field length:**

$$
N = \frac{D^{2}}{4 \lambda} = \frac{(0.012)^{2}}{4 \times 3.08 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{1.232 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.117 \text{ m} = 11.7 \text{ cm}
$$

**(d) Half-angle of divergence:**

$$
\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 3.08 \times 10^{-4}}{0.012} = 0.0313
$$

$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.0313) \approx 1.79^{\circ}
$$

**(e) Reflection coefficient (no matching layer):**

$Z_{\text{PZT}} = \rho_{\text{PZT}} \cdot v_c = 7500 \times 4000 = 30 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl
$Z_{\text{tissue}} = 1000 \times 1540 = 1.54 \times 10^{6}$ Rayl

$$
R_I = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{30 - 1.54}{30 + 1.54}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{28.46}{31.54}\right)^{2} = (0.902)^{2} \approx 0.814
$$

**Result:** **81.4% of intensity is reflected** — confirming the need for a matching layer.

**(f) Matching layer impedance:**

$$
Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{tissue}}} = \sqrt{30 \times 1.54} \times 10^{6} = 6.8 \text{ Mrayl}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Piezoelectric Ultrasound Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pulse["Transmission Path (TX)"]
        P1[Pulser: 100-300 V Spike] --> P2[Piezoelectric Crystal PZT]
        P2 --> P3[Matching Layer Z = sqrt Zpzt Ztissue]
        P3 --> P4[Acoustic Lens]
        P4 --> P5[Patient Tissue Medium]
    end
    subgraph Echo["Reception Path (RX)"]
        P5 --> P6[Reflected Echoes from Tissue Interfaces]
        P6 --> P4
        P4 --> P3
        P3 --> P2
        P2 --> P7[Tiny Voltage in microvolts]
        P7 --> P8[Low-Noise Preamplifier]
        P8 --> P9[Beamformer and Image Processor]
    end
```

**Functional Reading of the Block Diagram:**

| Block | Domain | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Pulser | Electrical | Delivers a brief high-voltage pulse (typically 100–300 V, 0.1–1 µs) |
| PZT Crystal | Mech-Electrical | Converts pulse voltage into mechanical vibration via converse piezoelectric effect |
| Matching Layer | Acoustic | Maximizes energy transfer from crystal (Z ≈ 30 Mrayl) into tissue (Z ≈ 1.5 Mrayl) |
| Acoustic Lens | Acoustic | Focuses the beam in the elevation (out-of-plane) direction |
| Patient Tissue | Acoustic | Site of scattering, reflection, attenuation |
| Returning Echo | Mechanical | Compresses/expands the crystal |
| PZT Crystal (RX) | Mech-Electrical | Generates a voltage via the direct piezoelectric effect |
| Preamplifier | Electrical | Boosts microvolt-level echo signals with low noise figure |
| Beamformer | Digital/Analog | Applies time delays to align echoes → forms scanline |

---

### 4.2 Detailed Transducer Probe — Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ProbeHousing["Flexible Probe Housing"]
        A1[Cable and Connector] --> A2[Backing Material Tungsten Epoxy]
        A2 --> A3[PZT Crystal Disc cut to lambda over 2]
        A3 --> A4[Electrode Silver Ink on Crystal Faces]
        A4 --> A5[Matching Layer 1 Z = sqrt Zpzt Ztissue]
        A5 --> A6[Matching Layer 2 Optional for ultra wideband]
        A6 --> A7[Acoustic Lens RTV Silicone]
    end
```

**Design Rationale:**

- The **backing** (high acoustic impedance, lossy) absorbs backward-travelling waves from the crystal — this **shortens the pulse length**, which directly improves **axial resolution** $\Delta z = c \tau / 2$.
- The **matching layer** is sized to a thickness of $\lambda_{\text{match}} / 4$ inside its own material, so that the reflected waves from the front and back surfaces of the matching layer **cancel out by destructive interference** (Derivation 3 above).
- The **acoustic lens** is a concave RTV silicone element providing a **fixed elevation focus** typically at 4–7 cm depth.

---

### 4.3 Resonance and Bandwidth — Frequency Response Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FreqAxis["Frequency Axis f (Hz)"]
        F1[f1 = 4.6 MHz]
        F2[fr = 5.0 MHz]
        F3[f2 = 5.4 MHz]
    end
    F1 --> F2 --> F3
    F2 -.->|delta f = 0.8 MHz| F3
    F1 -.->|Q = fr / delta f = 6.25| F2
```

**Interpretation of the Schematic:**

The resonance curve is centered at $f_r = 5$ MHz with −3 dB points at $f_1$ and $f_2$. The fractional bandwidth is:

$$
\text{BW} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_r} = \frac{0.8}{5.0} = 16\% \quad \text{(or ±8% around } f_r\text{)}
$$

A high-Q transducer (narrow Δf) has long ringing pulses — undesirable for pulse-echo imaging. Modern probes aim for **fractional bandwidths of 60–80%** by using **composite piezo materials** and **multi-layer matching**.

---

### 4.4 Beam Geometry — Near Field vs Far Field

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Piston Transducer Diameter D] --> N1[Last Axial Max at distance N]
    N1 --> N2[Far Field Region Beam Diverges]
    N2 --> N3[Beam Diverges as sin theta = 1.22 lambda over D]
    subgraph NearField["Fresnel Zone Near Field"]
        P
        N1
    end
    subgraph FarField["Fraunhofer Zone Far Field"]
        N2
        N3
    end
```

**Reading the Beam Diagram:**

- Within the **near field** (distance $< N$), the beam is approximately **cylindrical** with minimal lateral spread — best lateral resolution occurs here.
- At distance $> N$ in the **far field**, the beam **diverges** with half-angle $\theta$. A larger $D$ or higher $f$ (smaller $\lambda$) yields a tighter, more directional beam.

---

### 4.5 Piezoelectric Mode — Signal Flow in TX and RX

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TXMode["Transmission (Converse Effect)"]
        V1[High Voltage Pulse from Pulser] --> E1[Electric Field E across PZT]
        E1 --> S1[Mechanical Strain S in Crystal]
        S1 --> W1[Acoustic Wavefront Propagates into Tissue]
    end
    subgraph RXMode["Reception (Direct Effect)"]
        W2[Echo Pressure Wave Reaches Crystal] --> T1[Mechanical Stress T on PZT]
        T1 --> D1[Charge D Develops on Electrodes]
        D1 --> V2[Output Voltage V = microvolts to millivolts]
    end
    W1 -.->|propagates, reflects| W2
```

**Crucial Symmetry:** The *same* piezoelectric coefficient $d$ governs both directions — this is the principle of **transducer reciprocity** in linear acoustics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a transducer. List any **two** piezoelectric materials used in medical ultrasound transducers.

**Model Answer:**

A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of energy into another. In medical imaging, an **ultrasound transducer** converts electrical energy into high-frequency acoustic (sound) energy during transmission, and converts returning acoustic echoes back into electrical signals during reception — a reversible process exploited via the **piezoelectric effect**.

**Two piezoelectric materials used in medical ultrasound transducers:**

1. **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate, e.g., PZT-5H)** — the most widely used ceramic, with high electromechanical coupling coefficient $k \approx 0.5 - 0.6$ and high dielectric constant. Used in most diagnostic probes.

2. **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** — a flexible polymer film. Has lower $k$ (~0.20) but excellent broadband response; used in **high-frequency (>20 MHz) intravascular ultrasound (IVUS)** and **hockey-stick** probes where flexibility is required.

*(Other valid answers: Lithium Niobate LiNbO₃, Quartz, PZT-polymer composites, Relaxor ferroelectrics like PMN-PT.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Distinguish between the **direct** and **converse** piezoelectric effects.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct Piezoelectric Effect | Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of energy conversion** | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical |
| **Triggering input** | Mechanical stress (force) on the crystal | Applied electric field (voltage) across the crystal |
| **Output** | Electric charge / voltage on electrodes | Mechanical strain / deformation of the crystal |
| **Used in** | **Reception** of ultrasound echoes | **Transmission** of ultrasound pulses |
| **Governing equation** | $D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^{T} E$ | $S = s^{E} \cdot T + d \cdot E$ |
| **Discovery** | Pierre & Jacques Curie, 1880 | Gabriel Lippmann, 1881 (predicted theoretically) |

> **Key Point:** Both effects are governed by the **same piezoelectric coefficient $d$**, demonstrating the **reciprocity** of the electromechanical coupling.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 1):**
> Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a typical split of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — you must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Each sub-part must be answered in structured form with diagrams.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a piezoelectric ultrasound transducer**. Discuss the role of the **backing layer** and **matching layer(s)** in determining transducer performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **near-field (Fresnel) length** of a circular piston transducer of diameter $D$ operating at frequency $f$ in a medium of sound velocity $c$. A 3.5 MHz probe of diameter 20 mm is used in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). Calculate the **near-field length** and the **half-angle of beam divergence** in the far field. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Working Principle:** A piezoelectric ultrasound transducer operates on the **reversible piezoelectric effect**. The same crystal is used for both transmission (converse effect) and reception (direct effect).

**Block Diagram / Cross-Section (drawn from Section 4.2 above):**

**Layer-by-Layer Description:**

1. **Piezoelectric Crystal (PZT) — Active Element:**
   - Cut to thickness $t = v_c / (2 f_r)$ for the desired operating frequency.
   - Electroded on both faces with thin silver ink.
   - Stores and releases mechanical energy via molecular dipole reorientation.

2. **Backing / Damping Layer (Rear of crystal):**
   - Made of **tungsten-epoxy composite** or similar high-impedance lossy material.
   - **Function 1:** Absorbs the acoustic wave radiated backward by the crystal → prevents re-ringing.
   - **Function 2:** **Shortens the pulse length** → improves **axial resolution** $\Delta z = c \tau / 2$.
   - **Function 3:** Lowers the **Q factor** → widens the bandwidth → better image quality.

3. **Matching Layer(s) (Front of crystal):**
   - One or more quarter-wavelength (λ/4) layers of **intermediate acoustic impedance** $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{tissue}}}$.
   - **Function:** Reduces the **acoustic impedance mismatch** between the high-$Z$ PZT (~30 Mrayl) and low-$Z$ tissue (~1.54 Mrayl) — boosts transmission efficiency from ~19% to >95%.

4. **Acoustic Lens (Outer surface):**
   - Concave **RTV silicone** element.
   - **Function:** Focuses the ultrasound beam in the **elevation (out-of-plane) direction** to improve slice thickness.

**Working Cycle:**

```
Pulser (TX) → High V pulse across PZT → Converse PE → Acoustic wave → Tissue
Tissue → Echo returns → Direct PE → Voltage on electrodes → Preamplifier (RX)
```

---

#### **Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Derivation:** (See Derivation 2 in Section 3.2 for the full step-by-step proof.)

**Result:**

$$
N = \frac{D^{2}}{4 \lambda} = \frac{D^{2} f}{4 c}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $f = 3.5$ MHz $= 3.5 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $D = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m, $c = 1540$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Wavelength in tissue:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{3.5 \times 10^{6}} = 4.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.44 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Near-field length:**

$$
N = \frac{D^{2} f}{4 c} = \frac{(0.020)^{2} \times 3.5 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 1540}
$$

$$
N = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.5 \times 10^{6}}{6160} = \frac{1400}{6160} = 0.227 \text{ m} \approx 22.7 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Far-field divergence half-angle:**

$$
\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda}{D} = \frac{1.22 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.020} = \frac{5.37 \times 10^{-4}}{0.020} = 0.0268
$$

$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.0268) = 1.54^{\circ}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,N \approx 22.7 \text{ cm}, \quad \theta \approx 1.54^{\circ}\,}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **piezoelectric effect** with the relevant constitutive equations. Define the **electromechanical coupling coefficient $k$** and state its significance in transducer material selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **frequency response and Q factor** of an ultrasound transducer. With a sketch, explain the trade-off between **Q factor, bandwidth, and axial resolution**. A transducer has $f_r = 5$ MHz and a 6 dB bandwidth of 2 MHz. Calculate its **Q factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Piezoelectric Effect and Coupling Coefficient (7 marks)**

**Definition:**
The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., quartz, PZT, PVDF) to interconvert mechanical and electrical energy. It exists in two reversible forms.

**Direct Piezoelectric Effect (used in Reception):**
When a mechanical stress $T$ is applied to the crystal, an electric displacement $D$ is produced on the electrodes:

$$
D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^{T} \cdot E
$$

**Converse Piezoelectric Effect (used in Transmission):**
When an electric field $E$ is applied, the crystal mechanically strains by $S$:

$$
S = s^{E} \cdot T + d \cdot E
$$

The coefficient $d$ is **identical in both equations** — this is the **principle of reciprocity**.

**Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient $k$:**

$$
k^{2} = \frac{\text{Stored mechanical energy}}{\text{Input electrical energy}} = \frac{\text{Stored electrical energy}}{\text{Input mechanical energy}}
$$

$0 \leq k \leq 1$. Higher $k$ means more efficient energy conversion.

**Significance in Transducer Material Selection:**

| Material | $k$ | Choice Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Quartz | 0.10 | Stable, narrow-band; obsolete in imaging |
| PZT-5H | 0.50–0.60 | **Standard choice** for diagnostic imaging — high sensitivity, broadband |
| PVDF | 0.20 | Low $k$, but flexible and broadband; for IVUS/hockey-stick |
| PMN-PT (relaxor) | 0.90 | Highest $k$; emerging material for very-high-frequency probes |

---

#### **Part (b) — Q Factor, Bandwidth, and Axial Resolution (7 marks)**

**Definitions:**

- **Q factor** = $Q = f_r / \Delta f$, where $\Delta f$ is the −3 dB (or 6 dB) bandwidth around the resonant frequency $f_r$.
- **Bandwidth (BW)** = the range of frequencies over which the transducer responds efficiently.
- **Axial resolution** = the minimum distance along the beam axis at which two reflectors can be distinguished: $\Delta z = c \cdot \tau / 2$, where $\tau$ is the pulse duration.

**Trade-off Sketch:**

- **High Q (narrow band):** Long ringing pulse → poor axial resolution. (CW Doppler probes have $Q \approx 50 - 100$.)
