- **Low Q (wide band):** Short pulse → excellent axial resolution. (Pulsed imaging probes have $Q \approx 2 - 5$.)

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $f_r = 5$ MHz, **6 dB bandwidth** = 2 MHz (i.e., $\Delta f = 2$ MHz measured at the −6 dB points).

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{f_r}{\Delta f} = \frac{5 \text{ MHz}}{2 \text{ MHz}} = 2.5\,}
$$

This is a typical low-Q value appropriate for **pulsed diagnostic imaging** — short pulses give good axial resolution.

**Practical note:** Some texts define Q using the −3 dB bandwidth. If the question specifies 6 dB (half-amplitude), the calculation is identical — only the definition of $\Delta f$ changes.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing direct vs converse effect:** Many students write "converse effect generates voltage" — WRONG. Direct effect = mechanical → electrical (RX). Converse = electrical → mechanical (TX). **−2 to −3 marks** if confused.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 1/2 in $\Delta z = c\tau/2$:** The pulse travels to the reflector and back — remember the **round-trip**! **−1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Using $v_{\text{tissue}}$ instead of $v_{\text{crystal}}$ to compute crystal thickness:** The formula $t = v_c / (2 f_r)$ uses the **acoustic velocity inside the PZT crystal** ($\approx 4000$ m/s), NOT the tissue velocity (1540 m/s). **−2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Not stating units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners strictly deduct **½ to 1 mark** for missing units in the final answer.
>
> 5. **Skipping the matching layer justification:** In numerical problems involving reflection at a tissue interface, the question almost always requires a discussion of why a matching layer is necessary (Z mismatch → 88% reflection). Omitting this **loses 2–3 marks** in long answers.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Q factor and bandwidth:** Remember: $Q = f_r / \Delta f$ → **High Q = Narrow BW = Long Pulse = Poor Axial Resolution**. State the relationship explicitly.
>
> 7. **Drawing a "speaker" diagram instead of a layered cross-section:** The transducer construction question requires a **layered cross-section** (backing → PZT → matching → lens). A simple box diagram is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Transducer Principle**

- [x] **Transducer** = device that converts one form of energy to another; in medical US, electrical ⇌ acoustic via the **piezoelectric effect**.
- [x] **Direct piezoelectric effect:** mechanical stress → electric charge. **Used in RECEPTION.**
- [x] **Converse piezoelectric effect:** electric field → mechanical strain. **Used in TRANSMISSION.**
- [x] **Coupling coefficient $k$:** $k^2$ = ratio of stored mechanical energy to input electrical energy. PZT-5H has $k \approx 0.5 - 0.6$.
- [x] **PZT** = most common piezoelectric material in diagnostic US; **PVDF** = preferred for high-frequency flexible probes (IVUS).
- [x] **Transducer layers (rear to front):** Backing → PZT → Matching Layer(s) → Acoustic Lens.
- [x] **Backing layer:** shortens pulse, lowers Q, widens bandwidth, improves axial resolution.
- [x] **Matching layer:** quarter-wavelength, impedance $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_1 Z_3}$ — eliminates acoustic mismatch.
- [x] **Resonant frequency (thickness mode):** $f_r = v_c / (2t)$.
- [x] **Wavelength in tissue:** $\lambda = c / f$, with $c \approx 1540$ m/s in soft tissue.
- [x] **Q factor:** $Q = f_r / \Delta f$. **High Q = narrow band = poor axial resolution**; **low Q = wide band = good axial resolution**.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$. Air $\approx 0.0004$ Mrayl; soft tissue $\approx 1.54$ Mrayl; PZT $\approx 30$ Mrayl.
- [x] **Reflection coefficient (intensity):** $R_I = \left[\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right]^2$. At PZT–tissue: $\approx 81\%$ reflected without matching.
- [x] **Transmission coefficient (intensity):** $T_I = 4 Z_1 Z_2 / (Z_1 + Z_2)^2$. With proper matching: $> 95\%$.
- [x] **Near-field length:** $N = D^2 / (4 \lambda) = D^2 f / (4 c)$. Within $N$, beam is approximately collimated.
- [x] **Far-field divergence:** $\sin \theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$. Higher frequency or larger aperture = tighter beam.
- [x] **Axial resolution:** $\Delta z = c \tau / 2$. Shorter pulse $\tau$ → better resolution.
- [x] **Lateral resolution:** best at the focal point (near-field end or electronic focal zone).
- [x] **Reciprocity:** the same crystal coefficient $d$ governs TX and RX — same probe can both transmit and receive.
- [x] **Quarter-wave transformer principle** is borrowed directly from electromagnetic transmission-line theory.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Transducer Principle**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasound Instrumentation – Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ultrasound Instrumentation – Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Ultrasound Instrumentation – Block Diagram

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCBMT503 / Module 1):**
> An **Ultrasound Imaging System** is a non-invasive, real-time, radiation-free diagnostic modality that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **1 MHz to 20 MHz**) to produce cross-sectional images of internal body tissues. The block diagram of an ultrasound system defines the functional sub-systems that together generate, transmit, receive, process, and display the acoustic information derived from the body.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 1):**
> *"Generation of Ultrasound – Block diagram description of ultrasound system – Functions of each block."*
> This topic is **CO1-mapped** (Understand the principles of various medical imaging modalities) and tests both **Remembering** and **Understanding** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an ultrasound machine as a sophisticated **"echo-locator"** — the same principle a bat or a submarine sonar uses.

- The system **shouts** a very short, high-frequency sound pulse (inaudible to humans) into the body.
- Different tissues **bounce the sound back** with different intensities and at different times.
- The system **listens** carefully to these returning echoes and figures out:
  1. **How far** the reflecting structure is (from the *time delay*).
  2. **How dense or stiff** the tissue is (from the *echo amplitude*).
- Finally, it **paints a picture** on the screen, where the brightness of each dot represents the strength of the echo.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine standing in a dark room with a flashlight and a stopwatch. You shout at a wall and listen for the echo. The longer you wait to hear it, the farther the wall. If the echo is loud, the wall is hard; if soft, it's cushioned. Ultrasound does exactly this with sound waves — **hundreds of times per second** — in a thin slice of your body.

### 1.2 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard metrics are essential for KTU board answers:

| Metric | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Medical ultrasound frequency | **1 MHz – 20 MHz** |
| Diagnostic imaging range | **2 MHz – 10 MHz** |
| Speed of sound in soft tissue (average) | **1540 m/s** |
| Wavelength in soft tissue | **0.15 mm – 0.75 mm** |
| Typical PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency) | **1 kHz – 10 kHz** |
| Pulse duration | **0.5 µs – 2 µs** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Block Diagram – Functional Architecture

A complete diagnostic ultrasound system comprises **seven functional blocks**, each performing a defined signal operation. For a 14-mark KTU question, the student is expected to **draw, label, and explain the function of each block**.

The canonical KTU board diagram of an ultrasound system is:

> **[Block Diagram – Refer to Section 4 for the Mermaid flow diagram]**

The functional blocks are:

1. **Master Synchronizer (Clock / Pulser)**
2. **Transmitter (Pulser / HV Pulse Generator)**
3. **Transducer (Probe)**
4. **Receiver** (sub-blocks: RF Amplifier → TGC → Demodulator → Envelope Detector → Compression)
5. **Display (CRT / LCD / Scan Converter)**
6. **Scan Converter / Memory (for B-mode)**
7. **Power Supply & Cooling**

### 2.2 Block-by-Block Function Analysis

#### 🔹 Block 1: Master Synchronizer (Clock Generator)
- Acts as the **"heart"** of the system.
- Generates the **master timing pulses** that synchronize **all other blocks**.
- Determines the **PRF (Pulse Repetition Frequency)**, frame rate, and line rate.
- Ensures transmit and receive cycles do not overlap (the system cannot transmit and receive simultaneously in the same line).

#### 🔹 Block 2: Transmitter (Pulser)
- Produces **high-voltage (HV) electrical pulses** (typically **±100 V to ±300 V**) on command from the synchronizer.
- These pulses are used to *excite* the piezoelectric crystals in the transducer.
- Controls **pulse width** (number of cycles per pulse) and **pulse amplitude**.

#### 🔹 Block 3: Transducer (Probe)
- The **hand-held probe** held by the sonologist.
- Contains **piezoelectric crystals** (commonly Lead Zirconate Titanate – **PZT**).
- Operates on the **converse piezoelectric effect** (transmit) and **direct piezoelectric effect** (receive).
- Also contains an **acoustic matching layer** (impedance matching to skin) and a **damping backing block** (shortens pulse for better axial resolution).

#### 🔹 Block 4: Receiver
This is a **multi-stage sub-system** with five critical sub-blocks:

| Sub-block | Function |
|---|---|
| **RF Amplifier** | Initial low-noise amplification of weak returning echoes. |
| **TGC (Time Gain Compensation)** | Compensates for **depth-dependent attenuation** by increasing gain with time/depth. |
| **Demodulator / Rectifier** | Converts bipolar RF signal into unipolar form. |
| **Envelope Detector** | Extracts the *amplitude envelope* of the demodulated signal. |
| **Logarithmic Compressor** | Reduces the dynamic range from **~100 dB** down to display range (**~30–40 dB**). |

> [!NOTE]
> **TGC is the most heavily tested sub-block.** A common KTU question is: *"Why is TGC required? Explain its function with a graph."*

#### 🔹 Block 5: Display
- Converts the processed signal into a visible image.
- **A-mode:** Deflection vs. depth (1D spike display).
- **B-mode:** Brightness vs. depth (2D cross-sectional image).
- **M-mode:** Motion vs. time (used in echocardiography).
- Modern systems use **LCD/LED displays** driven by digital scan converters.

#### 🔹 Block 6: Scan Converter & Memory
- Required only for **B-mode (2D) imaging**.
- Stores the echo amplitude values in a **digital memory matrix** (pixel map).
- Each memory location corresponds to one **pixel** on the display.
- Reads out in a TV-compatible raster format (RS-170 or PAL).

#### 🔹 Block 7: Power Supply
- Provides regulated DC voltages to all sub-systems.
- Includes isolation to protect patient from electrical hazards (medical-grade isolation).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$Z = \rho \cdot c$$ | **Acoustic Impedance** (product of tissue density and sound velocity) | **Rayl** (kg/m²·s) |
| 2 | $$R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$ | **Reflection Coefficient** (intensity) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $$T = 1 - R = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$ | **Transmission Coefficient** | dimensionless |
| 4 | $$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$ | **Attenuation of intensity** with depth | W/m² |
| 5 | $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot x$$ | Attenuation in **dB** | dB |
| 6 | $$dB = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$$ | Pressure ratio in decibels | dB |
| 7 | $$dB = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ | Intensity ratio in decibels | dB |
| 8 | $$d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$$ | **Depth of reflector** (factor 2 for round-trip) | m |
| 9 | $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{\text{Pulse Width}}{\text{Period}} = \tau \cdot PRF$$ | Fraction of time system is transmitting | dimensionless |
| 10 | $$\text{Axial Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{Pulse Length}}{2} = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$ | Minimum resolvable depth difference | m |

**Where:**
- $Z_1, Z_2$ = acoustic impedances of two media
- $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (Np/m, then converted to dB)
- $c$ = speed of sound (**1540 m/s in soft tissue**)
- $t$ = time delay of echo
- $\tau$ = pulse duration
- $I_0$ = incident intensity

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Ultrasound instrumentation is the **workhorse of obstetrics, cardiology, and emergency medicine** because:
- **Zero ionizing radiation** (safe for *fetuses* and *children*).
- **Real-time imaging** (>30 frames/sec).
- **Portable and inexpensive** compared to CT/MRI.
- **Doppler extensions** enable blood-flow quantification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth of Reflector from Echo Time Delay

> **Physical Setup:** An ultrasound pulse is transmitted at $t = 0$. The echo from a reflector inside the body is received at time $t$. The pulse travels the **same path twice** (transmit path + return path).

**Step 1:** Let $d$ be the depth of the reflector from the transducer surface.
**Step 2:** The total distance travelled by the pulse is $2d$ (down and back).
**Step 3:** By definition, distance = velocity × time:

$$ 2d = c \cdot t $$

**Step 4:** Solve for $d$:

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$

**Step 5:** Substitute $c = 1540$ m/s for soft tissue:

$$ d = \frac{1540 \cdot t}{2} = 770 \cdot t \quad \text{(m, with } t \text{ in seconds)} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** The factor of **2** appears because the pulse must traverse the path **twice**. Forgetting this factor is a common KTU valuation error.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Acoustic Impedance & Reflection Coefficient

> **Physical Setup:** A longitudinal acoustic wave traveling in medium 1 (impedance $Z_1$) strikes a planar interface with medium 2 (impedance $Z_2$) at normal incidence.

**Step 1:** Define acoustic impedance for a longitudinal wave:

$$ Z = \rho \cdot c $$

where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) and $c$ = longitudinal sound velocity (m/s).

**Step 2:** Define the **pressure reflection coefficient** $r_p$ (ratio of reflected to incident pressure amplitudes):

$$ r_p = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} $$

**Step 3:** The **intensity reflection coefficient** $R$ is the square of the pressure coefficient (since intensity ∝ pressure²):

$$ R = r_p^{\,2} = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Step 4:** By conservation of energy, transmission coefficient $T$ is:

$$ T = 1 - R = 1 - \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Step 5:** Algebraic simplification:

$$ T = \frac{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2 - (Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2} $$

**Step 6:** Use identity $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$:

$$ T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** $R = 0$ requires $Z_1 = Z_2$ (no mismatch → no reflection, e.g., coupling gel). $R \to 1$ when $Z_2 \gg Z_1$ (e.g., soft-tissue/air interface gives ~99.9% reflection — hence the coupling gel is mandatory!).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Depth-Dependent Attenuation & Need for TGC

> **Physical Setup:** A plane wave of initial intensity $I_0$ propagates through tissue and is attenuated exponentially with distance $x$ due to absorption and scattering.

**Step 1:** The intensity at depth $x$ follows **Beer's Law** for acoustic waves:

$$ I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x} $$

**Step 2:** Convert to decibels for engineering convenience:

$$ \text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I(x)}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(e^{\alpha x}\right) = 10 \cdot \alpha \cdot x \cdot \log_{10}(e) $$

**Step 3:** Since $10 \log_{10}(e) = 4.343$ and $20 \log_{10}(e) = 8.686$:

$$ \text{Attenuation (dB)} = 4.343 \cdot \alpha x = 8.686 \cdot \alpha x \quad \text{(when } \alpha \text{ in Np/m or dB/m respectively)} $$

**Step 4:** Round-trip attenuation at depth $d$ (factor 2 for echo return):

$$ A_{\text{round-trip}} = 2 \times 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot d \quad \text{(dB)} $$

**Step 5:** A typical attenuation coefficient in soft tissue is **$\alpha = 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz**.

> **Conclusion:** Echoes from deep reflectors arrive **weaker** than those from shallow reflectors, even if the reflectors are identical. This is precisely why **TGC is required** — to apply a *time-varying gain* that compensates for this depth loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix – Effect of Attenuation (α = 0.5 dB/cm/MHz):**
>
> | Depth (cm) | One-way Loss (dB) | Round-Trip Loss (dB) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 0.5 | 1.0 |
> | 5 | 2.5 | 5.0 |
> | 10 | 5.0 | 10.0 |
> | 20 | 10.0 | 20.0 |
> | 30 | 15.0 | 30.0 |
>
> *At 5 MHz, an echo from 20 cm depth has lost **100 dB** compared to the surface pulse — a factor of **10¹⁰** in power!*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Axial Resolution from Pulse Length

> **Physical Setup:** Two reflectors are separated by a small axial distance. To resolve them, the **transmit pulse must end before the second echo returns** to the transducer.

**Step 1:** The spatial length of the ultrasonic pulse in tissue is:

$$ L = c \cdot \tau $$

**Step 2:** Two reflectors separated by $\Delta d$ are resolvable only if:

$$ \Delta d \geq \frac{L}{2} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} $$

**Step 3:** Therefore, **axial resolution**:

$$ \text{Axial Resolution} = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2} $$

> **Engineering Insight:** For $\tau = 1\,\mu s$ and $c = 1540$ m/s:
> $$\text{Axial Resolution} = \frac{1540 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.77 \text{ mm}$$
> This matches the clinical expectation of **sub-millimeter resolution** in modern scanners.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full Ultrasound System

> **KTU Board Standard:** The diagram below represents the canonical block diagram expected in a 14-mark answer. Arrows indicate the **direction of signal flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Master Synchronizer / Clock] --> B[Transmitter / Pulser]
    B --> C[Transducer Probe PZT Crystal]
    C --> D[Patient Body Tissue]
    D -- Reflected Echoes --> C
    C --> E[Receiver Pre Amplifier]
    E --> F[TGC Time Gain Compensator]
    F --> G[Demodulator / Rectifier]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector]
    H --> I[Logarithmic Compressor]
    I --> J[Scan Converter and Memory]
    J --> K[Display Monitor LCD]
    A -.Sync Pulses.-> E
    A -.Sync Pulses.-> F
    A -.Sync Pulses.-> J
    L[Power Supply] --- A
    L --- B
    L --- E
    L --- K
```

### Block Functional Description (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT [Transmit Path]
        T1[1 Master Synchronizer: Generates PRF and timing]
        T2[2 Transmitter Pulser: Produces HV electrical pulse]
        T3[3 Transducer: Converts electrical to acoustic via PZT]
    end

    subgraph MEDIUM [Patient]
        M1[Tissue Interfaces: Produce reflections]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [Receive Path]
        R1[4a Pre Amplifier: LNA stage]
        R2[4b TGC: Compensates attenuation]
        R3[4c Demodulator: RF to baseband]
        R4[4d Envelope Detector: Extracts amplitude]
        R5[4e Log Compressor: Reduces dynamic range]
    end

    subgraph DISPLAY [Image Formation]
        D1[5 Scan Converter: Digital pixel memory]
        D2[6 Display: 2D B mode image]
    end

    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> M1
    M1 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> D1 --> D2
    T1 -.Sync.- R1
    T1 -.Sync.- R2
    T1 -.Sync.- D1
```

## 4.2 Transducer Internal Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Acoustic Matching Layer: Z approximately Z tissue] --> B[Piezoelectric Crystal PZT: 1540 m/s]
    B --> C[Damping Backing Block: Absorbs rear vibrations]
    D[Electrodes on Crystal Faces: Apply HV pulse] --> B
    E[Acoustic Lens: Focuses beam laterally] --> A
```

### 4.3 TGC Function — Graphical Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Echo from shallow tissue: Strong amplitude] --> B[Receiver]
    C[Echo from deep tissue: Weak amplitude] --> B
    B --> D[TGC Gain Ramp]
    D --> E[Equalized Echo Amplitudes on Display]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Board Answer:**
> When drawing the block diagram, always show:
> 1. **Master Synchronizer** connected by **dashed lines** to *all* other blocks (indicating control/sync).
> 2. **Transducer** placed at the *boundary* between *system* and *patient* (drawn as a separate shaded region).
> 3. **Scan Converter** shown *only* for B-mode systems; omit for A-mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Drawing a *linear* signal-flow block diagram *without* showing the **transducer as an interface** to the patient. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> - **Common Error 2:** Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the depth formula ($d = ct/2$). Costs 1 mark.
> - **Common Error 3:** Confusing **transmission coefficient** with **reflection coefficient** — they are *not* simply additive. $T = 1 - R$ only for *intensities*, not for *pressure amplitudes*.
> - **Common Error 4:** Writing the units of acoustic impedance as "kg/m³" (that is *density*, not *impedance*). Correct unit is **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)**.
> - **Common Error 5:** Not explaining the **dual role of the transducer** (transmitter and receiver). Examiner expects mention of *converse* and *direct* piezoelectric effects.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define acoustic impedance. What is its SI unit?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Acoustic impedance is the product of the density of a medium and the velocity of sound in that medium. It is defined as the **opposition offered by a medium to the propagation of acoustic waves**.

$$ Z = \rho \cdot c $$

where $\rho$ is the density (kg/m³) and $c$ is the longitudinal sound velocity (m/s).
**SI Unit:** **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹), also called MKS Rayl. 1 Rayl = 1 kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹.

> *Mark split: Definition (1), Formula (1), Unit (1).*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the principle of piezoelectric effect used in ultrasound transducers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The piezoelectric effect works in two directions:

1. **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** (used in *receive* mode):
   Certain crystals (e.g., **PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate**, quartz) generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical pressure. Returning acoustic echoes deform the crystal, producing a measurable voltage.

2. **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect** (used in *transmit* mode):
   When an alternating voltage is applied across such a crystal, it undergoes mechanical deformation (vibrates) at the frequency of the applied voltage, producing ultrasonic waves.

> *Mark split: Direct effect (1), Converse effect (1), Naming PZT/material (1).*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]*

> **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a diagnostic ultrasound imaging system and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the relationship between the depth of a reflector and the time delay of the received echo. Why is the factor of 2 included? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The block diagram (drawn as in Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Master Synchronizer (1 mark):** Generates master clock pulses that synchronize all subsystems. Sets the **PRF** and frame rate.
2. **Transmitter / Pulser (1 mark):** Produces **high-voltage short pulses** (≈100–300 V, ≈1 µs) to excite the transducer.
3. **Transducer (Probe) (1 mark):** Contains **piezoelectric crystal (PZT)**. Converts electrical pulses to ultrasound (converse effect) and back to voltage on receive (direct effect).
4. **Receiver (2 marks):** Has five sub-stages:
   - **Pre-amplifier** (low-noise).
   - **TGC** (compensates depth attenuation).
   - **Demodulator** (rectifier).
   - **Envelope detector** (extracts amplitude).
   - **Logarithmic compressor** (reduces 100 dB dynamic range to ~30 dB).
5. **Scan Converter (1 mark):** Stores echo amplitudes in a 2D pixel memory for B-mode display.
6. **Display (1 mark):** Shows the final B-mode grayscale image.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1:** Let $d$ = depth of reflector, $t$ = time delay of echo, $c$ = velocity of sound in tissue (1540 m/s).

**Step 2:** The pulse travels **down** to the reflector and **back** to the transducer. Total path = $2d$.

**Step 3:** Equating distance = velocity × time:

$$ 2d = c \cdot t $$

**Step 4:** Solving for $d$:

$$ d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$

**Step 5:** Substituting $c = 1540$ m/s:

$$ d = 770 \cdot t \quad \text{(m, with } t \text{ in s)} $$

**Step 6:** **Why the factor of 2?**
The ultrasound pulse makes a **round trip** — from the transducer down to the reflector and back. Hence the total path length is **twice the depth**. If the factor of 2 were omitted, the depth would be **overestimated by 100%**, giving a clinically dangerous error.

> *Mark split for (b): Setting up equation (2), Deriving formula (2), Numerical substitution (1), Factor-of-2 explanation (2).*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Set B]*

> **(a)** Explain the function of the receiver block in detail. Why is **Time Gain Compensation (TGC)** essential? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define acoustic impedance. Derive the reflection and transmission coefficients for a normal-incidence acoustic wave at a tissue interface. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**The Receiver Block performs 5 sequential operations:**

1. **Pre-Amplifier (1 mark):** Weak returning echoes (often microvolt-level) are first amplified with a low-noise amplifier (LNA) placed as close to the transducer as possible.

2. **TGC — Time Gain Compensation (2 marks):** Echoes from deeper tissues are weaker due to **attenuation** ($I = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$). TGC applies a *time-varying gain* — small for early (shallow) echoes, large for late (deep) echoes — so that echoes from equal reflectors at different depths appear **equal in brightness** on the display.

3. **Demodulator (Rectifier) (1 mark):** Converts the bipolar RF signal into a unipolar signal (full-wave rectification).

4. **Envelope Detector (1 mark):** Extracts the slowly-varying amplitude envelope from the rectified signal using a diode-RC circuit.

5. **Logarithmic Compressor (1 mark):** Reduces the dynamic range from **~100 dB** (raw) to **~30 dB** (displayable) using a logarithmic amplifier.

6. **Why TGC is essential (1 mark):** Without TGC, deep structures would appear too dark and shallow ones too bright, making diagnostic interpretation impossible.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Acoustic impedance $Z$ is the product of density and longitudinal sound velocity in a medium:
$$ Z = \rho \cdot c \quad \text{(Rayl)} $$

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a longitudinal acoustic wave of pressure amplitude $P_i$ traveling in medium 1 ($Z_1$) striking medium 2 ($Z_2$) normally.

**Step 2:** Define **pressure reflection coefficient** $r_p$:

$$ r_p = \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} $$

**Step 3:** The **intensity reflection coefficient** $R$ (ratio of reflected to incident *intensity*):

$$ R = \left(\frac{P_r}{P_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Step 4:** By conservation of energy, **transmission coefficient** $T$:

$$ T = 1 - R = 1 - \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$

**Step 5:** Simplify using $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$:

$$ T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2} $$

> *Mark split: Definition (1), Pressure coefficient (2), Intensity R (1), T formula (2), Final simplification (1).*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ⭐ Ultrasound frequency range for diagnosis: **2–10 MHz**; sound velocity in soft tissue: **1540 m/s**.
- ⭐ Block diagram has **6 main blocks** (some references show 7) — always start from **Master Synchronizer**.
- ⭐ Transducer uses **PZT crystal** operating on **direct + converse piezoelectric effect**.
- ⭐ **Depth formula** always has **factor of 2**: $d = ct/2$. Forgetting it = loss of marks.
- ⭐ **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$, unit **Rayl**. It governs reflection at interfaces.
- ⭐ **Reflection coefficient** is for *intensity*; pressure reflection has a *linear* form, not squared.
- ⭐ **TGC** compensates for **exponential attenuation** with depth — its gain *increases* with time.
- ⭐ **Attenuation coefficient** in soft tissue ≈ **0.5 dB/cm/MHz** (round-trip doubles it).
- ⭐ **Axial resolution** = $c\tau/2$ (depends on **pulse length**, not frequency alone).
- ⭐ **Scan converter** is needed *only* for **B-mode** 2D imaging, not for A-mode.
- ⭐ **Display modes:** A = Amplitude, B = Brightness (2D), M = Motion (time-based).
- ⭐ **Logarithmic compression** reduces **~100 dB** dynamic range to **~30–40 dB** for display.
- ⭐ **Pulse repetition frequency (PRF)** must be **low enough** to allow echoes to return from maximum imaging depth (e.g., PRF ≤ c/2D, where D = max depth).

> *End of Module 1 Notes — Ultrasound Instrumentation – Block Diagram.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Artifacts
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Artifacts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **image artifact** in medical imaging is defined as any **discrepancy, distortion, or signal anomaly** between the reconstructed image and the true physical/anatomical structure of the object being imaged, which does not correspond to the actual tissue characteristics. Artifacts are *non-pathological* features that arise due to imperfections in the imaging system, the reconstruction algorithm, the patient, or the physical interaction between the imaging radiation and the tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Image Artifacts):**
> Artifacts are not real anatomical structures. They are **unwanted signals** that degrade the **diagnostic accuracy**, **contrast resolution**, and **spatial fidelity** of the medical image. Their recognition is a fundamental competency in radiology and biomedical engineering.

**Mathematical Representation:**

If $I_{true}(x,y)$ is the ideal image and $I_{observed}(x,y)$ is the actually reconstructed image, then the artifact map can be expressed as:

$$
A(x,y) = I_{observed}(x,y) - I_{true}(x,y)
$$

Where $A(x,y)$ represents the artifact component — ideally $A(x,y) = 0$ for a perfect imaging system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a photograph of a beautiful sunset through a dirty window. The dust smudges, fingerprints, and water droplets on the glass are **not part of the sunset** — they are unwanted distortions that hide the true beauty of the scene. Similarly, in medical imaging:

- The **sunset** = the true anatomy inside the patient's body.
- The **dirty window** = the imaging system, physical laws, and patient movement.
- The **smudges & droplets** = image artifacts.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as you would clean the window before photographing the sunset, a radiographer must recognize and minimize artifacts (using techniques like patient immobilization, proper shielding, calibration) to obtain a clean diagnostic image.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of Artifacts

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Origin** | System, physics, patient, or algorithm-induced |
| **Appearance** | Geometric, intensity-based, structured, or random |
| **Effect** | Degrades **diagnostic confidence** and may **mimic pathology** |
| **Modality** | Specific to X-ray, CT, MRI, Ultrasound, PET, SPECT |
| **Correction** | Hardware, software, or protocol-based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:**
> - **Artifact-to-Signal Ratio (ASR)** is sometimes used to quantify artifact severity: $ASR = \frac{\sigma_{artifact}}{\mu_{signal}}$ where $\sigma_{artifact}$ is the standard deviation in the artifact region and $\mu_{signal}$ is the mean signal intensity.
> - In CT, the Hounsfield Unit (HU) deviation $\Delta HU > 5$ is generally considered a clinically significant artifact.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Image artifacts are broadly classified into **four fundamental categories**:

1. **Physics-Based Artifacts** – Arise from the inherent physical interaction of the radiation/field with matter (e.g., beam hardening, acoustic shadowing).
2. **System/Hardware-Based Artifacts** – Caused by detector imperfections, calibration errors, or scanner malfunction (e.g., ring artifacts, gradient nonlinearity).
3. **Patient-Based Artifacts** – Caused by voluntary/involuntary motion, metal implants, or physiological processes (e.g., motion blur, metal streak).
4. **Reconstruction/Algorithm-Based Artifacts** – Originate from mathematical approximations in image reconstruction (e.g., aliasing, partial volume effect).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Image Artifacts

Below is a systematic breakdown of the major artifact types across imaging modalities, structured for rapid KTU exam preparation.

### A. X-Ray and CT Imaging Artifacts

#### 1. Beam Hardening Artifact
- **Cause:** As the polychromatic X-ray beam passes through tissue, **lower-energy photons are preferentially absorbed** (photoelectric effect dominates at low energies), leaving a beam with a higher mean energy — hence "harder."
- **Appearance:** Dark streaks/cupping artifact between two dense structures (e.g., between the petrous bones in head CT, or between the shoulders).
- **Mathematical Basis:** The linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(E)$ is energy-dependent. For a true CT reconstruction, we assume:

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\int \mu(x,E) \, dx}
$$

For polychromatic beams, the effective linear attenuation becomes:

$$
\bar{\mu} = \frac{\int \mu(E) \, S(E) \, e^{-\int \mu(x,E)dx} \, dE}{\int S(E) \, e^{-\int \mu(x,E)dx} \, dE}
$$

Where $S(E)$ is the X-ray spectrum. The variation of $\bar{\mu}$ from the assumed monochromatic value produces the artifact.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Methods:** Pre-hardening filtration (e.g., using a thin aluminum or copper filter at the X-ray tube), beam-hardening correction algorithms (polynomial correction, iterative reconstruction).

#### 2. Motion Artifact
- **Cause:** Patient movement (voluntary, respiratory, cardiac, peristaltic) during acquisition.
- **Appearance:** Blurring, ghosting, or double-contour edges.
- **CT-specific:** In helical CT, motion causes **step artifacts** at the edges of moving structures.
- **Correction:** Patient immobilization, breath-hold techniques, ECG gating, faster acquisition times.

#### 3. Metal / High-Density Artifact
- **Cause:** Dental fillings, surgical clips, prosthetic implants cause **photon starvation** — almost all photons are absorbed.
- **Appearance:** Bright streaks radiating from the metal object.
- **Mathematical Insight:** When the line integral $\int \mu(x) dx$ is extremely large, the measured signal $I$ approaches noise levels. Logarithmic transformation amplifies this noise:

$$
p = -\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \int \mu(x) \, dx
$$

When $I \to 0$, $p \to \infty$, causing extreme streak artifacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** Dual-energy CT, metal artifact reduction (MAR) algorithms, increased tube voltage (kVp), or using iterative reconstruction.

#### 4. Ring Artifact
- **Cause:** Miscalibrated or defective CT detector element. Each detector contributes to a circular ring in the image.
- **Appearance:** Concentric rings centered at the rotation axis.
- **Correction:** Detector calibration, flat-field correction, sinogram smoothing.

#### 5. Partial Volume Artifact
- **Cause:** When a single voxel contains multiple tissue types (e.g., bone and air at the skull base), the resulting CT number is a weighted average, misrepresenting all tissues.
- **Appearance:** Blurring at tissue boundaries; "pseudo-" lesions.

$$
HU_{voxel} = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot HU_i, \quad \text{where } \sum f_i = 1
$$

#### 6. Aliasing (Undersampling) Artifact
- **Cause:** Insufficient sampling rate. Violation of the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Appearance:** Streaks radiating from high-contrast edges.

#### 7. Quantum Mottle (Noise) Artifact
- **Cause:** Statistical fluctuation in photon number. Per **Poisson statistics**: $\sigma_N = \sqrt{N}$, so signal-to-noise ratio $SNR = N / \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{N}$.
- **Appearance:** Grainy, noisy image, especially at low-dose acquisitions.

### B. MRI-Specific Artifacts

#### 1. Motion Artifact (Ghosting)
- **Cause:** Periodic motion (cardiac, respiratory) coupled with phase-encoding direction. Ghosts appear as repeated copies of the structure.
- **Correction:** Gating, triggering, breath-hold, PROPELLER/BLADE sequences.

#### 2. Susceptibility Artifact
- **Cause:** Local magnetic field distortions near interfaces of differing magnetic susceptibility (e.g., air-tissue, metal-tissue).
- **Mathematical:** The local field perturbation is:

$$
\Delta B_{local} = \chi \cdot B_0
$$

where $\chi$ is the magnetic susceptibility difference.

- **Correction:** Smaller voxel size, higher readout bandwidth, spin-echo (vs. gradient-echo) sequences, susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) compensation.

#### 3. Chemical Shift Artifact
- **Cause:** Difference in resonance frequency between fat ($\sim 3.5$ ppm) and water protons. The frequency offset is:

$$
\Delta f = \gamma \cdot B_0 \cdot \sigma_{cs}
$$

where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio and $\sigma_{cs}$ is the chemical shift (in ppm).

- **Appearance:** Spatial misregistration between fat and water — water signal appears shifted in the frequency-encoding direction.

#### 4. Aliasing (Wrap-around) Artifact
- **Cause:** Anatomy outside the field of view (FOV) is folded back into the image.
- **Correction:** Increase FOV, use oversampling (no phase wrap), surface coils.

#### 5. Truncation Artifact (Gibbs Phenomenon)
- **Cause:** Finite sampling of k-space. The reconstructed image shows oscillating ripples near sharp edges.
- **Correction:** Higher matrix size, filtering, partial Fourier reconstruction.

#### 6. RF Artifact (Zipper Artifact)
- **Cause:** External RF interference leaking into the Faraday-shielded room.

### C. Ultrasound Artifacts

| Artifact | Cause | Appearance |
|----------|-------|------------|
| **Acoustic Shadowing** | Highly attenuating structure (bone, stone, gas) | Dark region behind the structure |
| **Acoustic Enhancement** | Fluid-filled structure (cyst) less attenuating than surrounding | Bright region behind the cyst |
| **Reverberation** | Sound bouncing between two strong reflectors | Multiple equally-spaced echoes |
| **Mirror Image** | Strong reflector (diaphragm) acts as mirror | Duplicate of structure on opposite side |
| **Speckle Noise** | Random interference of scattered echoes | Granular texture |
| **Refraction Artifact** | Beam bends at tissue interfaces | Edge shadowing |

### D. Nuclear Medicine (PET/SPECT) Artifacts

- **Attenuation Artifact:** Photons absorbed before reaching detector → underestimation of activity in deep structures.
- **Partial Volume Effect:** Spillover between adjacent regions of differing activity.
- **Motion Artifact:** Patient or organ movement.
- **Randoms & Scatter:** Coincidence events from random photon pairs or Compton scatter.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Reference Table for KTU University Examinations (PCBMT503 Module 1)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $A(x,y) = I_{obs}(x,y) - I_{true}(x,y)$ | Artifact map definition | Conceptual |
| 2 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer-Lambert attenuation law | X-ray, CT |
| 3 | $p = -\ln(I/I_0) = \int \mu(x)dx$ | CT projection (line integral) | CT reconstruction |
| 4 | $HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | Hounsfield Unit definition | CT |
| 5 | $\Delta B_{local} = \chi \cdot B_0$ | Susceptibility-induced field | MRI |
| 6 | $\Delta f = \gamma B_0 \sigma_{cs}$ | Chemical shift frequency offset | MRI |
| 7 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | All modalities |
| 8 | $SNR = N/\sqrt{N} = \sqrt{N}$ | Quantum-limited SNR | X-ray, CT |
| 9 | $ASR = \sigma_{artifact}/\mu_{signal}$ | Artifact-to-signal ratio | Quality metrics |
| 10 | $\bar{\mu} = \frac{\int \mu(E)S(E)e^{-\int \mu dx}dE}{\int S(E)e^{-\int \mu dx}dE}$ | Polyenergetic effective $\mu$ | Beam hardening |
| 11 | $\sigma_N = \sqrt{N}$ | Poisson noise std deviation | Statistical noise |
| 12 | $Z_{ref} = \int_0^T v(t) e^{-i2\pi k\cdot r(t)} dt$ | Motion during k-space acquisition | MRI motion |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Relevance of Artifact Reduction:**
> - **AI/Deep Learning Reconstruction:** Modern CT/MRI scanners (e.g., Siemens AI-Rad, GE TrueFidelity, Canon AiCE) use deep convolutional neural networks to suppress noise and streak artifacts.
> - **Adaptive Filtering:** Real-time beam-hardening correction in interventional fluoroscopy.
> - **Metal Artifact Reduction (MAR):** Critical in orthopedic and dental imaging where implants are common.
> - **Quantitative Imaging:** Accurate HU measurements in radiotherapy planning demand strict artifact control.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Beam Hardening & Cupping Artifact

We start from the polychromatic Beer-Lambert law and derive the effective attenuation that leads to cupping artifact.

**Step 1: Define the X-ray Spectrum**

The polychromatic X-ray beam emerging from the tube has a photon flux spectrum $S(E)$, where $E$ is photon energy. The spectrum has a minimum cutoff $E_{min}$ (after filtration) and a maximum $E_{max} = kVp$.

$$
I_0 = \int_{E_{min}}^{E_{max}} S(E) \, dE
$$

**Step 2: Transmitted Intensity After Path Length $x$**

Each energy component attenuates independently:

$$
I(x) = \int_{E_{min}}^{E_{max}} S(E) \, e^{-\mu(E) x} \, dE
$$

**Step 3: Apparent Linear Attenuation Coefficient**

The CT system assumes a *monochromatic* relationship:

$$
I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\bar{\mu} x}
$$

Solving for $\bar{\mu}$:

$$
\bar{\mu}(x) = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left( \frac{I(x)}{I_0} \right) = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left( \frac{\int S(E) e^{-\mu(E)x} dE}{\int S(E) dE} \right)
$$

**Step 4: Substitution and Manipulation**

To express this in KTU's standard exam form, we rewrite:

$$
\bar{\mu}(x) = \frac{\int S(E) \mu(E) e^{-\mu(E)x} dE}{\int S(E) e^{-\mu(E)x} dE} \quad \text{(weighted average form)}
$$

This is the **effective attenuation coefficient** seen by the system. The key insight: as $x$ increases (thicker tissue), the low-energy photons are removed first (since $\mu(E)$ is higher for low $E$), making the beam "harder" (higher mean energy) and reducing $\bar{\mu}$.

**Step 5: Deriving the Cupping Artifact**

For a uniform cylindrical object of radius $R$ imaged in CT, the reconstructed $\mu_{recon}$ at a radial position $r$ from the center equals $\bar{\mu}(2\sqrt{R^2 - r^2})$, which is the effective $\mu$ along a chord of length $2\sqrt{R^2 - r^2}$.

- At the **center** ($r = 0$), chord length $= 2R$ (longest path) → $\bar{\mu}$ is smallest.
- At the **edge** ($r = R$), chord length $\to 0$ → $\bar{\mu} \to$ true $\mu$.

This means reconstructed values are **lower at the center** than at the edges, producing a "cup-shaped" intensity profile — the **cupping artifact**.

**Step 6: Mathematical Expression for Cupping**

$$
\Delta HU(r) = HU_{true} - HU_{recon}(r) = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{true} - \bar{\mu}(2\sqrt{R^2-r^2})}{\mu_{water}}
$$

This $\Delta HU$ is **maximum at the center** and **zero at the edge** — a clear signature of beam-hardening cupping.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sampling Theorem & Aliasing Artifact

**Step 1: Nyquist Criterion**

To reconstruct a bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ without aliasing, the sampling rate $f_s$ must satisfy:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

**Step 2: Sampling in Projection Space (CT)**

In CT, the projection data $p(\theta, s)$ is sampled at discrete detector positions $s_n = n \cdot \Delta s$ and discrete angles $\theta_m = m \cdot \Delta \theta$.

For an object of radius $R$ with maximum spatial frequency $f_{max} = 1/(2 \cdot \Delta x)$ where $\Delta x$ is the smallest resolvable feature, the required detector pitch is:

$$
\Delta s \leq \frac{1}{2 f_{max}} = \Delta x
$$

**Step 3: Aliasing Condition**

If $\Delta s > \Delta x$, high-frequency components above $f_{Nyquist} = 1/(2\Delta s)$ fold back into the reconstructed image, appearing as streak artifacts radiating from high-contrast edges.

The aliased frequency $f_{alias}$ from a true frequency $f_{true}$ is:

$$
f_{alias} = \vert f_{true} - n f_s \vert, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad f_{alias} \in [-f_{Nyquist}, f_{Nyquist}]
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Chemical Shift Artifact in MRI

**Step 1: Larmor Frequency**

The Larmor frequency of protons in a static field $B_0$ is:

$$
\omega_0 = \gamma B_0
$$

**Step 2: Chemical Shift**

The local electron cloud shields the nucleus. The effective field is:

$$
B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma_{cs})
$$

The resonance frequency becomes:

$$
\omega = \gamma B_0 (1 - \sigma_{cs})
$$

**Step 3: Frequency Difference Between Fat and Water**

$$
\Delta \omega = \gamma B_0 (\sigma_{fat} - \sigma_{water}) = \gamma B_0 \cdot \Delta \sigma_{cs}
$$

For protons at $1.5\,T$: $\sigma_{fat} \approx 3.5$ ppm, $\sigma_{water} \approx 4.7$ ppm → $\Delta\sigma_{cs} \approx 3.5$ ppm (fat relative to water reference).

The frequency offset in Hz is:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{\gamma B_0 \cdot \Delta \sigma_{cs}}{2\pi} = 42.58 \cdot B_0 \cdot \Delta \sigma_{cs} \quad [Hz]
$$

**Step 4: Spatial Misregistration**

In the frequency-encoding direction, the spatial position is determined by frequency. A frequency offset $\Delta f$ corresponds to a positional shift:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{\Delta f}{BW_{pixel}}
$$

where $BW_{pixel}$ is the receiver bandwidth per pixel.

**Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

For $B_0 = 1.5\,T$, $\Delta\sigma_{cs} = 3.5$ ppm:

$$
\Delta f = 42.58 \times 1.5 \times 3.5 = 223.5 \quad \text{Hz}
$$

For a typical pixel bandwidth of $200$ Hz/pixel:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{223.5}{200} = 1.12 \text{ pixels}
$$

So fat appears shifted by ~1.12 pixels relative to water in the readout direction — a clinically visible artifact in abdominal and orbital MRI.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Artifact Classification & Diagnosis

| Modality | Artifact Name | Root Cause | Diagnostic Appearance | Primary Correction |
|----------|---------------|------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| X-ray | Quantum mottle | Low photon statistics | Grainy image | Increase mAs |
| CT | Beam hardening | Polychromatic spectrum | Cupping / dark streaks | Pre-filtration, software correction |
| CT | Ring | Detector miscalibration | Concentric rings | Recalibration |
| CT | Motion | Patient breathing | Blurring, step edges | Breath-hold, faster scan |
| CT | Metal streak | Photon starvation | Bright streaks from metal | MAR algorithms, dual-energy |
| CT | Partial volume | Voxel heterogeneity | Blurred boundaries | Thinner slices |
| CT | Aliasing | Undersampling | Streak patterns | Increase projections |
| MRI | Susceptibility | $\chi$ mismatch | Signal loss near metal/air | Spin-echo, smaller voxels |
| MRI | Chemical shift | $\Delta \sigma_{cs}$ fat/water | Fat-water misregistration | Higher BW, fat suppression |
| MRI | Motion ghosting | Periodic motion | Ghosts in phase direction | Gating, triggering |
| MRI | Wrap-around | Small FOV | Anatomy folds in | Increase FOV, oversample |
| MRI | Truncation (Gibbs) | Finite k-space | Ripples near edges | Higher matrix |
| US | Acoustic shadow | High attenuation | Dark region | Adjust angle, frequency |
| US | Enhancement | Low attenuation (fluid) | Bright region | Recognize normal variant |
| US | Reverberation | Multiple reflections | Parallel echoes | Change probe angle |
| US | Speckle | Coherent interference | Granular noise | Spatial compounding |
| PET | Attenuation | Photon absorption | Apparent low activity deep | Attenuation correction (CT-AC) |
| PET | Scatter | Compton events | Reduced contrast | Energy windowing, correction |
| SPECT | Motion blur | Patient motion | Blurred hot/cold spots | Shorter acquisition, gating |
