where the integration path is the ray parametrized by $r$ and $\theta$:

$$
x = r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta \qquad y = r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the rotated coordinates into the line integral:**

$$
P_\theta(r) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta)\, ds
$$

**Step 2 — Take the 1-D Fourier Transform of $P_\theta(r)$ with respect to $r$:**

$$
S_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} P_\theta(r)\, e^{-j\omega r}\, dr
$$

Substituting $P_\theta(r)$:

$$
S_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta)\, ds \right] e^{-j\omega r}\, dr
$$

**Step 3 — Swap the order of integration:**

$$
S_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta)\, e^{-j\omega r}\, dr\, ds
$$

**Step 4 — Change of variables.** Define the rotated Fourier-space coordinates:

$$
\omega_x = \omega\cos\theta \qquad \omega_y = \omega\sin\theta
$$

A standard change-of-variables identity for the 2-D Fourier Transform gives:

$$
\iint f(x,y)\, e^{-j(\omega_x x + \omega_y y)}\, dx\, dy = F(\omega_x, \omega_y)
$$

With the change of variables $(x,y) \to (r,s)$ and recognizing that the Jacobian of the rotation is unity, the double integral becomes:

$$
S_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta,\; r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta)\, e^{-j\omega(\omega_x r / \omega + \omega_y s / \omega)} \cdot \text{(Jacobian)}\, dr\, ds
$$

Carrying out the change of variables carefully (using $x = r\cos\theta - s\sin\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta + s\cos\theta$) yields:

$$
S_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x,y)\, e^{-j(\omega_x x + \omega_y y)}\, dx\, dy
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize this as the 2-D Fourier Transform evaluated at the rotated coordinates:**

$$
S_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta,\; \omega\sin\theta)
$$

This is the **Fourier Slice Theorem** stated in equation form. $\quad\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ART vs SIRT vs Fourier

| Property | Direct Fourier Method | ART | SIRT |
|----------|----------------------|-----|------|
| Mathematical basis | Fourier Slice Theorem | Linear algebra, Kaczmarz | Linear algebra, simultaneous correction |
| Data requirement | Projection samples on radial k-space grid | Full ray-pixel matrix $A$ | Full ray-pixel matrix $A$ |
| Computational cost per iteration | One 1-D FFT per angle + 2-D IFFT | $O(MN)$ per sweep | $O(MN)$ per iteration |
| Noise handling | Poor (high-frequency amplification) | Moderate (one-ray sensitivity) | Good (averages over all rays) |
| Convergence speed | Single pass (after interpolation) | Fast (few sweeps) | Slow but stable |
| Implementation complexity | Medium (interpolation issues) | Low | Low |
| Use in clinical CT | Theoretical basis for FBP | Modern MBIR / low-dose CT | Modern MBIR / low-dose CT |
| When preferred | Real-time analytical recon | Sparse-view, low-dose | Noisy, incomplete data |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ART Iteration on a 2×2 Image

Suppose we have a 2×2 image with pixels $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$ and two measured projections (rays):

$$
x_1 + x_2 = 4 \qquad x_3 + x_4 = 2
$$

Initial guess: $x^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0, 0)$. Use $\lambda = 1$.

**Iteration 1, Ray 1:** Predicted $= 0$. Error $= 4 - 0 = 4$. Sum of $a_{1j}^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 = 2$. Correction term $= 4/2 = 2$.

- $x_1^{(1)} = 0 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2$
- $x_2^{(1)} = 0 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2$

**Iteration 1, Ray 2:** Predicted $= 0$. Error $= 2 - 0 = 2$. Correction term $= 2/2 = 1$.

- $x_3^{(1)} = 0 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1$
- $x_4^{(1)} = 0 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1$

**Result after 1 sweep:** $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} = (2, 2, 1, 1)$, which exactly satisfies both projection equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to demonstrate ART, choose a tiny 2×2 or 2-pixel example. Examiners give full credit for *any* working numerical walkthrough; the algebra is the focus, not the image complexity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fourier Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object f x,y] --> B[Acquire Parallel-Beam Projections P_theta r]
    B --> C[Stack Projections into Sinogram]
    C --> D[Apply 1D FFT to Each Projection Row]
    D --> E[Populate Radial Lines in 2D k-space Grid]
    E --> F[2D Interpolation onto Cartesian Grid]
    F --> G[Apply 2D Inverse FFT]
    G --> H[Reconstructed Image f_hat x,y]
    H --> I{Compare with Ground Truth}
    I -->|Error too high| J[Refine Interpolation Method]
    J --> F
    I -->|Acceptable| K[Output Final CT Image]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Iterative Reconstruction Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measured Projections b_i] --> B[Initialize Pixel Vector x_j = 0]
    B --> C[Forward Project: Compute A x]
    C --> D[Compute Residual r_i = b_i minus A x_i]
    D --> E{ART or SIRT?}
    E -->|ART| F1[Update Pixels After Each Ray]
    E -->|SIRT| F2[Accumulate Updates Across All Rays]
    F1 --> G[Apply Relaxation lambda]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Compute New Image Estimate x new]
    H --> I{Convergence Met?}
    I -->|No| C
    I -->|Yes| J[Output Reconstructed Image]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map of the Fourier Slice Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Object Domain f x,y] -- 2D FFT --> B[Fourier Domain F omega_x, omega_y]
    C[Projection P_theta r] -- 1D FFT --> D[Slice S_theta omega]
    B -. radial line at angle theta .-> D
    D -- Inverse mapping --> C
    B -- 2D Inverse FFT --> A
```

### 4.4 ART vs SIRT Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Reconstruction Method] --> B{Data Quality}
    B -->|Clean, dense| C[Direct Fourier / FBP]
    B -->|Sparse or noisy| D{Real-time Need?}
    D -->|Yes| E[ART]
    D -->|No| F[SIRT or MBIR]
    E --> G[Apply ART Update Rule]
    F --> H[Apply SIRT Update Rule]
    G --> I[Output Image]
    H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Fourier Slice Theorem. Why is it called the "Central" Slice Theorem?*

**Model Answer:**
The Fourier Slice Theorem states that the 1-D Fourier Transform of a parallel-beam projection of an object $f(x,y)$, taken at projection angle $\theta$, is equal to a 1-D slice through the 2-D Fourier Transform $F(\omega_x, \omega_y)$ of the object at the same angle $\theta$. Mathematically, $S_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$.
It is called the *Central* Slice Theorem because each radial slice passes through the **origin** (centre) of the 2-D frequency plane.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between the Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART) and the Simultaneous Iterative Reconstruction Technique (SIRT) in CT.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ART | SIRT |
|---------|-----|------|
| Update strategy | Pixel update after **each individual ray** | Pixel update after **all rays in an iteration** |
| Convergence | Fast but noisy / salt-and-pepper artifacts | Slower but smoother |
| Noise sensitivity | High | Low |
| Computational cost per iteration | Lower | Higher |
| Preferred scenario | Sparse-view, faster recon | Noisy data, regularized recon |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *Derive the Fourier Slice Theorem (Central Slice Theorem) for parallel-beam CT projection data. Clearly state all assumptions and intermediate steps.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *With the help of a block diagram, explain the Direct Fourier Reconstruction (DFR) algorithm. Discuss its limitations and compare it with the Filtered Back-Projection method.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. Present all five derivation steps:

1. Define parallel-beam projection (Radon transform).
2. Substitute rotated coordinates.
3. Take 1-D FT of $P_\theta(r)$ with respect to $r$.
4. Perform change of variables using rotation matrix.
5. Recognize the resulting double integral as the 2-D FT of $f(x,y)$ at $(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$.
6. State the final result: $S_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> - Forgetting to state that the Jacobian of a 2-D rotation is **unity** (a 1-mark deduction).
> - Skipping the change-of-variables step and jumping to the result (loses 2–3 marks).
> - Not specifying that the slice passes through the **origin** of k-space.
> - Confusing $\omega$ (spatial frequency) with $\theta$ (angle).

**(b) Direct Fourier Reconstruction (7 Marks):**

- Acquire $M$ parallel-beam projections $P_{\theta_m}(r)$ at angles $\theta_m = m\Delta\theta$, $m = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$.
- Compute 1-D FFT of each projection: $S_{\theta_m}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}_1\{P_{\theta_m}(r)\}$.
- For each $m$, populate the 2-D Cartesian grid at the radial line $(\omega_x, \omega_y) = (\omega\cos\theta_m, \omega\sin\theta_m)$.
- Apply 2-D interpolation (nearest-neighbor / bilinear / gridding) to obtain $F(\omega_x, \omega_y)$ on a regular grid.
- Apply 2-D Inverse FFT to obtain $f(x,y)$.

**Limitations:**
- Radial sampling causes **uneven density** in k-space — high frequencies near the periphery are under-sampled.
- Interpolation introduces **smoothing** and **aliasing**.
- Requires uniformly spaced angular sampling.

**Comparison with Filtered Back-Projection (FBP):**
- FBP works in the **spatial domain** by convolving projections with a ramp filter before back-projecting, avoiding 2-D interpolation.
- FBP is computationally faster and clinically preferred.
- DFR is conceptually elegant but rarely used directly in commercial scanners due to interpolation artifacts.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *Explain the Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART). Formulate the linear system and derive the ART pixel-update equation. Illustrate with a 2×2 pixel numerical example.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the SIRT algorithm. Compare its convergence behavior, noise immunity, and computational cost with ART. Discuss when SIRT is preferred in modern CT reconstruction.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) ART Explanation + Derivation (7 Marks):**

- Discretize the image into $N$ pixels $x_j$ with attenuation values.
- For each of $M$ projection rays indexed by $i$, the measured ray-sum $b_i$ equals $\sum_{j=1}^{N} a_{ij} x_j$, where $a_{ij}$ is the intersection length of ray $i$ with pixel $j$.
- Write the linear system: $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$.
- **Kaczmarz ART update** for the $k$-th iteration, processing ray $i$:

$$
x_j^{(k+1)} = x_j^{(k)} + \lambda\, \frac{b_i - \sum_{m} a_{im}\, x_m^{(k)}}{\sum_{m} a_{im}^2}\, a_{ij} \quad \forall j
$$

- Show a numerical 2×2 example (refer to **Section 3.3**).

**(b) SIRT Algorithm + Comparison (7 Marks):**

- **SIRT update:** Pixels are updated **after all rays** are processed, by averaging contributions across rays.
- Formula:

$$
x_j^{(k+1)} = x_j^{(k)} + \lambda\, \frac{1}{\sum_i a_{ij}}\sum_i \left[ a_{ij}\, \frac{b_i - \sum_m a_{im}\, x_m^{(k)}}{\sum_m a_{im}^2} \right]
$$

- **Convergence:** Slower than ART but smoother (monotonic error reduction).
- **Noise immunity:** Superior to ART because each pixel's update considers *all* projection residuals, not just one.
- **Computational cost:** Higher per iteration due to accumulation over all rays before update.
- **When preferred:** Low-dose CT, sparse-view CT, PET/SPECT reconstruction, and any situation where measurement noise dominates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> - Confusing **ART** with **SIRT** update formulas (they look similar but the placement of the summation differs).
> - Forgetting to mention that the relaxation parameter $\lambda$ controls stability ($\lambda = 1$ is Kaczmarz; $\lambda < 1$ gives under-relaxation).
> - Not drawing a clear block diagram showing the iteration loop (a 1-mark deduction if missing).
> - For ART, skipping the explanation of $a_{ij}$ as the ray-pixel intersection length.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 2D Image Reconstruction**

- ✅ The **Fourier Slice Theorem** links each parallel-beam projection to a radial slice through the 2-D k-space of the object.
- ✅ **Direct Fourier Reconstruction**: 1-D FFT per projection → 2-D interpolation → 2-D Inverse FFT.
- ✅ The Jacobian of a 2-D rotation is **unity**, which simplifies the FST derivation.
- ✅ **ART (Kaczmarz)**: updates pixels after every single ray; fast but noisy.
- ✅ **SIRT**: updates pixels after all rays; slower but more stable and noise-robust.
- ✅ The matrix equation $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ has $a_{ij}$ = intersection length of ray $i$ with pixel $j$.
- ✅ Relaxation parameter $\lambda \in (0, 1]$ — controls step size; $\lambda = 1$ is standard.
- ✅ Convergence is monitored using the relative residual norm $\Vert \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}^{(k)} \Vert_2 / \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert_2$.
- ✅ Modern clinical CT uses **MBIR (Model-Based Iterative Reconstruction)** which extends SIRT with statistical noise models.
- ✅ The Fourier Slice Theorem is the theoretical foundation of **Filtered Back-Projection** (FBP), the workhorse of clinical CT.
- ✅ For sparse data / low-dose CT, iterative methods outperform Fourier methods in noise-immunity but cost more compute time.
- ✅ KTU 14-mark questions expect a **derivation + numerical example + block diagram** structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Nuclear Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT503 - MEDICAL IMAGING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Nuclear Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Imaging** is a branch of medical imaging that utilizes **ionizing radiation emitted from radioactive tracers (radiopharmaceuticals) administered internally** to visualize, characterize, and quantify physiological, metabolic, and molecular processes within the human body, *in contrast to conventional radiological imaging which primarily depicts anatomical structure.*

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, nuclear imaging encompasses two principal modalities:

1. **SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography)** — using single gamma-ray-emitting radionuclides.
2. **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)** — using positron-emitting radionuclides and coincidence detection of annihilation photons.

The fundamental physical chain in nuclear imaging is:

$$\text{Radionuclide} \longrightarrow \text{Radiopharmaceutical} \longrightarrow \text{In-vivo biodistribution} \longrightarrow \text{Emitted Photons} \longrightarrow \text{Detector} \longrightarrow \text{Image}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Glowing Breadcrumb" Analogy**
> Imagine a patient as a large dark forest at night, and a disease (e.g., a tumor) as a hidden animal. You cannot see the animal through the trees (anatomical imaging gives only structure, not function). Now, you release thousands of tiny fireflies (radioactive tracers) that are chemically designed to be attracted *only* to the tumor cells. The fireflies accumulate at the tumor and glow. A sensitive camera (gamma camera / PET scanner) detects the glow from outside the body, and from the pattern of light, a map of the disease is created. **You don't see the tumor directly — you see the *light* coming from the tracers that have found the tumor.**

This is the essence of **functional/molecular imaging** — it images *biology and chemistry*, not just anatomy.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed of light ($c$)** | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ | Governs photon propagation |
| **Planck's constant ($h$)** | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}}$ | Quantum energy of photons |
| **Planck's relation** | $\mathbf{E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}}$ | Energy of emitted gamma photon |
| **Avogadro's number ($N_A$)** | $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}}$ | Atoms per mole of tracer |
| **Typical PET photon energy** | **511 keV** (annihilation) | Energy of detected photons in PET |
| **Typical SPECT photon energy** | **140 keV** (Tc-99m) | Energy of detected photons in SPECT |
| **Half-life of Tc-99m** | **6.01 hours** | Most widely used medical isotope |

## 1.4 Why Nuclear Imaging Matters in Modern Medicine

- **Early Disease Detection**: Metabolic changes precede anatomical changes by months.
- **Oncology**: Tumor staging, treatment response monitoring.
- **Cardiology**: Myocardial perfusion, viability assessment.
- **Neurology**: Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, epilepsy focus localization.
- **Bone Scintigraphy**: Detection of metastases, fractures, infection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Physics of Nuclear Imaging

### 2.1.1 Radioactive Decay
A radioactive nucleus is unstable. It transitions to a more stable state by emitting particles or photons. The three modes relevant to medical imaging are:

1. **Gamma Decay ($\gamma$)**: A nucleus in an excited state drops to a lower energy state, emitting a high-energy photon. The nucleus itself does not change (no change in $Z$ or $A$). *This is the basis of SPECT.*
2. **Beta-minus Decay ($\beta^-$)**: A neutron converts to a proton, emitting an electron and antineutrino. The atom becomes a different element ($Z$ increases by 1). *I-131, used in thyroid therapy, decays this way.*
3. **Beta-plus Decay ($\beta^+$)** (Positron Emission): A proton converts to a neutron, emitting a positron and neutrino. The atom becomes a different element ($Z$ decreases by 1). *This is the basis of PET. F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15 are positron emitters.*

### 2.1.2 Decay Law and Activity
The number of radioactive atoms $N(t)$ at time $t$ follows the exponential decay law:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (probability per unit time of a single atom decaying).

The number of remaining atoms at time $t$ is:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **activity** $A(t)$ is the number of decays per second (measured in Becquerel, Bq):

$$A(t) = \lambda N(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

The **half-life** $T_{1/2}$ is the time for activity to halve:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

The **mean lifetime** $\tau$ is:

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{\ln 2} \approx 1.4427 \, T_{1/2}$$

## 2.2 The Why and How of Each Step in the Imaging Chain

| Step | "Why" | "How" |
|---|---|---|
| **Radionuclide Production** | Need a tracer with appropriate half-life and emission energy | Reactor (Tc-99m from Mo-99), Cyclotron (F-18), Generator (Tc-99m from Mo-99/Tc-99m generator) |
| **Radiopharmaceutical Synthesis** | Tracer must localize in target tissue | Attach radionuclide to biologically active molecule (e.g., FDG for glucose metabolism) |
| **Administration & Uptake** | Tracer must reach target organ | IV injection, inhalation, or oral ingestion |
| **Photon Emission & Detection** | Photons must escape body and reach detector | Gamma rays (140-511 keV) have moderate penetration |
| **Image Formation** | Convert photon detections into spatial map | Collimation (SPECT), electronic collimation via coincidence (PET) |
| **Reconstruction** | Convert 2D projections into 3D volume | Filtered back-projection, iterative reconstruction (OSEM) |

## 2.3 Interaction of Photons with Matter (Critical for Detector Design)

The three dominant interactions in the 50–600 keV medical energy range are:

1. **Photoelectric Effect**: Photon is fully absorbed, ejecting a bound electron. Dominates at **lower energies** and in **high-Z detectors**. Probability $\propto \frac{Z^4}{E^3}$.
2. **Compton Scattering**: Photon scatters off a free electron, losing energy and changing direction. Dominates at **intermediate energies**. Probability $\propto \frac{Z}{E}$.
3. **Pair Production**: Photon converts to electron-positron pair. Requires $E \geq 1.022$ MeV (2 × rest mass of electron). Not relevant in routine nuclear imaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **photoelectric effect** is preferred for imaging because the photon's *full* energy is deposited in the detector, giving precise energy information. **Compton scattering** degrades image quality because the scattered photon has wrong energy and direction — this is why energy windowing (e.g., 140 keV ± 10%) is used to reject scatter.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for KTU 2024 ESE. Master these.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay of atoms | dimensionless count |
| 2 | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Activity decay over time | Becquerel (Bq) or Curie (Ci) |
| 3 | $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ | Decay constant from half-life | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (or eV) |
| 5 | $E_{\text{ann}} = 2m_e c^2 = 1.022 \text{ MeV}$ | Annihilation photon total energy | MeV |
| 6 | $E_{\gamma,\text{each}} = 511 \text{ keV}$ | Each annihilation photon's energy | keV |
| 7 | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Curie to Becquerel | Ci ↔ Bq |
| 8 | $\sigma_{PE} \propto \frac{Z^4}{E^3}$ | Photoelectric cross-section | cm²/atom |
| 9 | $\sigma_C \propto \frac{Z}{E}$ | Compton cross-section | cm²/electron |
| 10 | $R = \frac{N_{\text{true}} + N_{\text{random}}}{N_{\text{true}}}$ | Random coincidence rate factor | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{SNR} \propto \sqrt{N}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio in count-limited imaging | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Resolution}_{sys} \approx \sqrt{R_d^2 + R_c^2}$ | System resolution (intrinsic + collimator) | mm |
| 13 | $\text{Attenuation correction factor} = e^{-\int \mu(x) dx}$ | PET attenuation (line integral) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $A_{\text{calibrated}} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t_{\text{cal}}}$ | Dose calibration adjustment | MBq |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cyclotron Design**: Nuclear imaging PET relies on cyclotron-produced isotopes (F-18 has 110-min half-life, requiring a nearby cyclotron).
- **Detector Materials Engineering**: Choice of scintillator (NaI(Tl), BGO, LSO, LYSO) affects stopping power, decay time, and thus count-rate capability.
- **Collimator Engineering**: For SPECT, collimator hole geometry (parallel-hole, fan-beam, pinhole) determines resolution-sensitivity trade-off.
- **Image Reconstruction Algorithms**: Iterative methods (OSEM, MLEM) outperform analytical methods (FBP) at low counts, critical in nuclear imaging where counts are limited.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From First-Principles Decay Law to Half-Life

We begin with the rate equation governing radioactive decay. We then systematically derive the half-life formula and solve a typical KTU numerical problem.

### Step 1: Governing Differential Equation

Consider a sample containing $N(t)$ unstable nuclei at time $t$. The probability that any *one* nucleus decays in a small time interval $dt$ is $\lambda \, dt$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant. Since the nuclei decay independently, the expected number decaying in $dt$ is:

$$dN = -\lambda N \, dt$$

The negative sign indicates that $N$ decreases with time. This is the **fundamental decay law**.

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Rearranging the equation to separate $N$ and $t$:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

### Step 3: Integration

Integrating both sides from the initial time $0$ (with $N(0) = N_0$) to a later time $t$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

The left side gives a natural logarithm; the right side is a simple linear integral:

$$\ln N(t) - \ln N_0 = -\lambda t$$

### Step 4: Exponentiation

Exponentiating both sides:

$$e^{\ln N(t) - \ln N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

### Step 5: Activity Function

Since activity $A(t) = \lambda N(t)$ and $A_0 = \lambda N_0$, multiplying both sides by $\lambda$:

$$\boxed{A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

### Step 6: Definition of Half-Life

The half-life $T_{1/2}$ is defined as the time at which $A(t) = \frac{A_0}{2}$, i.e., the activity has reduced to one-half of its initial value.

Substituting into the activity equation:

$$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

### Step 7: Solving for $T_{1/2}$

Divide both sides by $A_0$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

Recall that $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$:

$$-\ln 2 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

Multiply by $-1$:

$$\ln 2 = \lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.693}{\lambda}}$$

### Step 8: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A patient is administered 740 MBq of Tc-99m for a bone scan. The half-life of Tc-99m is 6.01 hours. What is the activity remaining in the patient 18 hours after injection?

**Given**:
- Initial activity $A_0 = 740$ MBq
- Half-life $T_{1/2} = 6.01$ h
- Elapsed time $t = 18$ h

**Step 8.1**: Find the number of half-lives elapsed:

$$n = \frac{t}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{18 \text{ h}}{6.01 \text{ h}} = 2.995$$

**Step 8.2**: Apply the discrete half-life form (since 18 h is approximately 3 half-lives):

$$A(t) = \frac{A_0}{2^n} = \frac{740}{2^{2.995}} \text{ MBq}$$

**Step 8.3**: Compute $2^{2.995}$:

$$2^{2.995} = 2^3 \times 2^{-0.005} = 8 \times 0.9965 \approx 7.972$$

**Step 8.4**: Final activity:

$$A(18 \text{ h}) = \frac{740}{7.972} \approx 92.83 \text{ MBq}$$

**Answer**: $\boxed{A \approx 92.8 \text{ MBq}}$ — this is why Tc-99m with its 6-h half-life is so practical; significant activity remains even after a day.

## 3.2 Derivation: Photon Energy of Tc-99m Gamma Emission

The gamma ray emitted by Tc-99m has a wavelength of approximately $8.87 \times 10^{-12}$ m. Find its energy in keV.

**Step 1**: Use Planck's relation:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\lambda = 8.87 \times 10^{-12}$ m:

$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{8.87 \times 10^{-12}} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3**: Numerator:

$$hc = 6.626 \times 3 \times 10^{-26} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$

**Step 4**: Divide:

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{8.87 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.2241 \times 10^{-13} = 2.241 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5**: Convert to electron-volts using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J:

$$E = \frac{2.241 \times 10^{-14}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} = 1.399 \times 10^5 \text{ eV} = 139.9 \text{ keV}$$

**Answer**: $\boxed{E \approx 140 \text{ keV}}$ — matches the standard Tc-99m emission line.

## 3.3 Derivation: Photon Energy of PET Annihilation Photons

When a positron (emitted by F-18, for example) annihilates with an electron, two 511 keV photons are produced at 180° to each other (to conserve momentum).

**Step 1**: The total rest-mass energy of the electron-positron pair:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2 m_e c^2$$

**Step 2**: Electron rest mass $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2 \times (9.109 \times 10^{-31}) \times (3 \times 10^8)^2 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3**: Compute $c^2 = 9 \times 10^{16}$ m²/s²:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{16}$$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 18.218 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{16} = 163.96 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4**: Convert to MeV:

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{163.96 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-13}} \text{ MeV} = 1.0235 \text{ MeV} \approx 1.022 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 5**: By conservation of energy and momentum (assuming both photons emitted at 180° and approximately at rest before annihilation):

$$E_{\text{each photon}} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{2} = \frac{1.022}{2} = 0.511 \text{ MeV} = 511 \text{ keV}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{annihilation}} = 511 \text{ keV per photon}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **511 keV** energy is the *signature* of positron annihilation and is the cornerstone of PET imaging. PET detectors are tuned to detect this specific energy using energy windows.

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Atoms in a Typical Radiopharmaceutical Dose

A Tc-99m dose has activity 740 MBq and half-life 6.01 h. How many Tc-99m atoms are in the syringe at the time of injection?

**Step 1**: Convert half-life to seconds:

$$T_{1/2} = 6.01 \times 3600 = 21636 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2**: Compute decay constant:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{21636} = 3.203 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3**: Use $A = \lambda N$, so $N = \frac{A}{\lambda}$:

$$N = \frac{740 \times 10^6}{3.203 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$N = 2.31 \times 10^{13} \text{ atoms}$$

**Answer**: $\boxed{N \approx 2.3 \times 10^{13} \text{ atoms}}$ — a surprisingly small number of atoms, illustrating the extreme sensitivity of nuclear imaging detection.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Gamma Camera (SPECT)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the operational flow of a single-head gamma camera used in SPECT, since physical hardware diagrams are complex to render.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient with Radiopharmaceutical] --> B[Gamma Photons Emitted 140 keV]
    B --> C[Collimator Pb Holes Parallel Hex]
    C --> D[Scintillator Crystal NaI Tl 10 mm]
    D --> E[Visible Light Photons ~3 eV]
    E --> F[Photomultiplier Tube Array PMT 37 to 91]
    F --> G[Position Computer Logic]
    F --> H[Energy Computer Logic]
    G --> I[X Y Position Map]
    H --> J[Energy Window 140 keV ± 10 percent]
    J --> K{Within Window}
    K -->|Yes| L[Count Accepted]
    K -->|No| M[Count Rejected as Scatter]
    I --> L
    L --> N[Digital Image Matrix 64x64 to 512x512]
    N --> O[Display and Storage]
```

## 4.2 SPECT vs PET — Functional Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP[SPECT System]
        S1[Patient with Tc 99m] --> S2[Gamma Photon 140 keV]
        S2 --> S3[Lead Collimator Physical]
        S3 --> S4[NaI Crystal]
        S4 --> S5[PMT Array]
        S5 --> S6[2D Projection Image]
    end

    subgraph PP[PET System]
        P1[Patient with F 18 FDG] --> P2[Positron Emission]
        P2 --> P3[Annihilation 511 keV Photons Opposite 180 deg]
        P3 --> P4[Ring of Detectors No Physical Collimator]
        P4 --> P5[Coincidence Timing < 10 ns]
        P5 --> P6[Line of Response LOR]
    end

    S6 --> OUT[3D Reconstruction]
    P6 --> OUT
    OUT --> REC[Filtered Back Projection or OSEM]
    REC --> FINAL[Functional Image]
```

## 4.3 Radiopharmaceutical Localization Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RP[Radiopharmaceutical Administration] --> M1{Mechanism of Uptake}
    M1 -->|Metabolic Trapping| T1[FDG Phosphorylated by HK Trapped in Cell]
    M1 -->|Receptor Binding| T2[I 123 Ioflupane Binds Dopamine Receptors]
    M1 -->|Perfusion| T3[Tc 99m MAA Trapped in Lung Capillaries]
    M1 -->|Active Transport| T4[I 123 or I 131 Concentrated by Thyroid]
    M1 -->|Phagocytosis| T5[Tc 99m Sulfur Colloid Liver Spleen]
    M1 -->|Diffusion| T6[Xe 133 Inhaled Gas Brain Blood]
    T1 --> IMG[Emitted Photons Detected by Camera]
    T2 --> IMG
    T3 --> IMG
    T4 --> IMG
    T5 --> IMG
    T6 --> IMG
```

## 4.4 PET Coincidence Detection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Detector A] --> C[Coincidence Processor Window 10 ns]
    A2[Detector B] --> C
    A3[Detector C] --> C
    A4[Detector D] --> C
    C --> Q1{Detection within 10 ns}
    Q1 -->|No| X1[Single Event Discarded]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Energy 511 keV ± 20 percent}
    Q2 -->|No| X2[Scatter Rejected]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{True or Random}
    Q3 -->|True Coincidence| Y1[Valid LOR for Image]
    Q3 -->|Random Coincidence| Y2[Estimated and Subtracted]
    Y1 --> REC[Image Reconstruction with Attenuation Correction]
    Y2 --> REC
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define radiopharmaceutical. List any four radioisotopes used in nuclear medicine imaging along with their clinical applications.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Radiopharmaceutical**: A radiopharmaceutical is a pharmaceutical compound containing a radioactive isotope (radionuclide) that is used for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. The radionuclide component provides the radiation signal, while the pharmaceutical component determines the *in-vivo* biodistribution (i.e., where in the body it localizes).

**Four commonly used radioisotopes in nuclear imaging:**

| Radioisotope | Half-life | Emission | Clinical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tc-99m** | 6.01 h | Gamma (140 keV) | Bone scans, myocardial perfusion, thyroid |
| **I-131** | 8.02 days | Beta + Gamma (364 keV) | Thyroid imaging and therapy |
| **F-18 (as FDG)** | 110 min | Positron (511 keV) | PET oncology, neurology, cardiology |
| **Tl-201** | 73 h | Gamma (X-rays ~70–80 keV) | Myocardial perfusion imaging |

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between SPECT and PET imaging systems based on radionuclide, photon energy, detection principle, and sensitivity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SPECT | PET |
|---|---|---|
| **Radionuclide type** | Single-photon gamma emitters (e.g., Tc-99m) | Positron emitters (e.g., F-18, C-11) |
| **Photon energy detected** | 140 keV (Tc-99m) | 511 keV (annihilation photons) |
| **Detection principle** | Mechanical collimation using lead collimator | Electronic collimation via coincidence detection |
| **Number of photons per decay** | 1 | 2 (at 180°) |
| **Sensitivity** | Lower (~10⁻⁴ counts/disintegration) | Higher (~10⁻² counts/disintegration) |
| **Quantification** | Semi-quantitative (SUV less reliable) | Truly quantitative (SUV routinely used) |
| **Spatial resolution** | ~8–10 mm | ~4–6 mm |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (requires cyclotron for F-18) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of nuclear imaging with a neat block diagram of a gamma camera. Discuss the role of the collimator, scintillation crystal, and photomultiplier tube. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient is administered 800 MBq of Tc-99m for myocardial perfusion imaging. After 4 hours, what activity remains? Given the half-life of Tc-99m is 6 hours. Also calculate the number of Tc-99m atoms present in the original dose. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the principle of Positron Emission Tomography (PET). Explain the process of positron emission, annihilation, and coincidence detection. List any three PET radionuclides with their half-lives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the terms *activity*, *half-life*, and *decay constant*. Derive the relationship between them. A 100 MBq sample of I-131 (half-life 8 days) is shipped from a manufacturer. After 16 days of travel, what is the activity on arrival? **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Gamma Camera Principle

The **gamma camera** (also called the Anger camera, invented by Hal Anger in 1958) is the principal detection instrument in SPECT. Its operation can be broken into the following components:

**1. Collimator:**
- A 4–5 cm thick lead plate with thousands of parallel holes.
- Its function is to **mechanically select** the direction of incoming gamma photons.
- Only photons traveling nearly perpendicular to the detector face pass through; oblique photons are absorbed by the lead septa.
- It defines the **geometric field of view** and is the major resolution-limiting element (typical spatial resolution ~8–10 mm).

**2. Scintillation Crystal (NaI(Tl)):**
- A large thallium-doped sodium iodide crystal (~40–50 cm diameter, ~1 cm thick).
- When a gamma photon strikes the crystal, it interacts via photoelectric or Compton effect, depositing energy.
- The energy is converted into **visible light photons** (~3 eV each, ~4,000 photons per 140 keV gamma).
- The high atomic number of iodine (Z = 53) ensures good stopping power at 140 keV.

**3. Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) Array:**
- Typically 37 to 91 PMTs are arranged in a hexagonal array behind the crystal.
- The visible light photons strike the photocathode, ejecting electrons via the photoelectric effect.
- These electrons are accelerated through a **dynode chain** (typically 10 stages), with each stage multiplying the electron count by a factor of ~4–6, giving a total gain of ~10⁶.
- The output is a measurable electrical pulse proportional to the deposited energy.

**4. Position and Energy Computers:**
- The position computer uses the relative signals from neighboring PMTs to compute the (x, y) interaction location via Anger position logic.
- The energy computer sums all PMT signals to determine the total energy deposited.
- An **energy window** (e.g., 140 keV ± 10%, i.e., 126–154 keV) is applied to reject scattered photons.

**5. Display:**
- The (x, y) location and energy-validated count are stored in a digital image matrix (typically 64×64 or 128×128) and displayed on a monitor.

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram in Section 4.1 illustrates this complete signal flow.

#### Part (b) — Decay Calculation

**Given:**
- $A_0 = 800$ MBq
- $T_{1/2} = 6$ h
- $t = 4$ h

**Step 1: Compute decay constant:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{6} = 0.1155 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Compute remaining activity:**

$$A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} = 800 \times e^{-0.1155 \times 4}$$

$$A(t) = 800 \times e^{-0.462} = 800 \times 0.6297 = 503.7 \text{ MBq}$$

**Answer: $\boxed{A(4 \text{ h}) \approx 503.7 \text{ MBq}}$**

**Step 3: Number of atoms in original dose:**

Convert $\lambda$ to s⁻¹:

$$\lambda = 0.1155 \text{ h}^{-1} = \frac{0.1155}{3600} = 3.208 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Use $A_0 = \lambda N_0$:**

$$N_0 = \frac{A_0}{\lambda} = \frac{800 \times 10^6}{3.208 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$N_0 = 2.494 \times 10^{13} \text{ atoms}$$

**Answer: $\boxed{N_0 \approx 2.49 \times 10^{13} \text{ atoms}}$**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — PET Principle

**Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** is a nuclear imaging technique based on the detection of pairs of **511 keV annihilation photons** produced when a positron emitted by a radiotracer annihilates with a nearby electron in tissue.

**Step 1 — Positron Emission:**
- A proton-rich radionuclide (e.g., F-18) decays by converting a proton to a neutron, emitting a **positron** ($\beta^+$) and a neutrino:

$$p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e$$

**Step 2 — Annihilation:**
- The emitted positron travels a short distance (~1–2 mm in tissue) — this distance is called the **positron range** and limits spatial resolution.
- It then encounters an electron, and the pair annihilates, producing **two 511 keV photons emitted in opposite directions (180° apart)** to conserve momentum:

$$e^+ + e^- \rightarrow 2\gamma \quad (E_\gamma = 511 \text{ keV each})$$

**Step 3 — Coincidence Detection:**
- A ring of detectors surrounding the patient registers both photons *simultaneously* (within a coincidence timing window of typically 6–12 nanoseconds).
- Each simultaneous detection pair defines a **Line of Response (LOR)**.
- No physical collimator is needed — the geometry itself provides **electronic collimation**.

**Step 4 — Image Reconstruction:**
- Millions of LORs are collected and reconstructed into a 3D image using algorithms like Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) or iterative methods (OSEM).
- **Attenuation correction** is applied using a transmission scan (with a Ge-68 rod source) or CT-based attenuation correction in PET/CT scanners.

**Three PET radionuclides:**

| Radionuclide | Half-life | Pharmaceutical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **F-18** | 110 min | FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) — oncology, neurology |
| **C-11** | 20.4 min | C-11 raclopride — dopamine receptor imaging |
| **O-15** | 2.04 min | O-15 water — cerebral blood flow |
| **N-13** | 9.97 min | N-13 ammonia — myocardial perfusion |

#### Part (b) — Definitions and Decay Problem

**Definitions:**

- **Activity (A)**: The number of radioactive decays per unit time. SI unit: Becquerel (Bq) = 1 decay/second. Older unit: Curie (Ci) = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq.
- **Half-life ($T_{1/2}$)**: The time required for the activity of a radioactive sample to decrease to half of its initial value.
- **Decay constant ($\lambda$)**: The probability per unit time that a single radioactive nucleus will decay. SI unit: s⁻¹.

**Derivation of the relationship:** See Section 3.1, Steps 1–7. Final relationship:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$$

**Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- $A_0 = 100$ MBq
- $T_{1/2} = 8$ days
- $t = 16$ days

**Step 1: Number of half-lives elapsed:**

$$n = \frac{16}{8} = 2$$

**Step 2: Apply the discrete half-life formula:**

$$A(t) = \frac{A_0}{2^n} = \frac{100}{2^2} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ MBq}$$

**Answer: $\boxed{A(16 \text{ days}) = 25 \text{ MBq}}$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency**: Students frequently mix up hours and seconds when computing $\lambda$. Always convert $T_{1/2}$ to seconds if you want $\lambda$ in s⁻¹, *especially* when computing the number of atoms $N$ (since activity is in Bq, which is decays per *second*).
> 2. **In PET questions**, students often forget that the positron has a *range* in tissue (~1–2 mm) before annihilation. This range fundamentally limits PET resolution and is a favorite KTU question.
> 3. **Co-60 vs Tc-99m confusion**: For half-life calculations, do not mix up isotope data. Always re-state $T_{1/2}$ explicitly from the question.
> 4. **Activity vs. dose**: Activity is in Bq (decays/second); absorbed dose is in Gray (J/kg). They are *not* the same.
> 5. **Annihilation photon energy**: Many students write "1.022 MeV" as the photon energy, but this is the *total* energy of the pair. The energy of *each* photon is **511 keV**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Nuclear imaging** is a *functional* imaging modality that detects photons emitted from internal radiotracers, unlike X-ray/CT which is *anatomical*.
- [x] The **decay law** $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and the **half-life relation** $T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$ are the two most-tested equations.
- [x] **SPECT** uses gamma emitters (Tc-99m, 140 keV) and a **physical lead collimator**; sensitivity is lower.
- [x] **PET** uses positron emitters (F-18) and **electronic coincidence collimation** of 511 keV annihilation photons; sensitivity is higher, quantification is superior.
- [x] The **511 keV annihilation photon** energy comes from $E = m_e c^2$ for each photon; the *total* pair energy is 1.022 MeV.
- [x] The **positron range** (1–2 mm) limits PET spatial resolution.
- [x] The **gamma camera** has five functional blocks: collimator, scintillator (NaI(Tl)), PMT array, position/energy computers, and display.
- [x] **Energy windowing** (~140 keV ± 10% for Tc-99m, 511 keV ± 20% for PET) is essential to reject Compton-scattered photons.
- [x] **Activity units**: 1 Ci = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq; clinical doses are typically 100–1000 MBq.
- [x] The **photoelectric effect** dominates at low energies in high-Z materials; **Compton scattering** dominates at intermediate energies.
- [x] **Tc-99m** is the workhorse of nuclear medicine (ideal 6-h half-life, ideal 140 keV photon, available from Mo-99/Tc-99m generators).
- [x] **FDG (Fluorodeoxyglucose)** is the workhorse PET tracer; it accumulates in cells with high glucose metabolism (tumors, brain, inflammation).
- [x] **Coincidence timing window** in PET is typically 6–12 ns — this defines true vs. random coincidences.
- [x] **Image reconstruction** methods: FBP (analytical) and OSEM/MLEM (iterative) — iterative is preferred at low count densities.
- [x] **Attenuation correction** is mandatory in PET for accurate quantification (SUV — Standardized Uptake Value).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Radiation Detector?

A **radiation detector** is a device or instrument that converts incident ionizing radiation (X-rays, gamma rays, beta particles, neutrons, or charged particles) into a measurable, quantifiable electrical or optical signal that can be processed, displayed, and analyzed. In medical imaging, these detectors form the **front-end sensing element** that converts the radiation pattern exiting the patient into a diagnostic image or a numerical dose measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBMT503, Module 4):** Radiation detectors are transducers that interact with ionizing radiation and produce a measurable response (electrical pulse, light photon, or chemical change) proportional to the energy, intensity, or spatial distribution of the incident radiation.

In medical imaging systems, detectors are positioned **after the patient** along the beam path (for transmission imaging like X-ray, CT) or **around the patient** (for emission imaging like gamma camera, PET). Their job is to faithfully record the spatial, temporal, and energetic information carried by the photons/particles.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rain Gauge of the Atomic World"

Imagine standing in an open field during a rainstorm. You want to know:

1. **How hard is it raining?** → Intensity measurement.
2. **Where are the raindrops falling?** → Spatial/position measurement.
3. **How big are the raindrops?** → Energy measurement.
4. **How much total rain has fallen over time?** → Cumulative dose measurement.

A **radiation detector does exactly this for ionizing radiation**:

- A **Geiger-Müller (GM) counter** is like a simple rain bucket — it just counts individual drops (photons) but doesn't tell you their size.
- A **scintillation detector with a PMT** is like a sophisticated rain gauge with a microphone — it counts drops AND estimates their size (energy) by the loudness of the splash.
- A **flat-panel X-ray detector** is like a giant 2D array of rain gauges — it tells you the spatial pattern of rain across an entire field, generating an **image** of the rainstorm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Every detector in medical imaging relies on one fundamental principle — the radiation deposits energy in a sensitive medium, and that energy is then converted into a measurable signal (electrons, light, or chemical change).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Radiation Detectors Used in Medical Imaging

Radiation detectors are classified based on the **sensitive medium** and the **physical mechanism** used to convert radiation into a signal.

| Class | Detector Type | Sensitive Medium | Signal Type |
|:------|:--------------|:-----------------|:------------|
| **Gas-Filled** | Ionization Chamber | Gas (Air, Ar) | DC Current |
| **Gas-Filled** | Proportional Counter | Gas (Ar + CH₄) | Proportional Pulse |
| **Gas-Filled** | Geiger-Müller (GM) Tube | Gas (Ar + Halogen) | Saturated Pulse |
| **Scintillation** | NaI(Tl) Crystal + PMT | Crystal | Light → Electrical |
| **Scintillation** | CsI(Tl) for Flat-Panel | Crystal | Light → Electrical |
| **Semiconductor** | CdZnTe, Si, Ge | Solid Crystal | Electron-Hole Pairs |
| **Storage** | Film Badge | Emulsion | Latent Image |
| **Storage** | Thermoluminescent Dosimeter (TLD) | LiF:Mg,Ti | Trapped Electrons |
| **Storage** | Optically Stimulated Luminescence (OSLD) | Al₂O₃:C | Trapped Electrons |
| **Modern Digital** | Flat-Panel Detector (a-Si + CsI) | Solid + Scintillator | Digital Pixel Data |
| **Modern Digital** | CCD / CMOS Sensor | Silicon | Digital Pixel Data |
| **Image Intensifier** | II Tube | CsI + Phosphor | Bright Optical Image |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> These constants and units appear repeatedly in KTU numerical problems.

- **Average energy to create one ion pair in air (W-value):** **W = 33.97 eV/ion pair ≈ 34 eV/ion pair**
- **Density of air at STP:** **ρ_air = 1.293 kg/m³**
- **1 Roentgen (R):** Exposure that produces **2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg** of charge in air.
- **Specific charge of electron:** **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Photons per Roentgen per cm³ of air:** **N = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg ÷ 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C = 1.61 × 10¹⁵ ion pairs/kg**
- **Planck's constant:** **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Conversion factor:** **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**

The figure **33.97 eV/ion pair** is critical — it is the average energy required to produce one electron-ion pair in air. This means an **X-ray photon of 50 keV** deposits enough energy to produce approximately:

$$N_{ion} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ eV}}{33.97 \text{ eV/ion pair}} \approx 1472 \text{ ion pairs}$$

This number of charge carriers is small but measurable with sensitive electrometers — the foundation of all gas-filled dosimetry.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Operating Regions of a Gas-Filled Detector

A cylindrical gas-filled detector consists of a **central anode wire** (small diameter, positive potential) surrounded by a **cylindrical cathode wall** (negative potential), filled with a suitable gas. When ionizing radiation enters, it creates ion pairs. Depending on the **applied voltage V**, the detector behaves very differently.

The relationship between the **applied voltage** and the **charge collected at the anode** is shown in the classic **operating characteristic curve**:

```
Q (collected)
│
│                          ┌───── Region V: GM Region (Geiger Discharge)
│                         ╱
│                        ╱
│                      ╱  ──── Region IV: Limited Proportionality
│                    ╱
│                  ╱  ──────── Region III: Proportional Region
│                ╱
│             ╱  ───────────── Region II: Ionization (Saturation) Region
│          ╱
│       ╱  ─────────────────── Region I: Recombination Region
│    ╱
└─────────────────────────────────────────── V (applied voltage)
```

### 2.1.1 Region I — Recombination Region
- **Voltage:** Low (below threshold V₁).
- **Behavior:** Ions and electrons recombine before reaching electrodes.
- **Signal:** Small, non-linear, **NOT useful** for measurement.
- **Field strength:** Too weak to separate ion pairs efficiently.

### 2.1.2 Region II — Ionization (Saturation) Region
- **Voltage:** Moderate (V₁ to V₂, typically **100–300 V**).
- **Behavior:** Electric field is strong enough to collect **ALL** primary ion pairs created by the radiation, but **NOT strong enough to cause secondary ionization (gas multiplication)**.
- **Signal:** **DC current proportional to exposure rate** (for DC mode) or **pulse proportional to energy deposited** (for pulse mode).
- **Device:** **Ionization Chamber** operates here.
- **Key property:** The collected charge equals the original ion pairs created:

$$Q_{collected} = N_{ion} \cdot e = \frac{E_{deposited}}{W/e}$$

where W/e = 33.97 V (the effective ionization potential per ion pair).

### 2.1.3 Region III — Proportional Region
- **Voltage:** Higher (V₂ to V₃, typically **300–800 V**).
- **Behavior:** Electric field near the thin anode wire becomes intense enough to **accelerate primary electrons** to kinetic energies exceeding the ionization energy of the gas. These primary electrons cause **secondary ionizations (Townsend avalanches)**.
- **Gas multiplication factor M:** Typically **10² to 10⁴**.
- **Signal:** Pulse amplitude **proportional to the original energy deposited** — hence the name "proportional." A 100 keV photon gives 10× the pulse of a 10 keV photon.
- **Device:** **Proportional Counter** operates here.
- **Townsend multiplication formula:**

$$M = \exp\left(\int_{a}^{b} \alpha(x) \, dx\right)$$

where **α(x)** is the **Townsend first ionization coefficient** (number of ion pairs produced per electron per unit path length at position x), **a** is the anode radius, and **b** is the cathode radius.

### 2.1.4 Region IV — Limited Proportionality Region
- **Voltage:** Even higher (V₃ to V₄).
- **Behavior:** Space charge effects from positive ion sheath distort the field. Proportionality breaks down — pulses of the same energy produce varying amplitudes.
- **Use:** **Generally avoided** in routine measurements.

### 2.1.5 Region V — Geiger-Müller (GM) Region
- **Voltage:** High (V₄ to V₅, typically **800–1000 V**).
- **Behavior:** Avalanche is so intense that **UV photons emitted during de-excitation** trigger secondary avalanches throughout the entire tube volume. The discharge becomes **self-propagating** and saturates.
- **Signal:** Pulse amplitude is **independent of the original photon energy** — every event produces the same large pulse.
- **Device:** **Geiger-Müller counter** operates here.
- **Disadvantage:** Long **dead time** (typically **50–500 µs**) limits count rate.
- **Advantage:** Very large, easily countable pulses — no amplification electronics required.

---

## 2.2 The Scintillation Detection Mechanism

A **scintillation detector** is the workhorse of modern nuclear medicine (gamma cameras, PET) and many X-ray systems. It works by converting a single high-energy photon into thousands of visible-light photons, which are then detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT) or silicon photodiode.

### Step-by-Step Process:

1. **Photon Interaction:** An incoming γ-ray or X-ray photon (typically 50 keV – 1 MeV) interacts with the scintillator crystal via **photoelectric effect**, **Compton scattering**, or **pair production**.

2. **Energy Deposition:** The high-energy photoelectron/Compton electron/positron deposits its kinetic energy in the crystal lattice over a very short range (microns to mm).

3. **Excitation of Crystal Lattice:** The deposited energy excites electrons from the **valence band to the conduction band**, creating electron-hole pairs. Activator impurities (e.g., **Tl⁺** in NaI) create intermediate energy levels that allow efficient radiative de-excitation.

4. **Light Emission:** When electrons return to the ground state, they emit **visible or near-visible light photons**. Typical scintillator yields:
   - **NaI(Tl):** ~38 photons/keV (λ_max = 415 nm)
   - **CsI(Tl):** ~52 photons/keV (λ_max = 540 nm)
   - **BGO (Bi₄Ge₃O₁₂):** ~8 photons/keV (λ_max = 480 nm)
   - **LYSO (Lu₁.₉Y₀.₁SiO₅:Ce):** ~32 photons/keV (λ_max = 420 nm)

5. **Light Collection:** Light photons travel through the crystal, reaching the PMT photocathode (typically 20–50% collection efficiency).

6. **Photoelectric Conversion at PMT:** Photons strike the **photocathode** (e.g., bialkali Sb-K-Cs), ejecting photoelectrons via the photoelectric effect with quantum efficiency **η ~ 20–30%**.

7. **Electron Multiplication:** Photoelectrons are accelerated through a series of **dynodes** (typically 8–14 stages), each at increasing potential. At each dynode, **secondary emission** multiplies the electron count by a factor **δ ≈ 3–6**. Total gain:

$$G_{PMT} = \delta^{n}$$

where **n** is the number of dynodes. For **n = 10** and **δ = 4**: **G_PMT ≈ 4¹⁰ = 1,048,576 ≈ 10⁶**.

8. **Anode Collection:** The multiplied electron cloud is collected at the anode, producing a measurable voltage pulse.

### Key Performance Parameters of a Scintillation Detector:

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Value (NaI:Tl) |
|:----------|:-----------|:-----------------------|
| **Light yield** | Photons produced per keV deposited | 38 photons/keV |
| **Decay time (τ)** | Time for 63% of light to be emitted | 230 ns |
| **Density (ρ)** | Affects stopping power | 3.67 g/cm³ |
| **Effective Z (Z_eff)** | High Z → high photoelectric absorption | 50 |
| **Energy resolution (R)** | Ability to distinguish photon energies | ~7–8% at 662 keV |
| **Hygroscopic?** | Reacts with moisture? | **Yes — must be hermetically sealed** |

The **energy resolution R** of a scintillation detector is governed by:

$$R = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{2.35 \cdot \sqrt{N}}{N} = \frac{2.35}{\sqrt{N}}$$

where **N** is the total number of photoelectrons produced. This shows that **better light collection and higher PMT quantum efficiency directly improve energy resolution**.

---

## 2.3 Semiconductor (Solid-State) Detectors

Semiconductor detectors use a **p-n junction** (often reverse-biased) with a wide depletion region. When radiation interacts in the depletion region, it creates **electron-hole pairs**. Because the energy to create one e-h pair in silicon is only **3.62 eV** (compared to ~30 eV in gases), a single 100 keV photon produces:

$$N_{e-h} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ eV}}{3.62 \text{ eV}} \approx 27{,}624 \text{ e-h pairs}$$

This is **~20× more charge carriers** than a gas detector for the same energy, leading to **superior energy resolution**.

Common semiconductor detectors in medical imaging:
- **Si(Li)** — Early lithium-drifted silicon detectors.
- **High-Purity Germanium (HPGe)** — Excellent energy resolution but requires **liquid nitrogen cooling**.
- **CdZnTe (CZT)** — Room-temperature operation, high Z, used in **compact gamma cameras and SPECT**.
- **a-Si (amorphous silicon)** — Used in **flat-panel X-ray detectors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CZT (Cadmium Zinc Telluride)** is increasingly used in medical imaging because:
> - High Z (≈ 50) → excellent photoelectric absorption.
> - Wide bandgap (1.57 eV) → low leakage current at room temperature.
> - High density (5.78 g/cm³) → compact detectors.

---

## 2.4 Personnel Dosimetry Detectors (TLD, OSLD, Film Badge)

These are **storage-type detectors** that accumulate dose over weeks/months and are read out periodically — ideal for **radiation workers**.

### 2.4.1 Thermoluminescent Dosimeter (TLD)
- **Material:** LiF:Mg,Ti (most common), CaF₂:Dy, CaSO₄:Dy.
- **Mechanism:** Radiation ionizes the crystal; electrons become **trapped** at defect sites (introduced by dopants like Mg,Ti).
- **Reading:** The TLD chip is heated on a programmable planchet (~300°C). Trapped electrons are released and recombine, emitting **thermoluminescent light**.
- **Light intensity ∝ absorbed dose**.
- **TLD-100 (LiF:Mg,Ti):** Z_eff ≈ 8.2 (close to tissue); usable range **10 µGy – 10 Gy**.

### 2.4.2 Optically Stimulated Luminescence Dosimeter (OSLD)
- **Material:** Al₂O₃:C.
- **Mechanism:** Same as TLD but **read by optical stimulation** (green LED, ~540 nm) instead of heating.
- **Advantage:** Can be re-read multiple times (heating erases TLD signal completely).

### 2.4.3 Film Badge
- **Material:** Photographic film in a light-tight holder with multiple filters (Al, Cu, Pb).
- **Mechanism:** Radiation creates a **latent image** in silver halide (AgBr) emulsion.
- **Optical density ∝ dose**.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited dynamic range, energy dependent, requires chemical development.

---

## 2.5 Modern Digital X-Ray Detectors

### 2.5.1 Indirect Flat-Panel Detector (FPD)
- **Layers:**
  1. **Scintillator (CsI:Tl or Gd₂O₂S:Tb)** — converts X-rays to light.
  2. **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si) photodiode array** — converts light to charge.
  3. **TFT (Thin-Film Transistor) readout** — reads each pixel.
- **Pixel size:** Typically **100–200 µm**.

### 2.5.2 Direct Flat-Panel Detector
- **Layers:**
  1. **Photoconductor (a-Se)** — directly converts X-rays to charge.
  2. **TFT array** — reads each pixel.
- **Advantage:** Higher spatial resolution (no light spread).
- **Disadvantage:** Lower DQE at high energies.

### 2.5.3 CCD / CMOS Sensors
- **Used in:** Dental X-ray sensors, small-field mammography.
- **Mechanism:** Scintillator converts X-rays → light → focused onto CCD via fiber-optic taper or lens.

### 2.5.4 X-Ray Image Intensifier (II Tube)
- **Used in:** Fluoroscopy (still common in interventional radiology).
- **Components:**
  1. **Input phosphor (CsI:Na)** — converts X-rays to light.
  2. **Photocathode** — converts light to electrons.
  3. **Electrostatic lenses** — accelerate and focus electrons.
  4. **Output phosphor (ZnS:Ag,Ag on P45)** — converts electrons back to a brighter, smaller image.
  5. **CCD camera** — digitizes the output image.
- **Brightness gain:** Typically **5,000–20,000×** (flux gain × minification gain).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below have appeared in KTU university exams for PCBMT503 / similar biomedical courses. Memorize and understand the units!**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Values |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | $W = 33.97$ eV/ion pair | Average energy per ion pair in air | eV |
| 2 | $N_{ion} = \dfrac{E_{deposited}}{W/e}$ | Number of ion pairs created | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q = N_{ion} \cdot e$ | Charge collected in ionization chamber | Coulombs |
| 4 | $M = \exp\left(\int_{a}^{b} \alpha(x)\, dx\right)$ | Gas multiplication factor (Townsend) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $G_{PMT} = \delta^{n}$ | PMT gain (δ = dynode yield, n = # dynodes) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E} = \dfrac{2.35}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Energy resolution of scintillation detector | fraction / % |
| 7 | $N_{eh} = \dfrac{E}{3.62 \text{ eV}}$ | Electron-hole pairs in Si | dimensionless |
| 8 | $N_{eh} = \dfrac{E}{4.43 \text{ eV}}$ | Electron-hole pairs in Ge | dimensionless |
| 9 | $N_{eh} = \dfrac{E}{5.0 \text{ eV}}$ | Electron-hole pairs in CZT (approx.) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{DQE} = \dfrac{(SNR_{out})^2}{(SNR_{in})^2}$ | Detective Quantum Efficiency | 0 ≤ DQE ≤ 1 |
| 11 | $MTF(f) = \dfrac{\vert \mathcal{F}\{LSF(x)\} \vert}{\mathcal{F}\{LSF(x)\}\vert_{f=0}}$ | Modulation Transfer Function | 0 ≤ MTF ≤ 1 |
| 12 | $\text{Dose rate} = \dfrac{X \cdot (W/e)}{\rho_{air}}$ | Exposure to dose conversion | Gy/s |
| 13 | $1 \, R = 2.58 \times 10^{-4}$ C/kg | Definition of Roentgen | C/kg |
| 14 | $\dot{X}_{saturation} = \dfrac{I_{saturation}}{m_{air} \cdot (W/e)}$ | Saturation current to exposure rate | R/s |
| 15 | $V_{br} = \dfrac{e \, N_D \, d^2}{2 \, \epsilon \, \epsilon_0}$ | Breakdown voltage of p-n junction (semiconductor) | Volts |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas allow biomedical engineers to **design** detectors with specific sensitivity, **calibrate** dosimetry systems, **predict** signal-to-noise ratios in imaging systems, and **optimize** the balance between spatial resolution, contrast, and patient dose.
